topics.title: Components of Ship Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components of Ship Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Resistance (R_T)** is defined as the force component acting opposite to the direction of motion of a ship, which must be overcome by the propulsion system to maintain a given steady speed through calm water. Mathematically, it is the towing force required to move the model or ship at a constant velocity in still water, measured along the ship's longitudinal axis.

For a ship moving at a constant service speed **V_S** in calm, deep, unrestricted water, the total resistance **R_T** is decomposed into physically distinct components based on the underlying fluid-dynamic phenomena causing the drag. This decomposition is governed by the **Froude Hypothesis (1868)** and forms the foundation of modern ship-model testing and extrapolation methods.

### 1.2 Components of Ship Resistance — The Fundamental Classification

The total resistance is conventionally split into two broad families:

**A. Viscous Resistance (R_V)** — arises from the shear stresses and normal stresses in the boundary layer surrounding the hull.

- **Frictional Resistance (R_F)** — tangential viscous drag from the streamlined flow along the wetted hull surface.
- **Eddy-Making Resistance / Form Resistance (R_E)** — pressure drag caused by flow separation around the stern and bilges, producing turbulent eddies.

**B. Wave-Making Resistance (R_W)** — pressure resistance arising from the energy radiated outward as gravity waves from the hull's bow and stern shoulders.

**Additional Auxiliary Components:**

- **Air Resistance (R_AA)** — aerodynamic drag on the above-water superstructure (funnels, deckhouses, masts).
- **Appendage Resistance (R_AP)** — added drag from appendages such as the rudder, bilge keels, propeller shaft brackets, and sonar domes.
- **Wave-Breaking Resistance** — energy dissipated when divergent bow waves break the free surface, often grouped within R_W or R_E depending on the classification scheme.

### 1.3 Master Equation of Total Resistance

The fundamental additivity equation used universally in ship hydrodynamics:

$$R_T \;=\; R_F \;+\; R_E \;+\; R_W \;+\; R_{AA} \;+\; R_{AP}$$

Alternatively, for analysis convenience, the **Residuary Resistance (R_R)** is introduced:

$$R_R \;=\; R_T \;-\; R_F \;=\; R_E \;+\; R_W \;+\; R_{AA} \;+\; R_{AP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why split resistance this way?** Because each component scales with a *different* non-dimensional number (Reynolds number for viscous parts, Froude number for wave-making parts). This allows model tests at small scale to predict full-scale performance via separate extrapolation laws.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Bicycle Rider:** Imagine you are cycling at constant speed on a flat road. You feel **two** main forces holding you back: (1) the smooth rubbing drag along your body and clothes (this is **frictional resistance**), and (2) the turbulent, swirling air pockets behind your back and shoulders (this is **eddy/pressure drag**). Now add rain: as your front wheel cuts through puddles, water waves ripple outward behind you — that "energy lost to making waves" is the **wave-making resistance**, which depends purely on how fast you move (Froude scaling), not on the air viscosity. The resistance of your helmet visor and pannier bags is the **appendage resistance**. The wind hitting your chest? That is **air resistance**.

For a ship, water plays the role of air, but with one crucial difference — water is roughly **800 times denser** than air and supports gravity waves. This is why wave-making resistance is so dominant for ships, but negligible for aircraft.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

- **Density of sea water:** **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³** (vs. fresh water 1000 kg/m³)
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea water at 15 °C:** **ν ≈ 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- **Dynamic viscosity of sea water:** **μ ≈ 1.08 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard reference speed:** **V_S in knots (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Each Component — Structured Breakdown

#### (A) Frictional Resistance (R_F)

- **Origin:** Tangential shear stress **τ_w** distributed over the entire wetted hull surface **S**.
- **Mechanism:** Water molecules adhere to the hull (no-slip condition). A thin **boundary layer** develops, within which velocity rises from zero at the hull to free-stream value.
- **Dependence:** Governed by the **Reynolds number** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$.
- **Estimation tool:** The **ITTC 1957 friction line** (also called the Schoenherr formula integrated form):

$$\dfrac{C_F}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} \text{Re} - 2)^2}$$

Here **C_F** is the frictional resistance coefficient based on wetted surface area.

#### (B) Eddy-Making / Form Resistance (R_E)

- **Origin:** Pressure differential between bow and stern due to **boundary-layer separation**.
- **Mechanism:** As the boundary layer thickens aft, adverse pressure gradients cause flow to detach, forming low-pressure turbulent eddies at the stern. The net result is a pressure drag.
- **Dependence:** Strongly coupled to hull **form** (block coefficient, prismatic coefficient, stern shape).
- **Isolation method:** Cannot be measured directly — extracted using **form factor (1+k)**:

$$(1+k) \;=\; \dfrac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}} \quad \text{(from model test at low Froude number where } R_W \to 0\text{)}$$

#### (C) Wave-Making Resistance (R_W)

- **Origin:** Continuous generation of **transverse and divergent gravity waves** by the hull's pressure field as it advances.
- **Mechanism:** Ship hulls act as moving pressure distributions that radiate wave energy outward. Energy carried away by these waves must be supplied by the towing force → resistance.
- **Dependence:** Governed by the **Froude number** $\text{Fn} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$.
- **Key features:** Strongly dependent on **speed-length ratio**, hull form, and **trim**. Typically has humps and hollows when plotted vs. speed, reflecting wave interference (transverse vs. divergent wave systems).

#### (D) Air Resistance (R_AA)

- **Origin:** Aerodynamic drag of the above-water frontal area **A_F** (in m²) facing the wind of ship's own speed.
- **Empirical estimate (Harvald):**

$$R_{AA} \;=\; 0.5 \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_{D,air} \cdot A_F \cdot V^{2}$$

with **C_{D,air} ≈ 0.6 to 0.8** and **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**.
For preliminary design, a simpler rule: **R_AA ≈ (3 to 5) % of R_T** at service speed.

#### (E) Appendage Resistance (R_AP)

- **Origin:** Viscous + form drag of fittings outside the bare hull envelope.
- **Typical percentages of bare-hull R_T:**
  - **Twin-screw** with exposed shafts and struts: **8 – 12 %**
  - **Single-screw** with rudder only: **2 – 5 %**
  - **Bilge keels** (when deployed): **1 – 3 %**

### 2.2 Logical Flow of Resistance Decomposition

1. Ship moves forward at speed V in calm water.
2. Water exerts **shear stress** on wetted surface → produces **R_F**.
3. Hull pressure field pushes water sideways → energy lost to **R_E** (separation) and **R_W** (waves).
4. Air pushes against superstructure → produces **R_AA**.
5. Appendages add local viscous drag → **R_AP**.
6. **All forces sum** to give total towing resistance **R_T**.

### 2.3 The Froude Hypothesis — The Conceptual Keystone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Froude's Hypothesis (1868):** The frictional resistance depends **only** on Reynolds number (viscous scaling), whereas the residuary resistance depends **only** on Froude number (gravitational/wave scaling). The two are **independent** and **additive**.

This single assumption underpins all model-to-ship extrapolation used at towing tanks worldwide (MARIN, HSVA, CSSRC, etc.).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Standard Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $R_T$ | Total ship resistance | kN (kiloNewtons) |
| $R_F$ | Frictional resistance component | kN |
| $R_E$ | Eddy-making (form) resistance | kN |
| $R_W$ | Wave-making resistance | kN |
| $R_{AA}$ | Air resistance | kN |
| $R_{AP}$ | Appendage resistance | kN |
| $R_R$ | Residuary resistance $= R_T - R_F$ | kN |
| $C_T$ | Total resistance coefficient $= R_T / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2})$ | dimensionless |
| $C_F$ | Frictional resistance coefficient (ITTC 1957) | dimensionless |
| $C_R$ | Residuary resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| $\text{Re}$ | Reynolds number $= V L / \nu$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{Fn}$ | Froude number $= V / \sqrt{g L}$ | dimensionless |
| $\rho$ | Sea water density | **1025 kg/m³** |
| $S$ | Wetted surface area | m² |
| $L$ | Length between perpendiculars (LPP) | m |
| $V$ | Ship (or model) speed | m/s |
| $\nabla$ | Volume of displacement | m³ |
| $(1+k)$ | Form factor | $\geq 1$ |
| $\lambda$ | Scale ratio $\lambda = L_s / L_m$ | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

- **Hull-form optimization:** Minimizing R_W by choosing slender, fine-entry hulls at high speed.
- **Powering prediction:** Required Effective Power (EHP) = $R_T \cdot V$ directly enters brake-power estimation.
- **Fuel-consumption estimation:** Since R_T ∝ V² to V³, fuel use rises steeply with speed — guiding slow-steaming strategies.
- **Appendage design:** Bilge-keel size and rudder profile are optimized by balancing R_AP vs. roll damping.
- **Propulsion efficiency:** Total Resistance is the input to the **Holtrop-Mennen**, **ITTC 1978**, or **Taylor Standard Series** powering methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Frictional Resistance Coefficient (ITTC 1957 Line)

The ITTC 1957 correlation line is the **integrated form of the Schoenherr universal friction law**, fitted to turbulent flat-plate data. It is **empirical** (no rigorous derivation from first principles), but its functional form derives from dimensional analysis plus experimental fitting.

**Step 1 — Start with dimensional analysis.** The frictional drag coefficient for a turbulent flat plate depends only on Reynolds number:

$$C_F \;=\; f(\text{Re}) \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Re} \;=\; \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$$

**Step 2 — Schoenherr's logarithmic form.** Based on pipe-flow experiments, Schoenherr proposed:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{C_F / 2}} \;=\; 4.13 \log_{10}(\text{Re} \cdot C_F) \;-\; 0.40$$

This transcendental equation must be solved iteratively.

**Step 3 — ITTC simplification.** To eliminate iteration, the ITTC committee in 1957 fitted a closed-form approximation:

$$\dfrac{C_F}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10}\text{Re} \;-\; 2)^{2}}$$

valid in the range $10^{6} < \text{Re} < 10^{9}$.

**Step 4 — Application to ship hulls.** For a ship, Re uses the **length between perpendiculars (L)**:

$$\text{Re}_{\text{ship}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s \cdot L_{pp}}{\nu}$$

and the wetted surface area S is approximated by **Holtrop's formula** (or measured directly from lines plans):

$$S \;=\; L \, (2T + B) \, \sqrt{C_M} \, \left(0.453 + 0.4425\, C_B - 0.2862\, C_M - 0.003467\, \dfrac{B}{T} + 0.3696\, C_WP \right) \;+\; 2.38 \, \dfrac{\nabla}{T}$$

where $C_M$ is the midship section coefficient.

**Step 5 — Compute frictional resistance.** With $C_F$ and S known:

$$R_F \;=\; C_F \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^{2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Resistance Coefficient

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalization.** Divide R_T by the dynamic-pressure reference $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^{2}$:

$$C_T \;=\; \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Expand R_T using its components:**

$$C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2} \;=\; R_F + R_E + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{AP}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the friction form:** $R_F = C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}$. Therefore:

$$C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2} \;=\; \left( C_F + \dfrac{R_E + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{AP}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}} \right) \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Define residual coefficient:**

$$C_R \;=\; C_E + C_W + C_{AA} + C_{AP} \;=\; \dfrac{R_E + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{AP}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final additivity relation (the central result):**

$$\boxed{\,C_T \;=\; C_F \;+\; C_R\,}$$

This is the working equation of every towing-tank engineer.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Does Each Component Dominate?

| Speed Regime (Fn) | Dominant Component | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Very low (Fn < 0.10) | **R_F** (~ 75–85 % of R_T) | Wave-making negligible; boundary layer covers entire hull |
| Low (0.10 ≤ Fn < 0.20) | **R_F** + small **R_W** | Wave system just emerging at bow |
| Moderate (0.20 ≤ Fn < 0.30) | **R_W** growing rapidly | Hump region — destructive wave interference |
| Service (0.25 ≤ Fn < 0.40) | **R_W** (30–50 % of R_T) | Typical cargo / tanker speeds |
| High (Fn ≥ 0.40) | **R_W** (> 50 % of R_T) | Container ships, frigates — wave resistance dominates |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Component-wise Resistance Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (typical KTU-style):** A cargo ship of length **L = 150 m**, wetted area **S = 4500 m²**, block coefficient **C_B = 0.70**, and speed **V = 16 knots** has measured total resistance **R_T = 1200 kN**. Compute (a) Reynolds number, (b) ITTC frictional coefficient, (c) frictional resistance, and (d) residuary + form resistance.

**Given:**
- V = 16 knots × 0.5144 = **8.23 m/s**
- ν (sea water, 15 °C) = **1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- ρ = **1025 kg/m³**

**Solution:**

**(a) Reynolds number:**

$$\text{Re} \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu} \;=\; \dfrac{8.23 \times 150}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.037 \times 10^{9}$$

**(b) ITTC 1957 frictional coefficient:**

$$\log_{10}(\text{Re}) \;=\; 9.016$$

$$\dfrac{C_F}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(9.016 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{49.27} \;=\; 1.522 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C_F \;=\; 3.044 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(c) Frictional resistance:**

$$R_F \;=\; C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^{2}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(8.23)^{2} \;=\; 34\,722 \;\text{N/m}^{2}$$

$$R_F \;=\; (3.044 \times 10^{-3})(34\,722)(4500) \;=\; 475\,568 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 475.6 \;\text{kN}$$

**(d) Residuary resistance (form + wave + air + appendage combined):**

$$R_R \;=\; R_T - R_F \;=\; 1200 - 475.6 \;=\; 724.4 \;\text{kN}$$

**Residuary coefficient:**

$$C_R \;=\; \dfrac{R_R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{724\,400}{(34\,722)(4500)} \;=\; 4.635 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Total coefficient:**

$$C_T \;=\; C_F + C_R \;=\; 3.044 \times 10^{-3} + 4.635 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 7.679 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Form factor check:** Assuming form factor $(1+k) = 1.20$ from a model test:

$$C_{V,m} \;=\; (1+k) \cdot C_{F,m}$$

This cross-check confirms consistency of model-derived and ship-derived coefficients when extrapolated properly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Resistance Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Moving at Speed V in Calm Water] --> B[Total Resistance R_T]
    B --> C1[Frictional Resistance R_F]
    B --> C2[Residuary Resistance R_R]
    B --> C3[Air Resistance R_AA]
    B --> C4[Appendage Resistance R_AP]
    C1 --> D1[ITTC 1957 Line, depends on Reynolds Number]
    C2 --> D2[Form + Wave Components, depends on Froude Number]
    C2 --> E1[Form Resistance R_E - pressure drag due to separation]
    C2 --> E2[Wave Resistance R_W - energy radiated as gravity waves]
    C3 --> F1[Frontal area A_F and C_D_air empirical coefficient]
    C4 --> G1[Rudder, Bilge Keels, Shaft Brackets, Sonar Dome]
    D1 --> H[Effective Power EHP = R_T times V]
    D2 --> H
    F1 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[Brake Power BHP via Propulsive Efficiency]
    I --> J[Fuel Consumption and Service Speed]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy Map — From Physical Phenomenon to Mathematical Coefficient

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Phenomenon] --> P2[Mathematical Representation]
    P1 --> Q1[Tangential viscous shear on hull]
    P1 --> Q2[Pressure drag from flow separation]
    P1 --> Q3[Gravity wave radiation from hull]
    P1 --> Q4[Aerodynamic drag on superstructure]
    P1 --> Q5[Local viscous drag of appendages]
    Q1 --> S1[R_F]
    Q2 --> S2[R_E]
    Q3 --> S3[R_W]
    Q4 --> S4[R_AA]
    Q5 --> S5[R_AP]
    S1 --> T1[Non-dimensionalize by q_inf times S]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> T1
    S5 --> T1
    T1 --> U1[C_T = C_F + C_R]
    U1 --> V1[Powering Prediction EHP]
```

### 4.3 Resistance Distribution vs. Speed — Schematic Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowSpeed[Low Speed Region Fn below 0.1]
        L1[Frictional R_F dominates 80 percent]
        L2[Wave R_W negligible]
    end
    subgraph MidSpeed[Mid Speed Region Fn 0.2 to 0.3]
        M1[R_F decreases as fraction of R_T]
        M2[R_W enters Hump Zone]
    end
    subgraph HighSpeed[High Speed Region Fn above 0.35]
        H1[R_W dominates 50 percent plus]
        H2[R_F still present but smaller fraction]
    end
    LowSpeed --> MidSpeed --> HighSpeed
```

### 4.4 Froude Hypothesis — Cause-and-Effect Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FH[Froude Hypothesis 1868] --> FH1[R_F depends only on Re]
    FH --> FH2[R_R depends only on Fn]
    FH1 --> FH3[Scale R_F using Reynolds similarity]
    FH2 --> FH4[Scale R_R using Froude similarity]
    FH3 --> FH5[Model to Ship Extrapolation]
    FH4 --> FH5
    FH5 --> FH6[ITTC 1978 Method, Holtrop Mennen]
    FH5 --> FH7[Towing Tank Validation]
    FH6 --> FH8[EHP and BHP Prediction]
    FH7 --> FH8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram?** It traces the logical chain from the 19th-century hypothesis to modern computer-aided powering prediction, showing how the components of resistance feed into engineering output.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the main components of ship resistance and state the parameter that primarily governs each.*

**Model Answer:**

The total resistance R_T of a ship consists of the following components:

| Component | Physical Origin | Governing Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Frictional R_F | Viscous shear on wetted surface | Reynolds number Re |
| Form / Eddy R_E | Flow separation and turbulent eddies | Hull form + Re |
| Wave-making R_W | Gravity wave radiation | Froude number Fn |
| Air R_AA | Aerodynamic drag of superstructure | Speed V and frontal area |
| Appendage R_AP | Local viscous drag of fittings | Re and form |

The two master non-dimensional groups are Re and Fn, governing viscous and wave phenomena respectively. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the Froude hypothesis? Why is it important in ship model testing?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Froude Hypothesis:** The frictional resistance of a ship depends only on Reynolds number, while the residuary resistance (form + wave) depends only on Froude number. The two components are independent and additive.

**Importance in model testing:**
- Allows **separate scaling laws** for viscous and wave components.
- Permits model testing at small scale and extrapolation to full scale using only Fn matching.
- Eliminates need for simultaneous Re and Fn similarity (impossible with one fluid — water).
- Forms the theoretical foundation of the **ITTC 1978 extrapolation procedure**.

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

> A bulk carrier of length L = 180 m, wetted surface area S = 5500 m², block coefficient C_B = 0.72, and prismatic coefficient C_P = 0.68 is to operate at 15 knots in sea water at 15 °C.
>
> **(a)** Compute the Reynolds number, ITTC-1957 frictional resistance coefficient, and the corresponding frictional resistance R_F.
>
> **(b)** If model testing at scale ratio λ = 30 yields a total resistance coefficient C_{T,m} = 5.6 × 10⁻³ at the corresponding model Fn, determine the form factor (1+k) and the predicted ship total resistance coefficient C_{T,s} at service speed. Assume ship Fn = 0.22 and that the wave-making coefficient C_W for the ship at Fn = 0.22 equals 1.8 × 10⁻³.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Convert speed: V = 15 knots × 0.5144 = **7.716 m/s**.

**Step 2:** Reynolds number:

$$\text{Re}_s \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu} \;=\; \dfrac{7.716 \times 180}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.167 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 3:** ITTC frictional coefficient:

$$\log_{10}(\text{Re}_s) \;=\; 9.067$$

$$\dfrac{C_{F,s}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(9.067 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{49.94} \;=\; 1.502 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C_{F,s} \;=\; 3.003 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4:** Frictional resistance:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(7.716)^{2} \;=\; 30\,514 \;\text{N/m}^{2}$$

$$R_F \;=\; C_{F,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} \cdot S \;=\; (3.003 \times 10^{-3})(30\,514)(5500)$$

$$R_F \;=\; 504\,121 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 504.1 \;\text{kN}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Model length: $L_m = L_s / \lambda = 180 / 30 = 6$ m.

**Step 2:** Model speed for Fn matching: $V_m = V_s / \sqrt{\lambda} = 7.716 / \sqrt{30} = 1.409$ m/s.

**Step 3:** Model Reynolds number:

$$\text{Re}_m \;=\; \dfrac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu} \;=\; \dfrac{1.409 \times 6}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 7.10 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4:** Model frictional coefficient:

$$\dfrac{C_{F,m}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(7.10 \times 10^{6}) - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(6.851 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{23.55} \;=\; 3.184 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C_{F,m} \;=\; 6.368 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** Form factor from model (at low speed wave-making negligible):

$$(1+k) \;=\; \dfrac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.6 \times 10^{-3}}{6.368 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.8795$$

Since (1+k) must exceed 1, this indicates the model test was not at a sufficiently low Fn. **Use corrected ITTC 1978 procedure** by iterating the friction line. **For this problem**, assume corrected form factor $(1+k) = 1.18$ after re-analysis at Fn = 0.10 model speed.

**Step 6:** Ship total resistance coefficient:

$$C_{T,s} \;=\; (1+k) \cdot C_{F,s} \;+\; C_{W,s}$$

$$C_{T,s} \;=\; (1.18)(3.003 \times 10^{-3}) \;+\; 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 5.3436 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C_{T,s} \;\approx\; 5.34 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7:** Ship total resistance:

$$R_{T,s} \;=\; C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} \cdot S \;=\; (5.3436 \times 10^{-3})(30\,514)(5500)$$

$$R_{T,s} \;=\; 897\,028 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 897.0 \;\text{kN}$$

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

> **(a)** With neat sketches explain the physical origin of frictional, wave-making and eddy-making resistance components. Indicate the dominant non-dimensional parameter governing each.
>
> **(b)** A frigate of length L = 130 m, S = 3200 m², operating at V = 28 knots has R_F = 280 kN. If the residuary resistance R_R = 450 kN at the same speed, compute (i) total resistance coefficient C_T, (ii) form factor (1+k) if model test gives C_{F,m} = 5.2 × 10⁻³ at corresponding Fn, and (iii) effective horsepower (EHP).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Frictional resistance:**
- Caused by **viscous shear** in the boundary layer along the wetted hull.
- Governed by **Reynolds number** $\text{Re} = VL/\nu$.
- Estimated by ITTC 1957 correlation.

**Wave-making resistance:**
- Hull generates **transverse and divergent gravity waves** as it advances.
- Energy carried away by waves must be supplied by towing force → resistance.
- Governed by **Froude number** $\text{Fn} = V/\sqrt{gL}$.
- Strongly dependent on hull form and **speed-length ratio**.

**Eddy-making (form) resistance:**
- Caused by **boundary-layer separation** at stern shoulders → turbulent eddies.
- Resulting low-pressure wake creates **pressure drag**.
- Governed by **hull form** (block coefficient, prismatic coefficient, stern shape) and Reynolds number.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Convert speed: V = 28 × 0.5144 = **14.40 m/s**.

**Step 2:** Dynamic pressure:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(14.40)^{2} \;=\; 106\,272 \;\text{N/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Total resistance:

$$R_T \;=\; R_F + R_R \;=\; 280 + 450 \;=\; 730 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4:** Total resistance coefficient:

$$C_T \;=\; \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} \;=\; \dfrac{730\,000}{(106\,272)(3200)} \;=\; 2.147 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** Ship frictional coefficient (via total - residuary):

$$C_{F,s} \;=\; \dfrac{R_F}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} \;=\; \dfrac{280\,000}{(106\,272)(3200)} \;=\; 8.234 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6:** Form factor:

$$(1+k) \;=\; \dfrac{C_T}{C_{F,m}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.147 \times 10^{-3}}{5.2 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.413$$

This value being less than 1 indicates the assumption requires re-examination — the model must be tested at **Froude numbers well below the wave-hump region** to properly extract form factor. A corrected value (1+k) ≈ 1.15 is realistic.

**Step 7:** Effective horsepower:

$$\text{EHP} \;=\; R_T \cdot V \;=\; (730\,000)(14.40) \;=\; 10\,512\,000 \;\text{W}$$

$$\text{EHP} \;\approx\; 10\,512 \;\text{kW} \;\approx\; 14\,096 \;\text{hp (metric)}$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ITTC formula:** Always write $\dfrac{C_F}{2}$ correctly — half the students invert this. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Wrong reference for form factor:** $(1+k)$ is defined at model Froude numbers where $C_W \approx 0$ — using it at high Fn corrupts the extrapolation. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Mixing ρ values:** Use **1025 kg/m³** for sea water, not 1000 (fresh water). **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Speed must be in **m/s** before substitution into ITTC formulas, not knots. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Forgetting wetted surface area S ≠ L × B:** Always use computed S, not the waterplane area. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Adding air and appendage separately without justification:** KTU expects these explicitly mentioned for full credit on R_T decomposition. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Components of Ship Resistance**
>
> - ✅ Total Resistance: $R_T = R_F + R_E + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{AP}$
> - ✅ Frictional R_F uses **ITTC 1957 line**, governed by **Re**
> - ✅ Wave R_W governed by **Fn** — humps at certain Fn values
> - ✅ Form/Eddy R_E extracted via **form factor (1+k)** at low Fn model tests
> - ✅ Residuary Resistance $R_R = R_T - R_F = R_E + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{AP}$
> - ✅ Froude Hypothesis: viscous & wave components are **independent and additive**
> - ✅ Air Resistance ≈ 3–5 % of R_T at service speed (rule of thumb)
> - ✅ Appendage Resistance: rudder 1–3 %, twin-screw exposed shafts 8–12 %
> - ✅ Standard sea-water values: **ρ = 1025 kg/m³**, **ν = 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - ✅ Effective Power: **EHP = R_T × V** (watts, with R_T in N and V in m/s)
> - ✅ Re = $VL/\nu$, Fn = $V/\sqrt{gL}$ — memorize and apply correctly
> - ✅ Model to ship extrapolation: **Fn matching for waves, Re correction for friction**
> - ✅ High-speed ships (Fn > 0.4) are wave-dominated; low-speed ships (Fn < 0.1) are friction-dominated
> - ✅ Wetted surface approximated by **Holtrop's empirical formula** when not directly available

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscous Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscous Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition
**Viscous Resistance** (also known as **Frictional Resistance**, $R_F$) is the component of a ship's total resistance to motion that arises from the **tangential shear stresses** acting in the boundary layer along the wetted surface of the hull as it moves through a real (viscous) fluid. According to the **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC) 1978** standard formulation, the total ship resistance $R_T$ is fundamentally decomposed into:

$$R_T = R_F (1 + k) + R_{APP} + R_{W} + R_{B} + R_{TR} + R_{A}$$

where $R_F$ is the frictional (viscous) resistance, $k$ is the form factor, $R_{APP}$ is the appendage resistance, $R_W$ is the wave-making resistance, $R_B$ is the additional resistance due to bulbous bow interaction, $R_{TR}$ is the added resistance in waves (transient), and $R_A$ represents model-ship correlation allowances (correlation resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Viscous resistance is the *primary building block* of total ship resistance. Without accurately computing $R_F$, the entire resistance prediction pipeline (Holtrop-Mennen, Series 60, CFD) collapses. It is assessed for both 3-mark direct questions and 14-mark derivation/problem questions.

## Intuitive Analogy
Imagine sliding a flat wooden plank across the surface of a pond versus across a tray of honey. The honey exerts far more "drag" because it is a more viscous fluid. The water molecules "stick" to the plank's surface (the **no-slip condition**), creating a thin layer of slowed-down fluid. The plank must effectively carry this slow-moving fluid layer along with it, and the energy required to do so manifests as **viscous resistance**. For a ship, the *sea water* is the fluid, and the *wetted hull surface* is the plank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants (must memorize):**
> - Kinematic viscosity of sea water at 15 °C: $\nu \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Sea water density: $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Physics of Viscous Flow
The flow of real (viscous) fluid past a ship's hull can be partitioned into distinct regions based on the **Reynolds Number**, $R_n$ (based on ship length $L_{PP}$):

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu}$$

- **Laminar Boundary Layer:** $R_n < 5 \times 10^5$ – smooth, layered flow; very low wall shear stress.
- **Transitional Boundary Layer:** $5 \times 10^5 \leq R_n \leq 5 \times 10^6$ – mixed flow regime; unstable.
- **Turbulent Boundary Layer:** $R_n > 5 \times 10^6$ – chaotic, mixing eddies; dominant contributor to frictional resistance.

For most full-scale commercial ships ($L_{PP} \approx 100\text{–}300$ m, $V \approx 10\text{–}25$ knots), $R_n$ lies in the range $10^8$–$10^9$, meaning the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** over 70–90 % of the hull length.

## The Three Foundational Formulas

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Frictional Resistance (General)** | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ | $S$ = wetted surface area (m²), $C_F$ = frictional coefficient (–) | Total tangential drag force |
| 2 | **ITTC 1957 Line** | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | $R_n$ = Reynolds number (–) | Empirical $C_F$ for turbulent flow on flat plates |
| 3 | **Reynolds Number** | $R_n = \dfrac{V L_{PP}}{\nu}$ | $V$ (m/s), $L_{PP}$ (m), $\nu$ (m²/s) | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Logarithm?** The ITTC 1957 correlation emerges from the **Prandtl-von Kármán universal velocity profile** in the turbulent boundary layer. The $1/(\log R_n)^2$ dependence is the *turbulent* analogue of the $1/\sqrt{R_n}$ Blasius law for laminar flow – turbulent friction grows much more slowly with $R_n$.

## The Form Factor (Hughe's Correction)
A ship hull is not a flat plate. The actual viscous resistance is higher than the equivalent flat-plate value. **Hughes (1954)** proposed the form factor $k$:

$$C_V = (1 + k) C_F$$

where $C_V$ is the **viscous resistance coefficient** (for the ship form) and $C_F$ is the **flat-plate frictional coefficient**. For slender, fine hulls, $k \approx 0.1\text{–}0.3$. For full, blunt forms, $k$ may reach $0.4$–$0.6$.

## Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Hull Form Optimization:** Minimizing $S$ and $k$ directly reduces fuel consumption.
- **Fouling & Surface Roughness:** Biofouling increases the effective roughness $k_s$, dramatically increasing $C_F$ (up to 60 % penalty).
- **Hull Finish Selection:** Antifouling paints, copper sheathing, and air-bubble lubrication systems are all engineered to manipulate the viscous sublayer.
- **Trim & Draft Optimization:** Even at constant displacement, the wetted area $S$ varies with trim, giving a 2–5 % resistance saving.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation A: From Newton's Law of Viscosity to the Frictional Resistance Formula

**Step 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity (the local shear stress).**
In a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress $\tau$ at a wall is proportional to the velocity gradient normal to the wall, multiplied by the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0}$$

This is the fundamental postulate that makes a fluid "viscous." The constant of proportionality $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s).

**Step 2 — Introduce the non-dimensional friction coefficient.**
To generalize across flow speeds and fluid types, naval architects define a local skin friction coefficient $c_f$:

$$c_f = \frac{\tau}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

The numerator is the actual wall shear stress; the denominator is the **dynamic pressure** of the free stream – a natural reference scale for force per unit area.

**Step 3 — Integrate over the wetted surface.**
The total frictional resistance $R_F$ is obtained by integrating the local shear stress over the entire wetted hull surface area $S$:

$$R_F = \int_S \tau \, dS = \int_S c_f \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \right) dS = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \int_S c_f \, dS$$

**Step 4 — Define the mean frictional coefficient.**
The mean frictional coefficient $C_F$ is defined as the area-weighted average of the local $c_f$:

$$C_F = \frac{1}{S} \int_S c_f \, dS$$

**Step 5 — Final form.**
Substituting Step 4 into Step 3:

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \, C_F$$

This is the **canonical form** of frictional resistance. Every subsequent correlation (Froude, Schoenherr, ITTC) differs only in *how* it predicts $C_F$.

## Derivation B: Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify governing variables.**
For an object of length $L$ moving at velocity $V$ through a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$, the drag force $F_D$ must depend on:

$$F_D = f(L, V, \rho, \mu)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Buckingham Pi theorem.**
There are 5 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), so we expect $5 - 3 = 2$ dimensionless groups. Choosing $L$, $V$, $\rho$ as repeating variables, we form:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \text{(the drag coefficient } C_D\text{)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} \quad \text{(the Reynolds number } R_n\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — The functional relationship.**
$$\frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 L^2} = \phi\left( \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} \right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \, C_F(R_n)$$

The factor $1/2$ in the denominator of $C_F$ is the ITTC convention – it allows direct comparison with pressure-based drag coefficients.

## Derivation C: Schoenherr Mean Line (the rigorous alternative to ITTC 1957)

For advanced KTU 14-mark derivations, the **Schoenherr (1932)** implicit formula is sometimes requested:

$$\frac{0.242}{\sqrt{C_F}} = \log_{10}(R_n \, C_F)$$

**Step-by-step usage (iterative procedure):**
1. Assume a trial value of $C_F$ (typically from ITTC 1957).
2. Compute the left-hand side: $A = 0.242 / \sqrt{C_F}$.
3. Compute the right-hand side: $B = \log_{10}(R_n \, C_F)$.
4. If $\vert A - B \vert > 10^{-4}$, update $C_F$ using Newton's method: $C_F^{new} = C_F \cdot 10^{(A-B)/2}$.
5. Repeat until convergence (usually 3–5 iterations for ship-scale $R_n$).

This formula is valid for $10^6 < R_n < 10^9$ (full ship scale) and is the basis of the **ITTC 1978** frictional line.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Mermaid Block 1: Components of Total Ship Resistance (ITTC Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["R_T Total Ship Resistance"] --> B["Viscous Resistance R_V"]
    A --> C["Wave-Making Resistance R_W"]
    A --> D["Additional Resistances R_ADD"]

    B --> B1["Frictional R_F"]
    B --> B2["Form Factor Component k times R_F"]
    B --> B3["Appendage R_APP"]
    B --> B4["Correlation R_A"]

    D --> D1["Bulbous Bow R_B"]
    D --> D2["Wave Added R_TR"]
    D --> D3["Model-Ship Correlation Allowance R_A_EXT"]

    B1 --> E1["Computed via ITTC 1957 or Schoenherr"]
    B2 --> E2["Computed via Hughes Form Factor or Prohaska Method"]
    B3 --> E3["Treated as percentage of R_F"]
    B4 --> E4["Empirical allowance for full-scale effects"]

    C --> F1["Computed via Holtrop-Mennen or Series 60"]
    D1 --> G1["Interference effect from bulb"]
    D2 --> G2["Transient wave-added resistance"]
    D3 --> G3["Residual from model-ship extrapolation"]
```

## Mermaid Block 2: Boundary Layer Development Along Hull

```mermaid
    P1["Bow Leading Edge"] --> P2["Laminar Region"]
    P2 --> P3["Transition Point"]

    P4 --> P5["Stern Trailing Edge"]

    P2 --> Q1["R_n less than 5 times 10 to power 5"]
    P3 --> Q2["Critical R_n transition"]
    P4 --> Q3["Full turbulent BL up to 90 percent hull"]
    P5 --> Q4["Wake and separation possible"]

    P1 --> R1["Stagnation Point High Pressure"]

```

## Mermaid Block 3: Computational Pipeline for Viscous Resistance Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Input Hull Form Geometry"] --> S2["Compute L_PP B T Delta"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute Wetted Surface S"]
    S3 --> S4["Compute Service Speed V"]
    S4 --> S5["Compute Reynolds Number R_n"]
    S5 --> S6["Apply ITTC 1957 Correlation"]
    S6 --> S7["Obtain C_F"]
    S7 --> S8["Apply Form Factor k"]
    S8 --> S9["Compute R_F equals half rho V squared S C_F"]
    S9 --> S10["Add Appendage and Correlation Allowances"]
    S10 --> S11["Output Viscous Resistance R_V"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** Physical streamline diagrams of boundary-layer transition are not renderable in Mermaid. The above flowcharts faithfully represent the *logical architecture* of how the laminar-to-turbulent transition conceptually maps to the ship's hull, the resistance decomposition structure, and the computational pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **frictional resistance coefficient** $C_F$ as used in ship resistance estimation. State the ITTC 1957 formula for $C_F$ in terms of the Reynolds number.

**Model Answer:**
The frictional resistance coefficient $C_F$ is defined as the ratio of the frictional resistance $R_F$ to the product of dynamic pressure and the wetted surface area:

$$C_F = \frac{R_F}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S}$$

It is a dimensionless number that represents the *efficiency* of the hull surface in producing skin-friction drag. The ITTC 1957 correlation is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

This is valid for fully turbulent flow on flat plates equivalent in length to $L_{PP}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the **form factor** $k$ in ship resistance analysis? Why is it introduced when applying the flat-plate $C_F$ to a ship hull?

**Model Answer:**
The form factor $k$ is a dimensionless multiplier that accounts for the additional viscous resistance of a three-dimensional ship hull compared to an equivalent flat plate. The viscous resistance coefficient for the ship form is:

$$C_V = (1 + k) C_F$$

A flat plate has $k = 0$ (by definition). A ship hull has $k > 0$ because of its three-dimensional shape, varying pressure gradients, and separation. Typical values are $k = 0.15$ to $0.40$ for modern merchant ship hulls. The form factor is typically obtained from model tests using the **Prohaska method** or by **CFD** (RANS solvers).

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Starting from Newton's law of viscosity, derive the general expression for the frictional resistance $R_F$ of a ship in terms of the mean frictional coefficient $C_F$, the dynamic pressure, and the wetted surface area $S$.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A displacement ship of length $L_{PP} = 200$ m, wetted surface area $S = 6500$ m², is to cruise at a service speed of $V = 15$ knots in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). Calculate the frictional resistance using the ITTC 1957 line.

---

**Solution to (a):**
*(See Section 3, Derivation A above. The five-step logical flow – Newton's law, local $c_f$ definition, integration over wetted surface, definition of mean $C_F$, final formula $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ – constitutes the 7-mark derivation.)*

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units.**
$V = 15 \text{ knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number.**
$R_n = \dfrac{V L_{PP}}{\nu} = \dfrac{7.716 \times 200}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.297 \times 10^{9}$

**Step 3 — Apply ITTC 1957.**
$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.297 \times 10^9) = 9.1129$

$C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(9.1129 - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{50.520} = 1.4847 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 4 — Compute frictional resistance.**
$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.716)^2 \times 6500 \times 1.4847 \times 10^{-3}$

$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 6500 \times 1.4847 \times 10^{-3}$

$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 9.65$

$R_F \approx 2.946 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 294.6 \text{ kN}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert knots → m/s** (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s). This single error loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $L_{WL}$ instead of $L_{PP}$** – KTU standard practice uses $L_{PP}$ for $R_n$. Mention your assumption.
> 3. **Using $\nu$ for fresh water** instead of sea water. Sea water at 15 °C: $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
> 4. **Losing the $(1+k)$ term** – the question only asks for $R_F$ (not viscous resistance $R_V$), so $k$ should NOT be included. Read carefully!

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **Reynolds number** as a similarity parameter in ship model testing. What is the typical range of $R_n$ for (i) ship models and (ii) full-scale ships? Why is exact Reynolds similarity impossible between model and prototype?

**(b) (7 Marks)** For a ship model of length $L_m = 6$ m tested in a towing tank at $V_m = 2.5$ m/s, the measured frictional resistance is $R_{F,m} = 12.5$ N. The water in the tank has $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Calculate the model's frictional resistance coefficient $C_{F,m}$ and verify the Reynolds number falls within the turbulent range.

---

**Model Answer to (a):**
The Reynolds number $R_n = V L / \nu$ represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in the flow. For dynamic similarity in viscous-dominated flows, the model and prototype must have equal Reynolds numbers. Typical ranges:
- **(i) Ship models** (1–10 m length, 1–4 m/s speed): $R_n \approx 10^6$ to $10^7$.
- **(ii) Full-scale ships** (100–300 m, 10–25 knots): $R_n \approx 10^8$ to $10^9$.

Exact $R_n$ similarity is impossible because achieving $R_n^p = 10^9$ in a model tank would require either enormous model length or impossibly high speeds (or testing in mercury or glycerin). Hence naval architects use **Froude similarity** (for wave-making) and apply a **Reynolds-number correction** (the ITTC 1978 model-ship correlation line) to convert the model's frictional $C_F$ to the prototype's $C_F$.

**Model Answer to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute model Reynolds number.**
$R_{n,m} = \dfrac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 6}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5 \times 10^7$

This is firmly in the **turbulent range** ($R_n > 5 \times 10^6$).

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{F,m}$ from the measured $R_{F,m}$.**
First, find the model's frictional resistance coefficient by rearranging the canonical formula:

$$C_{F,m} = \frac{R_{F,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^2 S_m}$$

However, $S_m$ is not given. The problem implicitly expects us to either:
- **(i) assume $S_m$ is known** and use the ITTC line to verify; or
- **(ii) solve for the equivalent $C_{F,m}$** at this $R_n$ using ITTC 1957 to check consistency.

Using ITTC 1957:
$\log_{10} R_{n,m} = \log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^7) = 7.176$

$C_{F,m} = \dfrac{0.075}{(7.176 - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{26.78} = 2.801 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 3 — Back-calculate $S_m$.**
$S_m = \dfrac{R_{F,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^2 C_{F,m}} = \dfrac{12.5}{0.5 \times 1000 \times (2.5)^2 \times 2.801 \times 10^{-3}}$

$S_m = \dfrac{12.5}{8.753} = 1.428 \text{ m}^2$

This wetted surface area is consistent with a 6 m model. Verification complete.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing model and prototype** Reynolds numbers in the ITTC 1978 correction formula. Always compute both $C_{F,m}$ and $C_{F,p}$.
> 2. **Mixing up units of $\nu$** – $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s is the *standard freshwater* value. Sea water is $1.19 \times 10^{-6}$.
> 3. **Forgetting that ITTC 1957 is for turbulent flow only** – if $R_n < 5 \times 10^5$, the formula is invalid. State the assumption explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscous (frictional) resistance $R_F$** is the *dominant* component of total ship resistance at low-to-moderate Froude numbers, accounting for **60–80 %** of $R_T$ for displacement ships.
- The canonical formula $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ is **the single most-tested equation** in KTU Ship Resistance papers.
- The **ITTC 1957** line $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ is the default correlation; **Schoenherr** is the more rigorous alternative.
- The **form factor $k$** converts flat-plate $C_F$ to ship-form $C_V$ via $C_V = (1 + k) C_F$.
- **Wetted surface area $S$** for a ship is empirically estimated as $S \approx C_s \sqrt{\nabla L_{PP}}$, where $C_s \approx 2.0$–$2.2$ for typical merchant hulls (use the **Mumford formula** or **Harvald formula**).
- The **Reynolds number** decides the boundary-layer regime: $R_n < 5 \times 10^5$ is laminar, $R_n > 5 \times 10^6$ is turbulent.
- Always convert **knots to m/s** by multiplying by 0.5144.
- **Sea water at 15 °C:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
- **Fresh water at 20 °C:** $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
- The **ITTC 1978** correlation resistance allowance $C_A$ is a separate empirical add-on (typically $0.0004$) used in model-to-full-scale extrapolation – do NOT confuse it with $C_F$.
- For 14-mark derivations, always show **all five steps** of the $R_F$ derivation explicitly; partial credit is lost when steps are skipped.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscous Pressure Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscous Pressure Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In ship hydrodynamics, the **Total Resistance (R_T)** of a vessel moving through a real (viscous) fluid is decomposed into multiple physical components. The viscous portion of this resistance arises solely from the shear stresses (tangential) and normal pressure stresses generated by the fluid's viscosity as it flows past the wetted hull surface.

$$R_{VP} \;=\; R_{F} \;+\; R_{FP}$$

where:
- $R_{F}$ = **Frictional Resistance** — caused by tangential viscous shear stress ($\tau_w$) acting parallel to the hull surface.
- $R_{FP}$ = **Viscous Pressure Resistance (Form Drag)** — caused by the pressure differences induced by viscous normal stresses in regions of non-uniform boundary layer development, particularly due to flow separation and three-dimensional stern flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** *Viscous Pressure Resistance* ($R_{VP}$) is the portion of total resistance that scales with the **frictional resistance** of an equivalent flat plate. It is the **non-frictional** part of viscous resistance — i.e., the resistance attributable to hull form and viscous pressure effects, *not* to skin friction alone.

The total resistance is conventionally split as:

$$R_{T} \;=\; R_{F} \;+\; R_{VP} \;+\; R_{W}$$

where $R_{W}$ = **Wave-making resistance** (a pressure resistance of *inviscid* origin, generated by gravity waves).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine wading through a swimming pool:
- **Frictional resistance** is the *rubbing* drag you feel against your skin — your body's wet surface drags the surrounding water along, and that tangential "stickiness" pulls you back.
- **Viscous pressure resistance** is the *shape* drag — if you turn sideways and try to walk, the water piles up in front of you, separates behind your back, and forms low-pressure wake eddies. The pressure difference (high in front, low behind) literally *sucks* you backward.

> [!NOTE]
> For a perfectly streamlined, slender ship hull at modest Froude numbers, $R_{F}$ dominates ($R_{F} \approx 60\text{–}80\%$ of $R_{T}$). For bluff hull forms or full-form vessels, $R_{VP}$ can grow substantially, sometimes rivaling $R_{F}$.

A **flat plate** aligned with the flow has, by definition, **zero** viscous pressure resistance — only friction acts on it. The viscous pressure resistance is therefore a *measure of how much worse than a flat plate the ship hull behaves*, due to its shape.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Merchant Ships) |
|---|---|---|
| Form Factor | $k$ | $0.15 - 0.45$ |
| Appendage Factor | $k_{A}$ | $1.0 - 3.0$ (multiplier on $R_{F}$ for appendages) |
| Reynolds Number (model) | $R_{n,m}$ | $10^{6} - 10^{7}$ |
| Reynolds Number (ship) | $R_{n,s}$ | $10^{8} - 10^{9}$ |
| Frictional Coefficient (ITTC-57) | $C_{F}$ | $0.001 - 0.003$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The form factor $k$ is the dimensionless ratio **$R_{VP}/R_{F}$** — the KTU examiner expects students to be fluent with this definition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Does Viscous Pressure Resistance Exist?

In an **inviscid fluid**, Bernoulli's equation guarantees that a body moving steadily through the fluid experiences only normal pressure forces that integrate to **zero net force** in the direction of motion (D'Alembert's paradox). In a **real viscous fluid**, this balance is broken by two mechanisms:

1. **Tangential shear stress** in the boundary layer creates the frictional resistance $R_{F}$.
2. **Viscous dissipation** thickens the boundary layer asymmetrically around a three-dimensional body, producing an **adverse pressure gradient** especially near the stern. The flow may even **separate**, leaving a low-energy wake. The normal pressure forces around the hull no longer cancel — there is a net **suction** in the wake direction, yielding $R_{FP}$.

Thus:
- For a flat plate → boundary layer remains thin, no separation → $R_{FP} = 0$
- For a ship hull → three-dimensional shape, stern separation → $R_{FP} > 0$

## 2.2 The Form Factor Equation

A classical assumption (still the cornerstone of ship resistance prediction) is that **all viscous resistance scales with the frictional resistance of an equivalent flat plate** of the same wetted area and length. The proportionality constant is the **form factor** $k$:

$$R_{VP} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F}$$

Rearranging:
$$R_{VP} \;=\; R_{F} + k \cdot R_{F} \;=\; R_{F} + R_{FP}$$

Therefore:
$$k \;=\; \frac{R_{FP}}{R_{F}} \;=\; \frac{R_{VP} - R_{F}}{R_{F}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{T} = R_{F} + R_{VP} + R_{W}$ | Total resistance decomposition | Newtons [N] |
| 2 | $R_{VP} = (1+k)\,R_{F}$ | Viscous resistance from form factor | [N] |
| 3 | $k = R_{FP}/R_{F}$ | Form factor definition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{F} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_{n} - 2)^{2}}$ | ITTC-57 friction line (1957) | $R_{n} = V L / \nu$ |
| 5 | $C_{F} = \dfrac{0.4631}{(\log R_{n})^{2.6}}$ | Schoenherr friction line | turbulent flow |
| 6 | $\dfrac{0.242}{\sqrt{C_{F}}} = \log(R_{n}\,C_{F})$ | Prandtl-Schlichting implicit form | solve iteratively |
| 7 | $C_{T} = (1+k)\,C_{F} + C_{W}$ | Coefficient decomposition | non-dimensional |
| 8 | $C_{T} = C_{F} + C_{R}$ | Older notation: $C_{R}$ = residuary resistance | includes wave + viscous pressure |
| 9 | $R_{n} = \dfrac{V\,L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (fresh, 20°C) |
| 10 | $C_{F,s} = C_{F,m} \cdot (L_s/L_m)^{0.148}$ | Reynolds-scaling law (ITTC-78) | for friction scaling only |
| 11 | $C_{A} = k_{A}\,C_{F,s}$ | Appendage resistance coefficient | $k_{A}$ is appendage factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Distinction:** $R_{VP}$ contains **only** the viscous (form) part — it does *not* contain wave resistance. In older textbooks, "residuary resistance" $R_{R}$ was used, which combined $R_{VP}$ and $R_{W}$. The 2024 scheme explicitly separates them.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull-form optimization:** A hull designer aims to minimize $k$. Slender, fine-entry, balanced hulls typically achieve $k \approx 0.15$–$0.20$. Blocky or full vessels may reach $k > 0.4$.
- **Powering prediction:** Once $k$ is known (from model tests or CFD), the ship's viscous resistance at any speed is predicted by combining it with the friction line. This drives engine selection, fuel consumption estimates, and contractual speed guarantees.
- **Appendage design:** Bossing, shafts, struts, and rudders each add a small form factor ($k_{A,shaft}$, $k_{A,strut}$, etc.). The total $R_{VP}$ includes these via an effective appendage correction $k_{A} \cdot R_{F}$.

## 2.5 Practical Determination of $k$

Since $k$ cannot be computed analytically for a ship hull, it must be **extracted from model tests**. The most common KTU-relevant methods are:

1. **Prohaska's Method** — uses two model tests at low Froude numbers where $R_{W} \approx 0$ to extrapolate the linear $R_{F}$–$R_{n}^{-0.148}$ relationship and find $k$.
2. **Hughes' Method** — uses three model tests.
3. **CFD-based** — modern Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) solvers can compute $k$ numerically using the "virtual flat plate" approach (e.g., from the Prohaska-style decomposition of computed viscous forces).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence Between Frictional Resistance Definitions

**Starting point:** The total resistance coefficient of a ship is decomposed as:

$$C_{T} \;=\; (1 + k)\,C_{F} \;+\; C_{W}$$

**Step 1** — Multiply both sides by $\frac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}$ (where $S$ is wetted surface area):

$$R_{T} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F} \;+\; R_{W}$$

**Step 2** — Expand the right-hand side:

$$R_{T} \;=\; R_{F} + k\,R_{F} + R_{W}$$

**Step 3** — Identify the viscous pressure resistance as the term in $k$:

$$R_{VP} \;=\; k\,R_{F} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F} - R_{F}$$

**Conclusion:** Viscous pressure resistance is the *excess* of total viscous resistance over the friction of an equivalent flat plate. This excess arises purely from the hull's three-dimensional shape.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frictional Resistance from a Turbulent Flat-Plate Formula

Using the **ITTC-57 correlation** for a turbulent flat plate:

$$C_{F} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n} - 2)^{2}}$$

**Step 1** — Recall the resistance-force form of $C_{F}$:

$$R_{F} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,S\,V^{2}\,C_{F}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the ITTC-57 expression:

$$R_{F} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,S\,V^{2}\,\frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n} - 2)^{2}}$$

**Step 3** — Insert $R_{n} = VL/\nu$:

$$R_{F} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,S\,V^{2}\,\frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(VL/\nu) - 2)^{2}}$$

This is the **canonical frictional resistance equation** used as a building block for $R_{VP}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A model of a ship is tested at speed $V_m = 2.0$ m/s in fresh water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The model length is $L_m = 5.0$ m, wetted area $S_m = 7.5$ m². The total resistance measured is $R_{T,m} = 14.6$ N. Wave resistance is negligible at this low speed. Determine the form factor $k$.

**Step 1 — Compute the model Reynolds number:**

$$R_{n,m} \;=\; \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{2.0 \times 5.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ITTC-57 friction line:**

$$C_{F,m} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.0 \times 10^{7}) - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(7.0 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{25} \;=\; 0.00300$$

**Step 3 — Compute the frictional resistance of the model:**

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\rho S_m V_m^{2} C_{F,m} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1000 \times 7.5 \times (2.0)^{2} \times 0.00300$$

Evaluating step by step:

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1000 \times 7.5 \times 4.0 \times 0.00300$$

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; 15000 \times 0.00300 \;=\; 45.0 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Since $R_{W} \approx 0$ at low Froude number:**

$$R_{T,m} \;\approx\; R_{VP,m} \;=\; (1+k)\,R_{F,m}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k$:**

$$(1 + k) \;=\; \frac{R_{T,m}}{R_{F,m}} \;=\; \frac{14.6}{45.0} \;=\; 0.3244$$

$$k \;=\; 0.3244 - 1 \;=\; -0.6756$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative** $k$ is unphysical! This means our assumption that the model is in the *fully turbulent* regime is wrong — at $R_{n,m} = 10^{7}$ with such a small measured resistance, the boundary layer is **laminar-dominated**, and the ITTC-57 (which is purely turbulent) over-predicts friction. In practice, KTU students would use the **ITTC-78** formulation with a transitional allowance, or apply Prohaska's method (see §3.4).

**Corrected procedure (Prohaska, no wave):**

Re-test at a slightly higher speed where flow is fully turbulent, or use Schoenherr's line at the corrected $R_{n}$. The corrected $C_{F}$ (e.g., Schoenherr) gives a smaller $R_{F}$, making $k$ positive. For KTU exams, **always state your friction-line assumption explicitly**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Prohaska's Form-Factor Extraction

**Concept:** At low Froude numbers, $R_{W} \approx 0$. Then:

$$R_{T} \;\approx\; (1 + k)\,R_{F}$$

Express $R_{F}$ via the friction line in the form:

$$R_{F} \;=\; a\,R_{n}^{-b}$$

with $a$, $b$ constants from the chosen friction line. For ITTC-57, $b \approx 0.148$ (in $C_{F}$-form), so:

$$R_{T} \;\approx\; a\,(1 + k)\,R_{n}^{-b}$$

**Step 1** — Take the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln R_{T} \;\approx\; \ln[a\,(1+k)] - b\,\ln R_{n}$$

**Step 2** — Plot $\ln R_{T}$ versus $\ln R_{n}$ using two model tests at low $F_{n}$. The slope is $-b$ (which should be near $0.148$ if the friction line is correct); if not, the slope reveals the effective $b$.

**Step 3** — Extrapolate the best-fit line to the ship Reynolds number. The intercept gives $\ln[a\,(1+k)]$, from which $k$ is obtained:

$$k \;=\; \frac{e^{\text{intercept}}}{a} - 1$$

This is the **standard KTU approach** for experimental form-factor determination.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Component Behaviour

| Component | Origin | Scales With | Speed Power Law (approx.) | Dominates At |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frictional $R_{F}$ | Skin friction | $S$, $V^{1.83}$ | $V^{1.83}$ | All speeds; full-form ships |
| Viscous Pressure $R_{FP}$ | Form drag | $k \cdot R_{F}$ | $\sim V^{1.83}$ | All speeds; bluff forms |
| Wave $R_{W}$ | Gravity waves | $V^{n}$ (Havelock) | $V^{4}$–$V^{6}$ | Higher $F_{n}$ (>0.20) |
| Total $R_{T}$ | Sum | — | Non-monotonic | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Total Resistance Decomposition (KTU Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Resistance R_T"] --> B["Viscous Resistance R_V"]
    A --> C["Wave Resistance R_W"]
    B --> D["Frictional Resistance R_F"]
    B --> E["Viscous Pressure Resistance R_FP"]
    D --> F["Skin Friction from Boundary Layer Shear"]
    E --> G["Form Drag from Pressure Asymmetry"]
    E --> H["Stern Flow Separation Wake"]
    F --> I["Governed by R_n and Friction Line ITTC-57"]
    G --> I
    I --> J["Form Factor k = R_FP divided by R_F"]
    H --> J
    C --> K["Governed by F_n and Hull Slenderness"]
    J --> L["R_VP equals 1 plus k times R_F"]
    K --> M["R_T equals R_VP plus R_W"]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Process Diagram: Physical Origin of Viscous Pressure Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["Fluid Approaches Hull"] --> step2["Boundary Layer Forms on Hull"]
    step2 --> step3["Boundary Layer Thickens Toward Stern"]
    step3 --> step4["Adverse Pressure Gradient Develops"]
    step4 --> step5{"Does Flow Separate?"}
    step5 -- Yes --> step6["Wake Region of Low Pressure Forms"]
    step5 -- No --> step7["Mild Pressure Asymmetry"]
    step6 --> step8["Net Form Drag R_FP Generated"]
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9["Combine with Skin Friction to get R_VP"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Friction Line / Form Factor Prediction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Inputs: V, L, S, nu, rho, F_n"] --> P2["Compute R_n = V L divided by nu"]
    P2 --> P3["Choose Friction Line: ITTC-57, Schoenherr, or Prandtl-Schlichting"]
    P3 --> P4["Compute C_F from Friction Line"]
    P4 --> P5["Compute R_F = 0.5 rho S V squared C_F"]
    P5 --> P6["Apply Form Factor k from Model Test or Empirical Estimate"]
    P6 --> P7["Compute R_VP = 1 plus k times R_F"]
    P7 --> P8["Estimate R_W from Series or CFD"]
    P8 --> P9["Sum: R_T = R_VP plus R_W"]
    P9 --> P10["Compute Effective Power P_E = R_T times V"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

*Define the term **viscous pressure resistance** of a ship. How is it distinguished from wave-making resistance?*

**Model Answer:**

Viscous pressure resistance ($R_{VP}$ or $R_{FP}$) is the component of a ship's total resistance that arises from the **viscous normal pressure stresses** acting on the hull, primarily caused by asymmetric boundary layer development, three-dimensional flow effects, and stern flow separation. It is the **non-frictional** part of viscous resistance and is quantified as:

$$R_{VP} \;=\; k\,R_{F} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F} - R_{F}$$

where $k$ is the form factor. It is distinguished from wave-making resistance in that:

- **$R_{VP}$** is caused by **viscous effects** — it vanishes in an inviscid fluid and scales with the Reynolds number.
- **$R_{W}$** is caused by **gravity-wave generation** — it is an inviscid phenomenon that depends on the Froude number and hull form slenderness.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the formula, 1 for the distinction.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*State the **ITTC-57 friction line formula** and explain its use in computing viscous resistance.*

**Model Answer:**

$$C_{F} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n} - 2)^{2}}$$

The frictional resistance is then:

$$R_{F} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2} C_{F}$$

**Use:** The ITTC-57 formula gives the **turbulent flat-plate frictional coefficient** as a function of Reynolds number $R_{n} = VL/\nu$. It is used in ship resistance prediction to compute the **baseline frictional resistance** of an equivalent flat plate with the same wetted area $S$ and length $L$ as the ship. The actual ship's viscous resistance is then obtained by multiplying $R_{F}$ by $(1 + k)$, where $k$ is the form factor from model tests.

(3 marks: 1 for the formula, 1 for the resistance force form, 1 for the role of the friction line.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship $R_{VP} = (1 + k)\,R_{F}$ starting from the total resistance decomposition. Define every term. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A ship model of length $L_m = 6.0$ m, wetted area $S_m = 9.0$ m² is tested in fresh water at $V_m = 2.5$ m/s. Total resistance measured is $R_{T,m} = 28.0$ N. Froude number is low enough that wave resistance is negligible. Using ITTC-57, determine the **form factor** $k$ for the model. State any physical assumption you make. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

Starting from the **decomposition of total resistance** of a ship moving in a real viscous fluid:

$$R_{T} \;=\; R_{F} \;+\; R_{VP} \;+\; R_{W}$$

where:
- $R_{T}$ = total resistance
- $R_{F}$ = frictional resistance (tangential viscous shear)
- $R_{VP}$ = viscous pressure resistance (form drag)
- $R_{W}$ = wave-making resistance (inviscid, gravitational)

The **form factor assumption** postulates that all viscous resistance of a ship is **proportional** to the frictional resistance of an equivalent flat plate of the same wetted area $S$ and length $L$. The proportionality constant is $(1+k)$:

$$R_{F} + R_{VP} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{VP} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F} - R_{F} \;=\; k\,R_{F}$$

and the form factor is defined as:

$$k \;=\; \frac{R_{VP}}{R_{F}}$$

Substituting back into the total-resistance equation:

$$R_{T} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F} \;+\; R_{W}$$

This is the **canonical ship-resistance prediction equation** and is the basis of all ITTC-78 style powering predictions. (7 marks: 2 for the decomposition, 2 for the proportionality assumption, 2 for the algebraic derivation, 1 for the final canonical form.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Physical assumption:**

Assume the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** on the model (justified because $R_{n,m} > 5 \times 10^{5}$). Then ITTC-57 applies directly.

**Step 2 — Compute model Reynolds number** (fresh water, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s):

$$R_{n,m} \;=\; \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{2.5 \times 6.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3 — Apply ITTC-57:**

$$C_{F,m} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^{7}) - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(7.1761 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(5.1761)^{2}}$$

$$C_{F,m} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{26.792} \;=\; 0.002800$$

**Step 4 — Compute frictional resistance:**

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\rho S_m V_m^{2} C_{F,m} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1000 \times 9.0 \times (2.5)^{2} \times 0.002800$$

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1000 \times 9.0 \times 6.25 \times 0.002800$$

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; 28125 \times 0.002800 \;=\; 78.75 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Use $R_{W} = 0$ assumption:**

$$R_{T,m} \;=\; (1 + k)\,R_{F,m}$$

$$(1 + k) \;=\; \frac{R_{T,m}}{R_{F,m}} \;=\; \frac{28.0}{78.75} \;=\; 0.3556$$

$$k \;=\; 0.3556 - 1 \;=\; -0.6444$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:** A negative form factor is **physically impossible**! This indicates a serious problem — most likely the model is operating in the **transitional** boundary-layer regime, where ITTC-57 over-predicts friction. A KTU examiner will deduct up to **2 marks** for not catching this and not applying the **ITTC-78 transitional allowance** (i.e., adding a $0.0004$ correction to $C_{F}$). Correct procedure: $C_{F,\text{trans}} = C_{F,\text{turb}} + 0.0004$, recompute, and check if $k$ becomes positive.

(7 marks: 1 for the assumption, 2 for $C_{F}$, 2 for $R_{F}$, 2 for $k$.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **Prohaska's method** for determining the form factor $k$ from model tests. What is its main limitation? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A series of model tests is conducted on a 4.0 m model in a towing tank. The results are tabulated below. Using Prohaska's method, determine the form factor $k$ of the model. (7 Marks)

| Speed $V_m$ (m/s) | Reynolds $R_{n,m}$ ($\times 10^{6}$) | Total Resistance $R_{T,m}$ (N) |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
| 1.5 | 6.0 | 11.5 |
| 2.0 | 8.0 | 21.0 |

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Prohaska's Method — Procedure:**

Prohaska's method (1968) is the classical **graphical/extrapolation technique** for extracting the form factor $k$ from resistance model tests at **low Froude numbers** where wave-making resistance is negligibly small.

**Key idea:** At low $F_{n}$, $R_{W} \approx 0$ and the total resistance is approximately:

$$R_{T} \;\approx\; (1 + k)\,R_{F}$$

The ITTC-type friction line can be written in power-law form:

$$C_{F} \;=\; a\,R_{n}^{-b} \quad \text{with} \;\; b \approx 0.148 \;\; \text{(ITTC-78 equivalent)}$$

Hence:

$$R_{T} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2} \,(1 + k) \,a\,R_{n}^{-b}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\ln R_{T} \;\approx\; \text{const} - b\,\ln R_{n}$$

A **plot of $\ln R_{T}$ vs. $\ln R_{n}$** at low $F_{n}$ should yield a **straight line of slope $-b$** ($\approx -0.148$). Extrapolation of this line to the **ship Reynolds number** gives the value of $R_{T,\text{viscous, ship}}$ that would be measured if there were no wave-making. Then:

$$k \;=\; \frac{R_{T,\text{viscous, ship}} - R_{F,\text{ship}}}{R_{F,\text{ship}}}$$

**Main Limitation:** Prohaska's method requires the model tests to be conducted at **low Froude number** (typically $F_{n} < 0.15$) to ensure that $R_{W} \approx 0$. For vessels where wave resistance becomes significant even at low speeds (e.g., slender high-speed craft, multi-hulls), the method becomes inaccurate. It is also sensitive to turbulence stimulation of the model boundary layer.

(7 marks: 3 for procedure, 2 for the mathematical basis, 2 for limitations.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Take logarithms of the data:**

| $\ln R_{n,m}$ | $\ln R_{T,m}$ |
|---|---|
| $\ln(4.0 \times 10^{6}) = 15.201$ | $\ln(5.0) = 1.609$ |
| $\ln(6.0 \times 10^{6}) = 15.608$ | $\ln(11.5) = 2.442$ |
| $\ln(8.0 \times 10^{6}) = 15.894$ | $\ln(21.0) = 3.045$ |

**Step 2 — Check the slope.** Differences in $\ln R_{T}$ over differences in $\ln R_{n}$:

$$\text{slope}_1 = \frac{2.442 - 1.609}{15.608 - 15.201} = \frac{0.833}{0.407} = 2.046$$

$$\text{slope}_2 = \frac{3.045 - 2.442}{15.894 - 15.608} = \frac{0.603}{0.286} = 2.108$$

Average slope $\approx 2.08$ (positive!). This is anomalous because $\ln R_{T}$ vs. $\ln R_{n}$ should give a *negative* slope ($-b$) when $R_{W} \approx 0$. The positive slope implies wave resistance is **NOT negligible** in this data set, even at these low Froude numbers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students blindly apply Prohaska's formula and obtain a wildly wrong $k$. The slope sign check is a **mandatory** first step. If the slope is positive, Prohaska's method is **invalid** for this dataset. (1 mark lost for missing this.)

**Step 3 — Fallback: use the lowest Froude-number test only** (where $R_{W}$ is smallest):

Assuming $R_{W} \approx 0$ at $V_m = 1.0$ m/s, $R_{n,m} = 4.0 \times 10^{6}$:

ITTC-57 friction coefficient:

$$C_{F,m} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(4.0 \times 10^{6}) - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(6.602 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{21.194} \;=\; 0.003539$$

Frictional resistance:

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1000 \times S_m \times (1.0)^{2} \times 0.003539 \;=\; 1.7695 \cdot S_m$$

Without $S_m$ given explicitly, we use a typical model wetted area ratio. (In an exam, the problem would provide $S_m$.) For illustration, taking $S_m = 6.0$ m²:

$$R_{F,m} \;=\; 1.7695 \times 6.0 \;=\; 10.62 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $k$:**

$$(1 + k) \;=\; \frac{R_{T,m}}{R_{F,m}} \;=\; \frac{5.0}{10.62} \;=\; 0.4708$$

$$k \;=\; -0.5292$$

Once again, the result is unphysical — confirming that **transitional boundary layer effects** or **inadequate model size** are at play. The proper corrective action is to apply the **ITTC-78 transitional allowance** $C_{A} = 0.0004$ or use Hughes' three-point method.

(7 marks: 2 for the slope analysis, 2 for the friction-line application, 2 for the $k$ calculation, 1 for the transitional-correction discussion.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Viscous Pressure Resistance**

- **Definition:** $R_{VP}$ = viscous pressure resistance = form drag = $k \cdot R_{F}$. It is the part of viscous resistance **beyond** flat-plate skin friction.
- **Form factor:** $k = R_{VP}/R_{F}$ — **dimensionless**, typically $0.15$–$0.45$ for merchant hulls.
- **Key equation:** $R_{VP} = (1 + k)\,R_{F}$, so $R_{T} = (1 + k)\,R_{F} + R_{W}$.
- **Friction lines (memorize one):** ITTC-57: $C_{F} = 0.075 / (\log R_{n} - 2)^{2}$.
- **Reynolds number:** $R_{n} = VL/\nu$. **Always** include correct units of $\nu$.
- **Origin of $R_{VP}$:** three-dimensional boundary layer + stern flow separation → asymmetric pressure → net form drag.
- **Prohaska's method:** plots $\ln R_{T}$ vs $\ln R_{n}$ at low $F_{n}$; slope should be $\approx -0.148$ if $R_{W} \approx 0$.
- **Pitfalls:** (i) Negative $k$ → boundary layer is transitional, apply ITTC-78 correction; (ii) Mixing $R_{VP}$ with $R_{W}$ → remember the 2024 scheme separates them; (iii) Forgetting to multiply by $\frac{1}{2}\rho S V^{2}$ when going from $C_{F}$ to $R_{F}$.
- **Appendages:** Use appendage factor $k_{A}$: $R_{A} = k_{A}\,R_{F}$. Typical: shafts $\approx 0.3$, struts $\approx 0.4$, rudders $\approx 0.2$ (each on top of hull $k$).
- **Physical intuition:** $R_{VP}$ is what makes a *bluff* hull worse than a *slender* one — it is a **shape penalty** in viscous terms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
