<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Ship Resistance Fundamentals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
In the systematic progression of ship resistance estimation, once the frictional resistance ($R_F$) and the residual resistance ($R_R$) are independently evaluated, the *form factor* — commonly denoted by the symbol **$k$** (and sometimes $(1+k)$ when incorporating the bare-hull viscous multiplier) — serves as the **empirical viscous-resistance scaling coefficient**. It accounts for the additional viscous pressure drag attributable to the three-dimensional, non-streamlined geometry of a ship’s hull, which is not captured by the two-dimensional flat-plate frictional line used in the ITTC 1978 frictional formulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (High-Yield for KTU ESE)**
> The form factor $(1+k)$ is a **dimensionless multiplier** (no units) that augments the ITTC 1957 friction line to produce the model-scale viscous resistance:
> $$R_V = (1+k) \, R_F$$
> where $R_F$ is the frictional resistance computed from the ITTC 1978 formula, and $R_R$ is the residual resistance obtained from model testing. Together: $R_T = R_F + R_R$ (model) and $R_V = (1+k) R_F$ (model).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine swimming in a **bathing pool vs. a strong current in a river**. Even if the water feels equally "sticky" on your skin (which represents the frictional resistance $R_F$), the river water sloshing around your body creates extra pressure pushing you back. That extra push depends entirely on the **shape of your body** — broad-shouldered swimmers feel more push than streamlined Olympic swimmers. The form factor $(1+k)$ is essentially a **"shape penalty" number** that quantifies how much *extra* viscous drag your hull's specific shape causes, beyond what a flat plate of the same wetted area would experience.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it exists:** The ITTC 1957 friction line was derived assuming a **flat plate** in turbulent flow. Real ships are 3-D, blunt, curved bodies. Form factor $(1+k)$ corrects this 2-D-to-3-D gap. Standard ranges:
> - Full-form cargo ship: $k = 0.3$ to $0.5$
> - Fine-form container ship: $k = 0.1$ to $0.2$
> - Very slender warships: $k \approx 0.05$ to $0.1$

---

### KTU Visual Representation — Block Architecture
To support intuitive understanding, see **SECTION 4** for a full Mermaid block diagram showing where $(1+k)$ fits in the **resistance decomposition pipeline**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Three-Resistance Decomposition

The total ship resistance $R_T$ is rigorously partitioned using **Froude's hypothesis** (1877), which assumes independence between wave-making and viscous phenomena. On the **model scale**, the total resistance is:

$$R_{T,M} = R_{F,M} + R_{R,M}$$

where:
- $R_{F,M}$ = frictional resistance (computed analytically from ITTC 1978)
- $R_{R,M}$ = residual resistance (measured from model towing tests)

The *viscous resistance* of the ship, however, is not just $R_F$ — it includes the **form-effect** component. Hence:

$$R_{V,M} = (1+k) \, R_{F,M}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconciliation Identity (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> $R_R = R_V - R_F = k \, R_F$
> Therefore: $\boxed{R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R}$ is the standard model-scale decomposition.

## 2.2 Why $(1+k)$ and Not Just $k$?
- **$k$** is sometimes called the "form factor proper" — the *additional* viscous drag due to shape.
- **$(1+k)$** is the *viscous multiplier* — the total viscous drag normalized to the flat-plate friction.
- KTU examiners prefer using **$(1+k)$** in the formula. Both forms are accepted but **$(1+k)$ is convention**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $R_T = R_F + R_R$ | Froude's decomposition (model) | Model test analysis |
| 2 | $R_V = (1+k) R_F$ | Viscous resistance via form factor | Hull-form design |
| 3 | $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$ | Combined model resistance | Towing tank evaluation |
| 4 | $C_T = (1+k) C_F + C_R$ | Resistance coefficients form | Coefficient-based scaling |
| 5 | $C_V = (1+k) C_F$ | Viscous resistance coefficient | Reynolds-scaling prep |
| 6 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 1978 friction line | Friction calculation |
| 7 | $R_n = \dfrac{V L_{WL}}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (model/ship) | Flow regime check |
| 8 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L_{WL}}}$ | Froude number | Wave resistance check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Values (memorize for KTU ESE):**
> - $k_{\text{cargo}} \approx 0.4$ ; $k_{\text{tanker}} \approx 0.3$ ; $k_{\text{container}} \approx 0.2$ ; $k_{\text{Naval}} \approx 0.1$
> - $C_F$ from ITTC 1978 is the **mandatory** correlation for KTU problems unless otherwise stated.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Hull-form optimization**: Naval architects iterate hull lines to **minimize $(1+k)$**, reducing fuel consumption.
- **Powering predictions**: Used in the ITTC 1978 scaling procedure to extrapolate model results to full-scale ships.
- **CFD validation**: Form factors derived from experiments serve as **benchmarks** for viscous-flow CFD codes.
- **Contract specifications**: Shipowners specify permissible $C_T$ or $(1+k)$ bands in build contracts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Model-Scale Resistance Identity

The derivation of $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$ follows directly from the **Froude Hypothesis** (independence of wave and viscous phenomena) and the **empirical definition** of form factor.

### Step 1 — Froude's Original Decomposition
Total ship resistance at model scale, $R_{T,M}$, was proposed by William Froude (1877) to consist of two physically independent components:

$$R_{T,M} = R_{F,M} + R_{R,M}$$

**Logic:** Frictional resistance depends on Reynolds number only; wave/residual resistance depends on Froude number only. Empirically, the two were assumed to superpose linearly. This assumption — although approximate — forms the **foundation of model-to-ship extrapolation**.

### Step 2 — Identification of the "Form-Effect" Viscous Component
The actual viscous resistance of a 3-D ship hull is **not** identical to the frictional resistance of an equivalent flat plate of the same wetted area $S$. The ratio of true viscous resistance to flat-plate friction defines the form factor:

$$1+k = \frac{R_{V,\text{ship}}}{R_{F,\text{flat plate}}}$$

Rearranging:

$$R_V = (1+k) \, R_F$$

**Logic:** The dimensionless multiplier $(1+k)$ scales up the 2-D flat-plate friction to account for 3-D hull effects — including flow separation, cross-sectional shape variation, and pressure-induced viscous drag.

### Step 3 — Substituting into Total Resistance
By definition, the residual resistance measured in a model test is:

$$R_R = R_T - R_F$$

This $R_R$ is the measured quantity from towing tank tests. The *actual* viscous resistance of the ship is $(1+k) R_F$, which is **greater than** $R_F$. The difference between true viscous and 2-D frictional is:

$$R_V - R_F = (1+k) R_F - R_F = k \, R_F$$

In the ITTC 1978 procedure, the **measured $R_R$** from the model is taken to be the **wave-making component only**, while the form effect is treated separately through $(1+k)$. Therefore the standard decomposition used for scaling is:

$$\boxed{R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R}$$

### Step 4 — Conversion to Coefficient Form (Optional, but Frequently Tested)
Dividing through by $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S$:

$$C_T = (1+k) C_F + C_R$$

where:
- $C_T = R_T / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S)$
- $C_F = R_F / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S)$
- $C_R = R_R / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S)$

This coefficient form is the **basis of the ITTC 1978 scaling method**.

### Step 5 — Application in Full-Scale Extrapolation
At full scale (ship), the procedure is:

$$C_{T,S} = (1+k) \, C_{F,S} + C_R$$

where $C_{F,S}$ is the ITTC 1957/1978 line evaluated at ship Reynolds number, and $C_R$ is held **constant from model to ship** (since $F_n$ similarity ensures wave resistance scales with $F_n$ only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state explicitly: *"The residual resistance coefficient $C_R$ is assumed to be the same for model and ship because Froude similarity is preserved, while $C_F$ is recalculated at the ship Reynolds number using ITTC 1978."*

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A model tested at $V_M = 2.5$ m/s gives $R_{T,M} = 15$ N. The frictional resistance calculated from ITTC 1978 is $R_{F,M} = 10$ N. Compute $(1+k)$ and the form-effect viscous resistance.

**Solution:**

$R_R = R_T - R_F = 15 - 10 = 5$ N

To find $(1+k)$, we need $R_{V,\text{measured}}$ or another data point. **However**, the standard interpretation in model analysis where form factor is known is:

$R_V = (1+k) R_F = (1+k) \times 10$

If $k = 0.4$, then $(1+k) = 1.4$:

$R_V = 1.4 \times 10 = 14$ N
Form-effect component $= k R_F = 0.4 \times 10 = 4$ N

The wave residual $C_R \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S = 5$ N.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse $R_R$ (residual) with $R_V$ (viscous). They are different — $R_R$ contains wave resistance; $R_V$ contains only viscous form effects. KTU examiners often set traps here.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Position of Form Factor in Resistance Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resistance R sub T] --> B[Froude Hypothesis]
    B --> C[Model Scale Test]
    B --> D[Friction Line ITTC 1978]
    
    C --> E[Measured R sub T,M]
    D --> F[R sub F,M from ITTC Line]
    
    E --> G[Residual Resistance R sub R = R sub T - R sub F]
    F --> H[Form Factor Multiplier 1+k]
    
    H --> I[Viscous Resistance R sub V = 1+k times R sub F]
    
    G --> J[Full Resistance Identity R sub T = 1+k R sub F + R sub R]
    I --> J
    
    J --> K[Reynolds Scaling to Full Ship]
    K --> L[Ship Scale R sub T,S = 1+k C sub F,S half rho V squared S + C sub R half rho V squared S]
    
    L --> M[Effective Power P sub E = R sub T times V]
    M --> N[Brake Power P sub B = P sub E over eta sub D eta sub H]
```

## 4.2 Component Breakdown Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Ship Hull Geometry] --> Q[Viscous Pressure Drag 3D Effect]
    P --> R[Wave Making Drag]
    P --> S[Air Resistance]
    P --> T[Appendage Drag]
    
    Q --> U[Form Factor 1+k Multiplier]
    U --> V[Corrected Viscous Component R sub V]
    
    R --> W[Residual R sub R from Towing Tank]
    S --> X[Wind Correction]
    T --> Y[Appendage Correction]
    
    V --> Z[Total Effective Resistance]
    W --> Z
    X --> Z
    Y --> Z
```

## 4.3 Empirical Determination Methods for $(1+k)$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Form Factor 1+k Determination] --> B[Method 1 Holtrop Mensaar]
    A --> C[Method 2 Prohaska Method]
    A --> D[Method 3 Granville Method]
    A --> E[Method 4 Direct CFD RANS]
    
    B --> B1[Empirical Regression from Parent Forms]
    C --> C1[Two-Speed Model Test Extrapolation]
    D --> D1[1958 Granville Scaling]
    E --> E1[Modern 3D Viscous Solver]
    
    B1 --> F[Selected 1+k for Design]
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define form factor $(1+k)$ in ship hydrodynamics. Mention typical values for a cargo ship and a fine-form container vessel.**

**Model Answer:**
The form factor $(1+k)$ is a dimensionless empirical multiplier used to scale the two-dimensional flat-plate frictional resistance $R_F$ (computed from the ITTC 1978 friction line) to account for the additional viscous pressure drag of a three-dimensional ship hull. It is defined as:

$$1+k = \frac{R_{V,\text{ship}}}{R_{F,\text{flat plate}}}$$

**Typical Values:**
- Full-form cargo ship: $k \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ ; $(1+k) \approx 1.3$ to $1.5$
- Fine-form container ship: $k \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$ ; $(1+k) \approx 1.1$ to $1.2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note (3-mark question):** Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for typical values. Missing typical values → lose 1 mark.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State Froude's hypothesis of resistance decomposition. Write the expression for total resistance using the form factor.**

**Model Answer:**
**Froude's Hypothesis (1877):** The total resistance of a ship can be decomposed into two independent components — (i) the frictional (viscous) resistance, which depends on Reynolds number, and (ii) the wave-making (residual) resistance, which depends on Froude number. These can be superposed linearly.

**Expression with form factor:**

$$R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$$

where $R_F$ is the ITTC 1978 frictional resistance and $R_R$ is the residual resistance from model tests.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Modeled]

**(a)** Starting from Froude's hypothesis, derive the expression for total ship resistance in terms of the form factor $(1+k)$, frictional resistance $R_F$, and residual resistance $R_R$. Clearly explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A model of length $L_M = 6$ m is tested at $V_M = 2.0$ m/s. The measured total resistance is $R_{T,M} = 12$ N. The ITTC 1978 frictional resistance for the model is $R_{F,M} = 8$ N, and the form factor is $k = 0.5$. Determine: (i) the residual resistance, (ii) the viscous resistance using the form factor, and (iii) verify the identity $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Froude's Decomposition**
According to Froude, total resistance at model scale is:
$$R_{T,M} = R_{F,M} + R_{R,M}$$
where $R_{F,M}$ is frictional (Reynolds-dependent) and $R_{R,M}$ is residual/wave resistance (Froude-dependent).

**Step 2: Definition of Form Factor**
The true viscous resistance of a 3-D ship hull is greater than the 2-D flat-plate friction $R_F$. The ratio defines the form factor:
$$1+k = \frac{R_V}{R_F} \implies R_V = (1+k) R_F$$

**Step 3: Total Resistance Identity**
Adding the residual resistance (from model test) to the corrected viscous resistance:
$$R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$$

**Physical Significance:**
- $(1+k)$: shape-penalty multiplier (dimensionless, ~1.1–1.5).
- $R_F$: friction on equivalent flat plate (ITTC 1978).
- $R_R$: wave-making + form-induced residual (model-measured).

**Mark Distribution (Part a):** 2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Step 2, 2 marks for Step 3, 1 mark for physical significance.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $R_{T,M} = 12$ N ; $R_{F,M} = 8$ N ; $k = 0.5$ ; so $(1+k) = 1.5$

**(i) Residual resistance:**
$$R_R = R_{T,M} - R_{F,M} = 12 - 8 = 4 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Viscous resistance using form factor:**
$$R_V = (1+k) R_F = 1.5 \times 8 = 12 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Verification of identity:**
$$(1+k) R_F + R_R = 1.5 \times 8 + 4 = 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ N}$$

But $R_T = 12$ N. **Discrepancy = 4 N.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warning):**
> In this problem, $R_{T,M}$ given (12 N) is the **bare-hull measured total**, and the identity $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$ should equal the measured $R_{T,M}$. The 4 N discrepancy indicates that the **form factor must be derived from a separate procedure** (e.g., Prohaska or Granville) — not assumed. Students often blindly assume $k=0.5$ without consistency check. For full marks, derive $k$ from: $R_{T,M} = (1+k) R_F + R_R \Rightarrow 12 = (1+k) \times 8 + 4 \Rightarrow (1+k) = 1 \Rightarrow k = 0$ (which is **physically unrealistic**, signaling that either the given $R_{T,M}$ or $k$ is inconsistent). **State this inconsistency in the answer for full credit (1 mark reserved for critical analysis).**

**Corrected interpretation:** If $R_{T,M}=16$ N was intended, then $k=0.5$ is consistent. The 12 N value creates a paradox that **must be acknowledged**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Modeled]

**(a)** Explain the ITTC 1978 extrapolation procedure. How is the form factor $(1+k)$ used to scale model results to full-scale ship resistance? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of length $L_S = 200$ m is to operate at $V_S = 15$ knots. Using ITTC 1978 friction line, compute the frictional resistance coefficient $C_{F,S}$ at full scale. Take kinematic viscosity of seawater $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — ITTC 1978 Procedure (7 Marks):**

The ITTC 1978 extrapolation converts model resistance to full-scale ship resistance as follows:

**Step 1: Model test** at Froude similarity: $F_{n,M} = F_{n,S}$. Measure $R_{T,M}$.

**Step 2: Friction line** — Compute $C_{F,M}$ and $C_{F,S}$ from ITTC 1978:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Step 3: Compute $C_R$ from model:**
$$C_{R,M} = \frac{R_{T,M}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_M^2 S_M} - C_{F,M}$$

**Step 4: Apply form factor** to scale viscous component:
$$C_{V,M} = (1+k) C_{F,M}$$

**Step 5: Full-scale ship total coefficient:**
$$C_{T,S} = (1+k) C_{F,S} + C_R$$

**Step 6: Full-scale resistance:**
$$R_{T,S} = C_{T,S} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_S^2 S_S$$

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per step (6 marks) + 1 mark for writing the final formula clearly.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $L_S = 200$ m ; $V_S = 15$ knots = $15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s ; $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Reynolds number:**
$$R_n = \frac{V_S L_S}{\nu} = \frac{7.716 \times 200}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_n = \frac{1543.2}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.297 \times 10^9$$

**Step 2: ITTC 1978 friction coefficient:**
$$C_{F,S} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.297 \times 10^9) - 2)^2}$$

$\log_{10}(1.297 \times 10^9) = 9.113$

$$C_{F,S} = \frac{0.075}{(9.113 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.113)^2} = \frac{0.075}{50.59} = 0.001483$$

$$\boxed{C_{F,S} \approx 1.483 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks unit conversion, 2 marks Reynolds number, 2 marks friction formula, 1 mark final numerical answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting knot-to-m/s conversion** → wrong Reynolds number → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $\log_e$ instead of $\log_{10}$** in ITTC formula → wrong answer by factor of ~5 → lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Not including form factor** in extrapolation: students often write $C_{T,S} = C_{F,S} + C_R$ without $(1+k)$ — this is a **2-mark penalty** as it violates ITTC 1978 procedure.
> 4. **Forgetting the bare-hull $(1+k)$ applies to model viscous only**, not the full $C_{T,S}$ additively — common confusion.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Form factor** $(1+k)$ is a **dimensionless multiplier** (~1.1 to 1.5) that converts 2-D flat-plate friction $R_F$ into 3-D ship viscous resistance $R_V$.
- **Core Identity:** $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_R$ (must be memorized for ESE).
- **Coefficient form:** $C_T = (1+k) C_F + C_R$.
- **ITTC 1978 line:** $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ — always with $\log_{10}$.
- **Typical $k$ values:** Cargo ~0.4, Tanker ~0.3, Container ~0.2, Naval ~0.1.
- **Froude hypothesis** = independence of wave (residual) and viscous (frictional) resistance.
- **Scaling rule:** $C_R$ stays constant model→ship; $C_F$ is recomputed at ship $R_n$ using ITTC 1978.
- **Methods to find $(1+k)$:** Holtrop-Mensaar (regression), Prohaska (two-speed test), Granville (1958 scaling), CFD (modern).
- **Common exam traps:** Confusing $R_V$ with $R_R$; using $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$; omitting $(1+k)$ in extrapolation; mixing units (knot vs m/s).
- **Marks distribution reminder:** For 14-mark Q, Part (a) 7 marks (derivation/derivation logic) + Part (b) 7 marks (numerical). For 3-mark Q, definition + formula + values/uses = full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Roughness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hull Roughness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### Formal Academic Definition

**Hull Roughness** is a quantitative measure of the irregularities present on the wetted surface of a ship's hull. It is conventionally expressed as an **equivalent sand roughness height ($k_s$)**, defined as the diameter of a uniformly sized sand grain that would produce the same frictional resistance as the actual rough surface under fully rough turbulent boundary layer conditions. Hull roughness is measured in **micrometers ($\mu m$)** or **meters (m)** when used in dimensionless correlations.

The standard roughness values adopted by the **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC)** for design purposes are:

- **New ship with anti-fouling paint:** $k_s = 150 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 150 \, \mu m$
- **Lightly fouled condition:** $k_s = 200 - 300 \, \mu m$
- **Moderately fouled:** $k_s = 300 - 500 \, \mu m$
- **Heavily fouled / poorly maintained:** $k_s > 500 \, \mu m$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Hull roughness is treated under Module 1 as a primary contributor to **frictional resistance augmentation**. The student must be able to (i) state the ITTC roughness allowance formula, (ii) compute its numerical impact, and (iii) explain the physical regimes of the boundary layer that govern this behavior.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a flat wooden plank across a still pond. If the wood is freshly sanded and smooth, it glides with relatively little effort — this represents a **hydrodynamically smooth hull**.

Now imagine the same plank after it has been left in the ocean for six months. Algae, slime, and tiny barnacles coat its surface like a thin layer of Velcro. You now have to push noticeably harder to maintain the same speed — this is what happens to a **rough, fouled hull**.

In real terms, a tanker or bulk carrier can lose **5% to 20%** of its propulsive efficiency due to hull roughness alone. For a large vessel burning 50–100 tonnes of fuel per day, this single phenomenon can translate into **hundreds of thousands of dollars in additional annual fuel cost** — making hull roughness one of the most commercially significant topics in ship resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Reference:**
> Kinematic viscosity of seawater at 15°C: $\nu \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$
> Density of seawater: $\rho \approx 1025 \, kg/m^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Why Roughness Increases Resistance

When a fluid flows past a solid surface, a thin region known as the **boundary layer** forms, where viscous effects dominate. The character of this layer depends critically on the size of the surface irregularities relative to the viscous sublayer thickness:

- If the roughness elements are **buried inside the viscous sublayer**, the surface behaves as if it were smooth → **Hydrodynamically Smooth Regime**.
- If the roughness elements **protrude well beyond the viscous sublayer**, they continuously disturb the flow, generating form drag on each protrusion → **Fully Rough Regime**.
- Between these extremes lies the **Transitional Regime**, where the flow exhibits characteristics of both.

The governing dimensionless parameter is the **Roughness Reynolds Number**:

$$k^+ = \frac{k_s \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}$$

where $u_\tau$ is the friction velocity defined as $u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$, with $\tau_w$ being the wall shear stress.

The three regimes are:

| Regime | Range of $k^+$ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrodynamically Smooth | $k^+ < 5$ | Roughness fully submerged; no effect |
| Transitional | $5 \leq k^+ \leq 70$ | Increasing influence of roughness |
| Fully Rough | $k^+ > 70$ | Roughness fully dominates; $C_F$ independent of $Rn$ |

> [!TIP]
> At typical ship service speeds, $k^+$ is generally **above 70**, placing commercial ship hulls in the **fully rough regime** for most of their operational life.

---

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **minimum required formulas** for any KTU examination question on hull roughness. Memorize these and their applicability limits.

| # | Formula | Description / Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 1978 frictional coefficient for smooth hull (turbulent flow) |
| 2 | $\Delta C_F = \left[105 \left(\dfrac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} - 0.64 \right] \times 10^{-3}$ | ITTC roughness allowance (added to smooth $C_F$) |
| 3 | $R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number based on ship length and speed |
| 4 | $k^+ = \dfrac{k_s \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}$ | Roughness Reynolds number (regime indicator) |
| 5 | $u_\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho}}$ | Friction velocity |
| 6 | $C_T = C_F + \Delta C_F + C_R + C_{AA}$ | Total resistance coefficient decomposition |
| 7 | $\Delta P \approx P_{E} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C_F}{C_T}$ | Approximate power penalty from roughness |

In Formula 6:
- $C_T$ = Total resistance coefficient
- $C_F$ = Frictional resistance coefficient (smooth)
- $\Delta C_F$ = Roughness allowance
- $C_R$ = Residuary resistance coefficient
- $C_{AA}$ = Air resistance coefficient

---

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

The roughness allowance is **not just a textbook correction**. It plays three critical engineering roles:

1. **Powering Prediction:** Model tests are conducted on smooth painted hulls in fresh water. The roughness allowance is added to the model-derived resistance to obtain full-scale ship resistance predictions, accounting for the inevitable degradation between drydocking intervals.
2. **Operational Economics:** Ship operators use the same formulation to estimate the **economic optimum hull cleaning interval** — cleaning too often wastes money on dry-dock time; cleaning too late wastes enormous quantities of fuel.
3. **Regulatory & Class Society Compliance:** Speed-power-consumption warranties issued by shipyards are based on assumed hull roughness values. Any significant deviation becomes a contractual issue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the ITTC 1978 Smooth-Hull Frictional Coefficient

The ITTC 1978 line is an empirical fit to the Schoenherr mean line and is valid only for **turbulent flow** over a smooth flat plate.

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is presented in KTU textbooks as a "standard reference formula". It is **not derived from first principles** during examinations; instead, students are expected to know its form and apply it. However, its origin lies in the Prandtl-von Kármán universal velocity profile integrated across the turbulent boundary layer.

**The Prandtl-von Kármán log-law for turbulent flow is:**

$$\frac{u}{u_\tau} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}\right) + B$$

where $\kappa = 0.41$ (von Kármán constant) and $B \approx 5.0$ for smooth surfaces.

Integrating this momentum equation across the boundary layer thickness and matching with experimental data (notably the Schoenherr line) yields the empirical ITTC form. This is a **semi-empirical derivation** accepted as standard in naval architecture.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the ITTC Roughness Allowance

The roughness allowance corrects the smooth-hull frictional coefficient for a real, rough ship hull. It is based on the **velocity defect law** for fully rough turbulent flow.

**Starting Point — Velocity Profile for Fully Rough Flow:**

For fully rough turbulent flow, the log-law shifts upward due to roughness elements. The velocity profile becomes:

$$\frac{u}{u_\tau} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y}{k_s}\right) + C_{rough}$$

The shift in the log-law (compared to smooth flow) is quantified by the **roughness function** $\Delta U^+$:

$$\Delta U^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln(k^+) + \text{const}$$

**Step 1:** For a ship, the mean friction velocity is related to $C_F$ by:

$$C_F = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2} = 2\left(\frac{u_\tau}{V}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Applying this relation to both smooth and rough conditions and taking the difference leads to the empirical correction:

$$\Delta C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}\left[\text{roughness correction factor}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Through extensive analysis of full-scale trials and roughness measurements, the ITTC standardized the following compact empirical formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_F &= \left[105 \left(\frac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} - 0.64 \right] \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Derivation Note:** This formula is **empirically calibrated** against the velocity-defect law. The constants 105 and 0.64 are regression fits from towing-tank and full-scale correlation data, not pure theoretical constants. For the KTU examination, students are required to **apply** this formula, not re-derive the empirical constants.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A bulk carrier of length $L = 200 \, m$ operates at service speed $V = 15 \, \text{knots}$. The hull roughness is $k_s = 300 \, \mu m$ (moderately fouled). Seawater kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$. Calculate (a) Reynolds number, (b) smooth $C_F$, (c) roughness allowance, and (d) percentage increase in frictional resistance.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 200 \, m$
- $V = 15 \, \text{knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \, m/s$
- $k_s = 300 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 3 \times 10^{-4} \, m$
- $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$

#### (a) Reynolds Number

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} \\
&= \frac{7.716 \times 200}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{1543.2}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 1.4697 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Smooth-Hull Frictional Coefficient (ITTC 1978)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} R_n &= \log_{10}(1.4697 \times 10^9) = 9.1672 \\
\log_{10} R_n - 2 &= 9.1672 - 2 = 7.1672 \\
(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 &= (7.1672)^2 = 51.36 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{51.36} \\
&= 1.460 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Roughness Allowance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_s}{L} &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{200} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \\
\left(\frac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} &= (1.5)^{1/3} \times 10^{-2} = 1.1447 \times 10^{-2} \\
\Delta C_F &= \left[105 \times 1.1447 \times 10^{-2} - 0.64 \right] \times 10^{-3} \\
&= \left[1.2019 - 0.64\right] \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.5619 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (d) Percentage Increase in Frictional Resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Percentage Increase} &= \frac{\Delta C_F}{C_F} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{0.5619 \times 10^{-3}}{1.460 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\% \\
&= 38.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A roughness of just $300 \, \mu m$ (less than the thickness of three sheets of paper) increases the frictional resistance by nearly **38.5%**. This single calculation is why hull maintenance is one of the highest-priority operational concerns in commercial shipping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Boundary Layer Regime Classification Flow

The following functional architecture diagram maps the logical flow used to classify a ship's hull boundary layer into smooth, transitional, or fully rough regimes based on the roughness Reynolds number.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Compute k plus] --> B[Calculate u tau from CF]
    B --> C[Evaluate k plus = ks times u tau divided by nu]
    C --> D{k plus less than 5?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Hyrdodynamically Smooth Regime]
    D -- No --> F{k plus between 5 and 70?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Transitional Regime]
    F -- No --> H[Fully Rough Regime]
    E --> I[CF depends only on Rn]
    G --> J[Apply ITTC roughness correction]
    H --> K[CF depends on ks but not Rn]
    I --> L[Apply ITTC 1978 formula only]
    J --> M[Use ITTC allowance formula]
    K --> M
    L --> N[Total Resistance]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Hull Roughness Lifecycle & Resistance Build-Up Architecture

This diagram illustrates how hull roughness evolves over the service life of a vessel and how each stage contributes to the total resistance coefficient decomposition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Launch] --> B[k equals 150 micrometer]
    B --> C[Service Months 1 to 6]
    C --> D[k rises to 200 micrometer]
    D --> E[Service Months 6 to 18]
    E --> F[k rises to 300 micrometer]
    F --> G[Service Months 18 to 36]
    G --> H[k rises to 500 micrometer]
    H --> I{Operator Decision}
    I -- Clean Hull --> B
    I -- Continue Service --> J[Service Months 36 plus]
    J --> K[k exceeds 1000 micrometer]
    K --> L[Major Fuel Penalty]
    L --> M[Mandatory Dry Dock]
    M --> B
```

### 4.3 Resistance Coefficient Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[CT Total Resistance Coefficient] --> CF[CF Frictional Smooth]
    R --> DELTA[Delta CF Roughness Allowance]
    R --> CR[CR Residuary Resistance]
    R --> CAA[CAA Air Resistance]
    CF --> F1[ITTC 1978 Equation]
    DELTA --> F2[ITTC Allowance Formula]
    CR --> F3[Wave Making plus Viscous Pressure]
    CAA --> F4[Aerodynamic Drag]
    F1 --> OUT[Final CT for Powering]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    F4 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define hull roughness. List any four common causes of hull roughness in service.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Hull roughness is the quantitative measure of the surface irregularities on the wetted area of a ship's hull, conventionally expressed as an equivalent sand roughness height $k_s$ in micrometers, representing the size of a uniform sand grain that would produce the same frictional drag as the actual rough surface under fully rough turbulent flow.

**Four Causes (½ Mark each, total 2 Marks):**

1. **Marine Fouling** — accumulation of slime, barnacles, mussels, and weed growth on the underwater hull.
2. **Paint Deterioration** — loss of smoothness of anti-fouling paint due to aging, oxidation, or improper application.
3. **Corrosion Pitting** — electrochemical degradation of steel surfaces creating micro-cavities, particularly in way of ballast tanks and near the waterline.
4. **Mechanical Damage and Welding Irregularities** — dents from berthing contact, grounding abrasion, and residual weld bead roughness on plating.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State the ITTC formula for roughness allowance and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Formula (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\Delta C_F = \left[105 \left(\frac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} - 0.64 \right] \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Explanation of Each Term (1.5 Marks):**

- $\Delta C_F$ — the dimensionless increase in the frictional resistance coefficient due to hull roughness, added to the smooth-hull $C_F$ to obtain the actual frictional coefficient.
- $k_s$ — equivalent sand roughness height in meters; the larger the roughness, the greater the resistance penalty.
- $L$ — length of the ship in meters; enters via the cube root, indicating that longer ships are proportionally less sensitive to a given absolute roughness.
- The constants 105 and 0.64 are empirically calibrated by the ITTC from full-scale correlation data.

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the three regimes of turbulent boundary layer flow with respect to surface roughness, clearly defining the roughness Reynolds number and the threshold values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A container ship of length $L = 250 \, m$ operates at speed $V = 20 \, \text{knots}$ with hull roughness $k_s = 200 \, \mu m$. Take $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$. Calculate the smooth-hull frictional coefficient $C_F$, the roughness allowance $\Delta C_F$, and the percentage increase in frictional resistance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Three Regimes (7 Marks):**

The behaviour of a turbulent boundary layer over a rough surface is governed by the **roughness Reynolds number**:

$$k^+ = \frac{k_s \cdot u_\tau}{\nu}$$

**Regime 1 — Hydrodynamically Smooth ($k^+ < 5$):** (2 Marks)
The roughness elements are entirely submerged within the viscous sublayer. The flow does not "see" the roughness, and the surface behaves as if hydrodynamically smooth. The friction coefficient depends only on the Reynolds number, identical to the smooth-wall case.

**Regime 2 — Transitional ($5 \leq k^+ \leq 70$):** (2 Marks)
The roughness elements begin to protrude through the viscous sublayer but do not fully dominate. The boundary layer experiences **partial** roughness effects, and $C_F$ depends on **both** $R_n$ and $k_s$. Empirical interpolation is required between smooth and fully rough asymptotes.

**Regime 3 — Fully Rough ($k^+ > 70$):** (2 Marks)
The roughness elements project well beyond the viscous sublayer into the turbulent outer layer. They continuously shed vortices and contribute direct form drag. The friction coefficient becomes **independent of Reynolds number** and depends solely on the relative roughness $k_s/L$. This is the regime applicable to most operational ships.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** For most commercial vessels at service speed, $k^+$ is generally above 70, placing them firmly in the fully rough regime, validating the use of the ITTC roughness allowance formula.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $L = 250 \, m$, $V = 20 \, \text{knots}$, $k_s = 200 \, \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, m$, $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$.

**Step 1 — Velocity in SI units:** (0.5 Mark)
$$V = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288 \, m/s$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number:** (1 Mark)
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{V L}{\nu} = \frac{10.288 \times 250}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{2572}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.4495 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Smooth-Hull $C_F$:** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} R_n &= \log_{10}(2.4495 \times 10^9) = 9.3890 \\
\log_{10} R_n - 2 &= 7.3890 \\
(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 &= 54.60 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{54.60} = 1.374 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Roughness Allowance:** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_s}{L} &= \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{250} = 8 \times 10^{-7} \\
\left(\frac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} &= (8)^{1/3} \times 10^{-7/3} = 2 \times 10^{-2.333} \\
&= 2 \times 4.6416 \times 10^{-3} = 9.283 \times 10^{-3} \\
\Delta C_F &= [105 \times 9.283 \times 10^{-3} - 0.64] \times 10^{-3} \\
&= [0.9747 - 0.64] \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.3347 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Increase:** (1.5 Marks)
$$
\text{Percentage Increase} = \frac{0.3347 \times 10^{-3}}{1.374 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\% = 24.36\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Unit Conversion (1 Mark Lost Frequently):** Forgetting to convert knots to m/s by the factor 0.5144. Always write: $V_{m/s} = V_{knots} \times 0.5144$.
> - **Reynolds Number Magnitude (1 Mark Lost Frequently):** Writing $R_n$ without proper magnitude or skipping the $\log_{10}$ calculation step.
> - **Cube Root Arithmetic (1 Mark Lost Frequently):** Errors in computing $(k_s/L)^{1/3}$. Write the intermediate step of separating the number and the power of 10 clearly.
> - **Final Units of $\Delta C_F$:** Students often omit the $10^{-3}$ factor at the end, producing an answer that is **1000 times too large**. Always include this final factor explicitly in your final answer line.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the effect of hull roughness on the economic operation of a commercial ship. Include in your answer a justification for periodic hull cleaning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A naval frigate of length $L = 130 \, m$ operates at speed $V = 27 \, \text{knots}$. The hull has roughness $k_s = 150 \, \mu m$ when freshly painted. After 24 months of service, the roughness grows to $k_s = 450 \, \mu m$. Compute the increase in effective power required if the residuary resistance coefficient is $C_R = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$. Take $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Economic Effect Discussion (7 Marks):**

**Increased Fuel Consumption (2 Marks):** Hull roughness elevates the frictional resistance coefficient, requiring greater thrust to maintain service speed. Since propulsive power scales with the cube of speed for a given resistance increment, even a few percent rise in $C_F$ translates into substantial additional bunker fuel consumption. For a VLCC burning 80 tonnes of fuel per day, a 10% rise in frictional resistance may add 5–8 tonnes of daily fuel use.

**Reduced Service Speed (1.5 Marks):** To maintain the contracted schedule, the main engine must deliver more power. If engine power is limited (MCR constraint), the ship slows down, cascading into charter party disputes and off-hire claims.

**Shaft RPM and Engine Load Implications (1.5 Marks):** Increased resistance raises the brake power demand, which elevates cylinder pressures, exhaust temperatures, and specific fuel oil consumption. This accelerates engine wear and maintenance costs.

**Hull Cleaning Economics (2 Marks):** Periodic hull cleaning and re-painting restores $k_s$ toward $150 \, \mu m$. The economic optimum cleaning interval balances (i) the cost of in-water hull cleaning (typically USD 50,000–150,000 per event for a large vessel) against (ii) the daily fuel saving from reduced resistance. Modern operators use the **Hull Roughness Penalty Calculator** based on ITTC formulas to determine this interval scientifically, often cleaning every 24–36 months.

---

**Part (b) — Power Increase Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $L = 130 \, m$, $V = 27 \, \text{knots}$, $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$, $C_R = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 1 — Convert speed:** (0.5 Mark)
$$V = 27 \times 0.5144 = 13.889 \, m/s$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number:** (0.5 Mark)
$$
R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu} = \frac{13.889 \times 130}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1805.6}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.7196 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 3 — Smooth $C_F$:** (1 Mark)
$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.7196 \times 10^9) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.235 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.235)^2} = \frac{0.075}{52.35} = 1.433 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total $C_T$ for both conditions:** (3 Marks)

**Case A — Fresh Paint ($k_s = 150 \, \mu m$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_s}{L} &= \frac{150 \times 10^{-6}}{130} = 1.154 \times 10^{-6} \\
\left(\frac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} &= (1.154)^{1/3} \times 10^{-2} = 1.0488 \times 10^{-2} \\
\Delta C_F^{(A)} &= [105 \times 1.0488 \times 10^{-2} - 0.64] \times 10^{-3} \\
&= [1.1012 - 0.64] \times 10^{-3} = 0.4612 \times 10^{-3} \\
C_T^{(A)} &= C_F + \Delta C_F^{(A)} + C_R = 1.433 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4612 \times 10^{-3} + 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 2.494 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B — Fouled Hull ($k_s = 450 \, \mu m$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_s}{L} &= \frac{450 \times 10^{-6}}{130} = 3.462 \times 10^{-6} \\
\left(\frac{k_s}{L}\right)^{1/3} &= (3.462)^{1/3} \times 10^{-2} = 1.512 \times 10^{-2} \\
\Delta C_F^{(B)} &= [105 \times 1.512 \times 10^{-2} - 0.64] \times 10^{-3} \\
&= [1.5876 - 0.64] \times 10^{-3} = 0.9476 \times 10^{-3} \\
C_T^{(B)} &= 1.433 \times 10^{-3} + 0.9476 \times 10^{-3} + 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 2.981 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Increase in Total Resistance:** (1 Mark)
$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{C_T^{(B)} - C_T^{(A)}}{C_T^{(A)}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.981 - 2.494}{2.494} \times 100\% = 19.53\%
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Power Increase (using $P_E \propto C_T V^3$):** (1 Mark)
Since speed is unchanged at 27 knots, the effective power scales directly with $C_T$:
$$
\% \text{ Increase in } P_E \approx 19.53\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Rounding Discipline (1 Mark):** Avoid accumulating rounding errors. Carry at least 4 significant figures in intermediate steps.
> - **Total Resistance Decomposition (1 Mark):** Students frequently forget to include $C_R$ when computing $C_T$. Always state the decomposition: $C_T = C_F + \Delta C_F + C_R + C_{AA}$.
> - **Air Resistance Omission (Acceptable):** For most KTU problems, $C_{AA}$ is taken as zero unless explicitly given; do not invent a value.
> - **Cube Root Calculation (1 Mark):** Showing the intermediate step of separating the number from the power of 10 prevents arithmetic slips.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Hull Roughness**

- **Hull roughness** is measured as equivalent sand roughness $k_s$ in **micrometers**. Standard ITTC new-ship value: **$k_s = 150 \, \mu m$**.
- **Three boundary layer regimes** governed by $k^+ = k_s u_\tau / \nu$: Smooth ($<5$), Transitional ($5$–$70$), Fully Rough ($>70$). Operational ships lie in the fully rough regime.
- **ITTC 1978 smooth $C_F$:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$.
- **ITTC Roughness Allowance:** $\Delta C_F = [105 (k_s/L)^{1/3} - 0.64] \times 10^{-3}$.
- **Total Resistance Build-Up:** $C_T = C_F + \Delta C_F + C_R + C_{AA}$.
- **Speed conversion:** $V_{m/s} = V_{knots} \times 0.5144$.
- **Reynolds number:** $R_n = V L / \nu$, typically of order $10^{9}$ for ships.
- **Cube root handling:** When computing $(k_s/L)^{1/3}$, separate the numerical part from the power-of-10 part — this prevents errors.
- **Final factor $10^{-3}$:** The ITTC allowance formula requires the $\times 10^{-3}$ at the end; omitting it inflates the answer by 1000.
- **Economic impact:** Hull roughness of just $300 \, \mu m$ can increase frictional resistance by **~30–40%**, translating to millions of dollars in annual fuel costs for large vessels.
- **Hull cleaning intervals:** Modern operators clean every 24–36 months to optimize between fuel penalty and cleaning cost.
- **Always include $C_R$** in total resistance decomposition when problem data provides it.
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** unit conversion, cube root arithmetic, forgetting the $10^{-3}$ factor, and missing $C_R$ in $C_T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Making Resistance - Pressure Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wave Making Resistance - Pressure Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCNST402 – Ship Resistance**, the total resistance $R_T$ encountered by a ship moving at a steady speed $V$ through a calm, deep, open water is conventionally decomposed using the **1957 ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference) correlation**, as:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R$$

where $R_F$ is the **frictional resistance** (viscous shear stress acting tangentially on the wetted hull surface) and $R_R$ is the **residuary resistance** (which encompasses all pressure-related effects including wave-making and eddy-making components). The pressure resistance, more rigorously, is defined as:

$$R_P = R_T - R_F = R_W + R_{WB} + R_{E}$$

where:
- $R_W$ = **Wave-making resistance** (energy radiated away as gravity waves)
- $R_{WB}$ = **Wave-breaking resistance** (energy lost at the bow and stern due to flow separation and surface breaking)
- $R_E$ = **Eddy resistance** (viscous form drag due to three-dimensional flow separation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition – Pressure Resistance**
> The pressure resistance $R_P$ is the component of total resistance arising from the **normal pressure forces** (not shear stresses) acting on the wetted hull. It is mathematically the integral of the pressure deficit (relative to the free-stream hydrostatic pressure) projected onto the ship's longitudinal axis. Wave-making resistance is the dominant component of pressure resistance for slender, well-formed hulls operating in the **displacement mode** at moderate to high Froude numbers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are walking briskly across a still pond. Your feet push water aside, and you can clearly see **transverse ripples** spreading out behind and to the sides of you. That energy spent creating those ripples is what naval architects call **wave-making resistance**. Now imagine a long, slim canoe versus a short, wide, blunt barge moving at the same speed. The canoe barely disturbs the surface because its narrow form slips through, but the barge pushes a tall, energy-consuming bow wave ahead of it — its wave-making resistance is much larger.

For a ship, the hull is essentially "pushing" the water out of its way continuously. The reaction to that push is felt as a retarding force. Whenever the water surface is forced to oscillate and propagate away as a wave, the ship has lost kinetic energy to that wave — that loss is the **wave-making resistance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Intuition – The "Bottleneck" Effect**
> When a ship moves faster, the wave system it generates becomes steeper and the energy carried away grows roughly as the **fourth power of speed** at low Froude numbers. This is why ships have a famous "hump" in their resistance curve around $F_n \approx 0.25$, caused by the **transverse wave system** constructively interfering with the **divergent wave system**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in the Wave-Making Domain

The governing dimensionless parameter for wave-making resistance is the **Froude number** $F_n$, which expresses the ratio of inertial to gravitational forces:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}}$$

where:
- $V$ = ship speed in **m/s**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $L_{PP}$ = length between perpendiculars in **m**

Other key geometric and dynamic metrics used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length-to-beam ratio | $L/B$ | – | Hull slenderness |
| Beam-to-draft ratio | $B/T$ | – | Hull fullness |
| Block coefficient | $C_B$ | – | Volumetric fullness |
| Prismatic coefficient | $C_P$ | – | Prismatic fullness |
| Reynolds number | $R_n$ | – | Viscous scaling |
| Wave resistance coefficient | $C_W$ | – | Dimensionless wave resistance |
| Wetted surface area | $S$ | m² | Friction reference area |
| Displacement volume | $\nabla$ | m³ | Buoyancy reference |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of Resistance

Following **Froude's Hypothesis of Resistance Independence (1872)**, which forms the foundation of all KTU 2024 Module 1 derivations, the total resistance of a ship is considered as the linear superposition of two physically independent components:

- **Frictional (viscous) component** $R_F$ — depends only on Reynolds number $R_n$ and wetted surface area $S$.
- **Residuary (pressure) component** $R_R$ — depends only on Froude number $F_n$ and hull form geometry.

This hypothesis is **physically approximate** but **engineering-sufficient** for displacement-mode vessels. It is the bedrock of model-to-ship extrapolation using the **Froude scaling law** and the **ITTC 1978 / ITTC 2017 friction lines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Independent Coefficients?**
> Friction is governed by **viscous shear** (a boundary-layer phenomenon, dominated by $R_n$), while wave-making is governed by **gravity-capillary wave generation** (a free-surface potential-flow phenomenon, dominated by $F_n$). The two phenomena act on different physical scales and can be decoupled for engineering purposes.

### 2.2 Physical Origin of Wave-Making Resistance

The wave-making resistance arises from the **asymmetric pressure distribution** around the hull. The pressure field around a moving ship does two things simultaneously:

1. It satisfies the **hull boundary condition** (no flow through the wetted surface).
2. It satisfies the **free-surface kinematic and dynamic conditions** (the water surface adjusts dynamically to support the pressure field).

The free-surface dynamic condition (the **Bernoulli equation** applied at the air-water interface) couples the pressure field to the wave elevation. Wherever a hull element produces a wave crest, there is a **low pressure** on the hull surface beneath that crest; wherever it produces a trough, there is a **high pressure**. Because the wave field generated by a moving ship is **asymmetric** (waves exist primarily behind the ship), the integrated pressure force has a net rearward component — the **wave-making resistance**.

### 2.3 Kelvin Wake Pattern

When a pressure point moves steadily on the free surface, it generates a **Kelvin wake** consisting of:

- **Transverse waves** — wave crests roughly perpendicular to the direction of motion, confined within a half-angle of about $\arcsin(1/3) \approx 19.47°$ from the longitudinal axis.
- **Divergent waves** — wave crests emanating at an angle, lying on the outer boundary of the Kelvin pattern.

The transverse and divergent wave systems can **interfere constructively or destructively** depending on the hull's length-to-beam ratio and the Froude number. This interference is the key to **hull form optimization** for low wave resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hump" in the Resistance Curve**
> At low Froude numbers, the divergent wave from the bow and the transverse wave from the stern arrive at the same transverse position behind the ship — this is **constructive interference**, and the wave resistance peaks. This peak is the famous **"hump"** around $F_n \approx 0.20 - 0.30$ that limits the efficient operating speed of displacement hulls.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}}$ | Froude number — primary scaling parameter for wave resistance. |
| 2 | $R_T = R_F + R_R$ | Froude's decomposition of total resistance. |
| 3 | $R_R = R_W + R_{WB} + R_E$ | Residuary (pressure) resistance split. |
| 4 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2}$ | Total resistance coefficient. |
| 5 | $C_F = \dfrac{R_F}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2}$ | Frictional resistance coefficient. |
| 6 | $C_R = C_T - C_F$ | Residuary resistance coefficient (model-scale). |
| 7 | $C_W \approx C_R$ (slender hulls) | Wave resistance coefficient (from model test). |
| 8 | $C_F^{ITTC2017} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 2017 frictional line (correlation). |
| 9 | $R_W \propto F_n^4$ (low $F_n$) | Speed dependence of wave resistance. |
| 10 | $R_W \propto e^{-c/F_n^2}$ (high $F_n$) | Exponential decay past the hump. |
| 11 | $C_{F, ship} = C_{F, model} + \Delta C_F$ | Form factor / roughness allowance addition. |
| 12 | $k_{FR} = C_{T,s} - C_{F,s} - (C_{T,m} - C_{F,m})$ | Froude's resistance interference coefficient. |
| 13 | $R_{n,L} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number based on length. |
| 14 | $F_n^2 = \dfrac{R_n^2 \cdot \nu^2}{g L^3}$ | $F_n$-$R_n$ coupling relation. |
| 15 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_s}{L_m}$ | Geometric scale ratio. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Restriction Alert**
> In all KTU answer scripts and model solutions, never write absolute values using the keyboard pipe $\vert$ inside markdown tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX form to prevent table rendering breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull form optimization** (Slender, fine-lined hulls with low $C_B$ minimize $R_W$).
- **Speed-vs-power selection** during concept design (the $R_W$ hump dictates economical service speeds).
- **Model-to-full-scale extrapolation** using Froude scaling (used by IACS-classed vessels and naval architects).
- **Barge vs. ship selection** for inland/coastal transport (low $F_n$ operation suppresses $R_W$).
- **Hydrofoil and planing craft design** (lifting the hull out of water eliminates $R_W$ at high speed).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Froude Number from Dimensional Analysis

**Objective:** Show that the wave-making phenomenon is governed by the Froude number $F_n$.

**Step 1 — List the physical variables that govern wave resistance:**
- Ship speed $V$ (units: $LT^{-1}$)
- Acceleration due to gravity $g$ (units: $LT^{-2}$)
- Ship length $L$ (units: $L$)
- Water density $\rho$ (units: $ML^{-3}$)
- Wave resistance $R_W$ (units: $MLT^{-2}$)

**Step 2 — Apply the Buckingham Pi Theorem.** With 5 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions ($M, L, T$), we obtain $5 - 3 = 2$ independent Pi groups.

**Step 3 — Construct the dimensionless resistance coefficient:**

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{R_W}{\rho V^2 L^2} = C_W$$

**Step 4 — Construct the Froude number as the second Pi group:**

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}} = F_n$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (functional relationship):**

$$C_W = f(F_n)$$

This means wave resistance, when properly non-dimensionalized, depends **only on $F_n$** (and implicitly on hull form parameters like $C_B$, $L/B$, $B/T$). This is the foundation of Froude's similarity law used in all model testing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Havelock's Thin-Ship Wave Resistance Formula

**Objective:** Derive the classical integral for wave resistance of a thin ship.

**Step 1 — Start with the linearized free-surface condition** for a disturbance moving with speed $V$:

$$\left(g - V^2 \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\right) \zeta(x,y) = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0}$$

where $\zeta$ is the wave elevation and $\phi$ is the velocity potential. This is the **Kelvin-Michell free-surface condition** in the reference frame moving with the ship.

**Step 2 — Replace the hull by a distribution of sources** along the ship's centerline (thin-ship approximation). For a source of strength $m(x)$ located at $(x, 0, 0)$, the induced potential at the free surface is:

$$\phi_{source}(x,y,0) = -\frac{m(\xi)}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-k(z-h) + i k (x-\xi)} }{1 - \frac{V^2 k}{g} \tanh(kh)} \cdot \frac{1}{2} dk$$

For deep water ($\tanh(kh) \to 1$), the denominator simplifies to $1 - V^2 k / g$.

**Step 3 — Use Green's theorem** to express the wave resistance as an integral of the cross-section area $S(x)$ and the source strength $m(x) = \frac{dS}{dx}$ (for a slender ship):

$$R_W = \frac{\rho g}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} m(x) e^{k x \sec^2 \theta} dx \right]^2 \sec^3 \theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the standard Michell form** with the substitution $k = (g/V^2) \sec^2 \theta$:

$$R_W = \frac{4 \rho g}{\pi V^2} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \left[ \int_{0}^{L} S(x) \cos\left(\frac{g x}{V^2} \sec^2 \theta\right) dx \right]^2 \sec^3 \theta \, d\theta$$

where $S(x)$ is the cross-sectional area distribution along the ship's length and $V^2 / g$ is the **speed-length ratio** $\sqrt{L} \cdot F_n$.

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

The integral is dominated by the integrand near $\theta = 0$ (transverse waves) and near the **stationary-phase angle** $\theta_s$ where $\sec^2 \theta_s = V^2/(2gL) = \tfrac{1}{2} F_n^{-2}$. This stationary-phase condition defines the **wave-resistance humps and hollows** as $F_n$ varies.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Froude's Interference Coefficient (Model Test Analysis)

**Objective:** Show how a model test in a towing tank extracts $R_W$ from the measured $R_T$.

**Step 1 — Model resistance decomposition:**
At model scale, the total measured resistance is:

$$R_{T,m} = R_{F,m} + R_{R,m}$$

**Step 2 — Frictional resistance at model scale (ITTC 1957 / 2017 line):**

$$C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,m} - 2)^2}$$

where $R_{n,m} = V_m L_m / \nu$.

**Step 3 — Residuary resistance at model scale:**

$$R_{R,m} = R_{T,m} - R_{F,m} = R_{W,m} + R_{WB,m} + R_{E,m}$$

**Step 4 — Froude scaling of $R_R$ to full scale:** Because $R_R$ is governed by $F_n$ (and $F_n$ is preserved in Froude scaling), we have:

$$\frac{R_{R,s}}{R_{R,m}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \lambda^3$$

For fresh-water model in seawater (or vice versa), a **density correction** $\sigma = \rho_s / \rho_m$ is applied.

**Step 5 — Ship-scale frictional resistance (using ship-scale Reynolds number):**

$$R_{F,s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s \cdot C_{F,s}$$

**Step 6 — Full-scale total resistance:**

$$R_{T,s} = R_{F,s} + R_{R,s} = R_{F,s} + \sigma \lambda^3 R_{R,m}$$

**Step 7 — Interference coefficient (Froude):** This coefficient measures the **interaction** between friction and wave-making:

$$k_{FR} = (C_{T,s} - C_{F,s}) - (C_{T,m} - C_{F,m})$$

A positive $k_{FR}$ indicates friction is augmented by wave-making; a negative value indicates wave-making is **suppressed** by the boundary layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Form Factor $k$**
> A more refined approach (Hughes, 1954) uses a **form factor** $k$ such that $C_{V} = (1 + k) C_F$ accounts for viscous-pressure interaction, and $R_W = R_T - R_V$. This is the basis of the modern **Prohaska / Hughes / Townsin** method used in industry.

### 4.3 Schematic Description of the Kelvin Wake Pattern

Since Mermaid cannot physically draw a ship's wave pattern, the following verbal schematic is provided for your KTU answer scripts:

```
                     Ship moving right ===> 
                                          |
            \    \    \    \    \         |
              \ ~~~ \ ~~~ \ ~~~ \ ~~~     |  <-- Divergent waves
                \~~~ \ ~~~ \ ~~~ \~~~     |       (outer envelope)
            ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~   |
            ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~   |  <-- Transverse waves
            ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~   |       (inner pattern)
            ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~ ~~~~~~   |
                                          |
                   Kelvin half-angle = arcsin(1/3) approx 19.47 deg
```

The divergent wave crests lie along the **outer boundary of the wake**, and the transverse crests lie on the **inner region** behind the ship. Their **interference pattern** determines the magnitude of $R_W$.
