Similarly, the **frictional drag** on all superstructure surfaces:

$$R_{f} = C_{f}\,(A_{W})\,\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a} V_{S}^{2}$$

Combining both (and noting that for a moving ship the relevant velocity is the **apparent wind** $V_A$, not $V_S$ alone):

$$R_{AA} = R_{F} + R_{f} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a}V_{A}^{2}\,(C_{F}A_{F} + C_{f}A_{W})$$

Defining the equivalent area $A_T$ and lumped coefficient $C_{AA}$:

$$C_{AA}\,A_{T} = C_{F}A_{F} + C_{f}A_{W}$$

we obtain the ITTC standard form:

$$\boxed{\;R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{a}\,C_{AA}\,A_{T}\,V_{A}^{2}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Apparent Wind Vector Magnitude

Let $\vec{V_S}$ be the ship velocity (positive forward) and $\vec{V_T}$ be the true wind velocity (positive in the direction the wind is blowing **towards**). The air velocity seen by the ship (apparent wind) is:

$$\vec{V_A} = \vec{V_T} + \vec{V_S}$$

Taking magnitudes via the law of cosines, with $\theta$ being the angle between $\vec{V_T}$ and $\vec{V_S}$:

$$V_{A}^{2} = V_{T}^{2} + V_{S}^{2} + 2 V_{T} V_{S} \cos\theta$$

For a **head wind**, $\vec{V_T}$ opposes $\vec{V_S}$, so $\theta = 180°$ between vectors as typically defined, giving $\cos\theta = -1$ if measured from the bow direction; adopting the engineering convention (angle measured such that head wind is $\theta = 0$, beam wind $\theta = 90°$):

$$\boxed{\;V_{A}^{2} = V_{T}^{2} + V_{S}^{2} + 2 V_{T} V_{S} \cos\theta\;}$$

**Head wind** ($\theta = 0°$):
$$V_A = V_S + V_T$$

**Beam wind** ($\theta = 90°$):
$$V_A = \sqrt{V_{S}^{2} + V_{T}^{2}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Air Resistance with Head Wind

**Given:** A 9000 TEU container ship with $A_T = 1800$ m², $C_{AA} = 0.90$, $V_S = 22$ knots, $V_T = 10$ knots (head wind), $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert knots to m/s** (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s):
$$V_S = 22 \times 0.5144 = 11.317 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_T = 10 \times 0.5144 = 5.144 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute apparent wind for head wind** ($\theta = 0°$):
$$V_A = V_S + V_T = 11.317 + 5.144 = 16.461 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the ITTC formula:**
$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{a}\,C_{AA}\,A_{T}\,V_{A}^{2}$$
$$R_{AA} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.90 \times 1800 \times (16.461)^{2}$$
$$R_{AA} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.90 \times 1800 \times 270.965$$
$$R_{AA} \approx 268.2 \text{ kN}$$

This represents the **aerodynamic drag** at the bow due to combined ship motion and head wind. For a ULCV, this corresponds to a shaft-power penalty of roughly 6–8 % at cruising speed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bulbous Bow Wave-Interference Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship Hull Moving at Velocity V] --> nodeB[Bow Wave Generation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Hull Transverse Wavelength lambda hull]
    nodeA2[Bulbous Bow Submerged Near WL] --> nodeB2[Bulb Wave Generation]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[Bulb Transverse Wavelength lambda bulb]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Superposition at Far Field]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE{Phase Difference at Design Fn}
    nodeE -->|180 degrees out of phase| nodeF[Destructive Interference]
    nodeE -->|In phase| nodeG[Constructive Interference Increased RW]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Net Wave Amplitude Reduced]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Wave Making Resistance RW Reduced 10 to 15 percent]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Resistance Penalty Ship Slows]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Fuel Saving and Improved EHP]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Air Resistance Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[True Wind VT Direction theta] --> nodeD[Apparent Wind Vector Sum]
    nodeB[Ship Velocity VS] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[VA Squared Equation]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Air Density rho a 1.225 kg per m3]
    nodeF --> nodeG[ITTC Formula]
    nodeC[Transverse Projected Area AT] --> nodeG
    nodeH[Coefficient CAA Geometry Roughness] --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeI[Air Resistance RAA in kN]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Added Effective Horsepower]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Total Resistance RT Updated]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Effect of Bulbous Bow on Resistance Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Total Ship Resistance RT] --> nodeB[Wave Making RW]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Frictional RF]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Air RAA]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Eddy RES]
    nodeB --> nodeF{With Bulbous Bow at Design Fn}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[RW Reduced 10 to 15 percent]
    nodeF -->|No Off design| nodeH[RW Unchanged or Increased]
    nodeC --> nodeI[RF Slightly Increased 1 to 2 percent due to Wetted Surface]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[RAA Unchanged Bulb Below WL]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Net RT Reduction]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **physical mechanism** of destructive interference.
> - The second maps the **computational pipeline** of the ITTC air-resistance formula.
> - The third shows the **net effect on total resistance** — making it clear that a bulbous bow only helps $R_W$ and slightly penalises $R_F$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**Define a bulbous bow. Why is it not effective across the entire operating speed range of a ship?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bulbous bow is a streamlined protuberance fitted below the waterline at the forward end of a ship's hull, designed to reduce wave-making resistance by generating a wave system that interferes destructively with the hull's bow wave **(1 mark)**.

It is not effective across the entire speed range because wave interference depends on the wavelength $\lambda = 2\pi L_{pp}F_n^2$, which varies with speed **(1 mark)**. The bulb is designed for a specific Froude number (typically $F_n = 0.20$–$0.45$); at other speeds, the phase relationship between the bulb-wave and hull-wave shifts, and the interference can become constructive, increasing resistance **(1 mark)**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**What is meant by apparent wind? Write the ITTC formula for air resistance and explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Apparent wind is the wind velocity relative to the moving ship, equal to the vector sum of the true wind velocity and the ship's velocity **(1 mark)**.

$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a} C_{AA} A_{T} V_{A}^{2}$$

where:
- $\rho_a$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³) **(0.5 mark)**
- $C_{AA}$ = air resistance coefficient (depends on hull form, superstructure geometry) **(0.5 mark)**
- $A_T$ = transverse projected area of the ship above the waterline **(0.5 mark)**
- $V_A$ = apparent wind speed (m/s) **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the principle of operation of a bulbous bow. List the main design parameters and their typical ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of length between perpendiculars $L_{pp} = 220$ m and beam $B = 32$ m is fitted with a bulbous bow having breadth $b = 1.9$ m, length $l = 9$ m. The design speed of the bulb is 14 knots. Calculate:
  (i) the design Froude number of the bulb, and
  (ii) the design wavelength of the transverse wave system.
Comment on the **effectiveness** of the bulbous bow at 10 knots and 18 knots. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle of Operation (7 marks):**

A bulbous bow is a streamlined protrusion fitted at the bow of a ship just below the design waterline **(1 mark)**. When the ship moves forward, the parent hull generates a transverse wave system of wavelength $\lambda$ (the Kelvin wave pattern). Simultaneously, the bulb — being submerged — generates its own wave system **(1 mark)**.

If the bulb is properly designed for the ship's operating speed, the bulb-wave crest coincides with the trough of the hull-wave, producing a phase difference of 180°. This **destructive interference** reduces the resultant wave amplitude **(1 mark)**. Since wave-making resistance is proportional to the square of the wave amplitude, even modest amplitude reduction yields significant $R_W$ reduction **(1 mark)**.

**Design parameters and typical ranges:**

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Breadth ratio $b/B$ | 0.05 – 0.08 |
| Length ratio $l/L_{pp}$ | 0.02 – 0.05 |
| Cross-section shape | Circular / elliptical / ogival |
| Vertical position | At or slightly below WL |
| Design Froude number $F_n$ | 0.20 – 0.45 |

**(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for the diagram description — student should draw a labelled sketch showing hull wave, bulb wave, and the superposed resultant wave)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s:**
$$V = 14 \text{ knots} = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Froude number at design speed:**
$$F_{n} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL_{pp}}} = \frac{7.2016}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 220}}$$

$$\sqrt{9.81 \times 220} = \sqrt{2158.2} = 46.456 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$F_{n} = \frac{7.2016}{46.456} = 0.155$$

**Step 3 — Design wavelength of transverse wave system:**
$$\lambda = 2\pi L_{pp} F_{n}^{2} = 2\pi \times 220 \times (0.155)^{2}$$

$$\lambda = 2\pi \times 220 \times 0.024025 = 33.21 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Effectiveness at 10 knots and 18 knots:**

At $V = 10$ knots $= 5.144$ m/s:
$$F_n = \frac{5.144}{46.456} = 0.111$$
$$\lambda_{10} = 2\pi \times 220 \times (0.111)^{2} = 17.0 \text{ m}$$

The wavelength is much shorter than the bulb length, so the bulb-wave is no longer out of phase — the bulb becomes **ineffective** at low speed, possibly even increasing $R_W$ **(1 mark)**.

At $V = 18$ knots $= 9.259$ m/s:
$$F_n = \frac{9.259}{46.456} = 0.199$$
$$\lambda_{18} = 2\pi \times 220 \times (0.199)^{2} = 54.8 \text{ m}$$

The wavelength is now much longer than the bulb length. The phase difference is no longer $\pi$; interference is partial. The bulb is **not optimised** for this speed, but partial benefit may exist **(1 mark)**.

**Conclusion:** The bulb is most effective **only at 14 knots** ($F_n = 0.155$), with sharp degradation away from this speed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** ⭐ *INTERNAL CHOICE*

**(a)** Derive the ITTC expression for the air resistance of a ship. State clearly the assumptions and discuss the factors that influence the air resistance coefficient $C_{AA}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 14,000 TEU container vessel has a transverse projected area above the waterline of 1850 m². The ship is proceeding at 24 knots in a **beam wind** of 18 knots true. Take $C_{AA} = 0.85$ and $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³. Compute:
  (i) the apparent wind speed, and
  (ii) the air resistance force.
  (iii) If the same ship were sailing in a **head wind of 18 knots**, by what factor would the air resistance change? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Air Resistance Formula (7 marks):**

Consider a ship moving with velocity $V_S$ through still air. The relative velocity of air with respect to the ship is $-V_S$. The ship experiences both **form (pressure) drag** $R_F$ on bluff bodies and **frictional (viscous) drag** $R_f$ on wetted surfaces **(1 mark)**.

**Form drag** (from pressure integration over frontal area $A_F$):
$$R_{F} = C_{F} \cdot A_{F} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a} V^{2}$$

**Frictional drag** (from shear stress on wetted surface $A_W$):
$$R_{f} = C_{f} \cdot A_{W} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a} V^{2}$$

Adding the two components:
$$R_{AA} = R_F + R_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a}V^{2}\,(C_{F}A_{F} + C_{f}A_{W})$$

Defining an **equivalent projected area** $A_T$ and a **lumped air-resistance coefficient** $C_{AA}$ such that $C_{AA}A_T = C_FA_F + C_fA_W$ **(1 mark)**, and recognising that the relevant velocity is the **apparent wind speed** $V_A$ **(1 mark)**:

$$\boxed{\;R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{a}C_{AA}A_{T}V_{A}^{2}\;}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady, incompressible flow of air.
2. $C_{AA}$ is a constant (low sensitivity to $Re$ in turbulent regime).
3. Above-waterline geometry is rigid and fixed.
4. Wind tunnel-style drag correlation is valid for full-scale ship.

**Factors affecting $C_{AA}$ (2 marks):**
- **Frontal area shape:** streamlined hull form gives lower $C_{AA}$.
- **Surface roughness:** paint condition, rust, marine fouling.
- **Superstructure layout:** shielding of aft structures by forward ones.
- **Apparent wind angle:** $C_{AA}$ varies with $\theta$.
- **Appendages:** masts, funnels, king-posts, deck cargo.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert knots to m/s:**
$$V_S = 24 \times 0.5144 = 12.346 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_T = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.259 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent wind for beam wind ($\theta = 90°$):**
$$V_A = \sqrt{V_{S}^{2} + V_{T}^{2}} = \sqrt{(12.346)^{2} + (9.259)^{2}}$$
$$V_A = \sqrt{152.42 + 85.73} = \sqrt{238.15} = 15.43 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Air resistance force:**
$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 1850 \times (15.43)^{2}$$
$$R_{AA} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 1850 \times 238.08$$
$$R_{AA} \approx 229.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Head wind case ($\theta = 0°$):**
$$V_{A,\text{head}} = V_S + V_T = 12.346 + 9.259 = 21.605 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Air resistance for head wind:**
$$R_{AA,\text{head}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 1850 \times (21.605)^{2}$$
$$R_{AA,\text{head}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 1850 \times 466.78$$
$$R_{AA,\text{head}} \approx 449.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Ratio:**
$$\frac{R_{AA,\text{head}}}{R_{AA,\text{beam}}} = \left(\frac{21.605}{15.43}\right)^{2} = (1.40)^{2} \approx 1.96$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT use the pipe `|` symbol in the table for absolute value — it will break markdown rendering. Use `\vert` instead.**
> 2. **Unit conversions:** Marks are frequently lost for not converting knots → m/s. Always show this step explicitly.
> 3. **Apparent wind angle convention:** State clearly whether $\theta = 0°$ is head wind or following wind. Different textbooks use different conventions; KTU examiners follow the convention where $\theta = 0°$ corresponds to a head wind.
> 4. **Coefficient interpretation:** $C_{AA}$ is *lumped* (form + friction). Some students incorrectly treat it as form drag only. Both contribute.
> 5. **Speed-band awareness:** For bulbous bow questions, marks are awarded for *commenting* on the off-design behaviour. Writing only the design calculation will lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, PCNST402**

### Bulbous Bows
- A bulbous bow is a **wave-resistance-reducing appendage** below the WL, **not** a frictional device.
- It works by **destructive wave interference** between its own wave system and the hull's bow wave.
- **Critical design equation:** $\lambda = 2\pi L_{pp}F_n^2$ — controls bulb length and longitudinal position.
- **Effective only** at a narrow Froude-number band (typically $F_n = 0.20$–$0.45$).
- **Off-design behaviour:** Bulb becomes ineffective at low $F_n$ and may **increase** $R_W$ at very high $F_n$.
- **Typical parameters:** $b/B = 0.05$–$0.08$; $l/L_{pp} = 0.02$–$0.05$.
- **Benefit:** 10–15 % reduction in $R_W$ for displacement-type ships.
- **Drawback:** Slight increase in frictional resistance (1–2 %) due to added wetted surface.

### Air and Wind Resistance
- $R_{AA}$ is a **small but non-negligible** component of total resistance (2–4 % typically; up to 8–10 % for ULCVs).
- **ITTC standard formula:** $R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{AA} A_T V_A^2$.
- **Apparent wind** $V_A$ is a vector sum — depends on **true wind speed, ship speed, and angle**.
- **Head wind** is the worst case (forces add directly).
- **Air density** $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ (ITTC standard at 15 °C).
- **$C_{AA}$** is a lumped coefficient combining form and frictional drag — depends on geometry, roughness, and wind angle.
- **Design implications:** Cruise liners, ULCVs, and car carriers are highly sensitive to superstructure design; streamlined bows and integrated funnels reduce $C_{AA}$.

### Frequently Tested Items in KTU
1. Bulb design Froude number calculation.
2. Design wavelength from $F_n$.
3. Apparent wind computation in all three cases (head, beam, following).
4. ITTC formula with full term definitions.
5. Off-design commentary for bulbous bows.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance of Appendages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistance of Appendages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Appendage Resistance ($R_{APP}$):** The component of the total ship resistance that arises from the hydrodynamic and aerodynamic drag of all fittings, protuberances, and auxiliary components attached to the bare hull, which lie outside the smooth, clean hull envelope.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the total calm-water ship resistance $R_T$ is decomposed as:

$$R_T = R_F + R_{APP} + R_A + R_W + R_{APP,A} + C_A$$

Where:
- $R_F$ = frictional resistance of the bare hull
- $R_{APP}$ = frictional + form (viscous pressure) resistance of appendages
- $R_A$ = aerodynamic resistance of superstructures
- $R_W$ = wave-making and wave-breaking resistance
- $R_{APP,A}$ = aerodynamic resistance of appendages exposed to air
- $C_A$ = correlation allowance (model-ship correlation)

Common **appendages** encountered in ship resistance problems include:
- **Ruders** (single, twin, spade, semi-spade)
- **Bilge keels** (passive roll-damping fins)
- **Shaft brackets / Struts** (supporting propeller shaft)
- **Propeller hub** (non-rotating part in towing condition)
- **Sonar domes** (underwater acoustic sensors)
- **Stabilizer fins** (active anti-roll appendages)
- **Hull openings / Sea chests** (intakes/discharges)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The 14-mark module questions frequently require you to *quantify* appendage resistance, justify typical $(1+k)$ values, and justify why bilge keels carry the *highest* form factor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a swimmer gliding through water with a smooth, streamlined body. Now strap a pair of swim fins, a snorkel, and a waterproof watch to the swimmer. The fins, snorkel, and watch don't help the swimmer go faster — they only create **extra drag**. That extra drag is exactly the concept of **appendage resistance** in naval architecture.

Specifically:
- The swimmer's body ≈ the bare hull (with $R_F$ and $R_W$).
- Swim fins ≈ rudders and bilge keels (they *generate forces* but also *resist motion*).
- Waterproof watch ≈ shaft brackets and sonar domes (purely parasitic drag, no useful force).

A common **misconception** is that appendage resistance is small. In reality, for a fully-fitted modern merchant ship, the total appendage drag can amount to **5%–15%** of the bare-hull frictional resistance — an enormous value when one considers the cumulative fuel cost over a 25-year service life.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard constants and reference metrics used throughout this module:
>
> - **Kinematic viscosity of seawater:** $\nu_{sw} = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (at 15°C, salinity 35 ppt)
> - **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water:** $\nu_{fw} = 1.1393 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (at 15°C)
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Density of air at 15°C:** $\rho_{air} = 1.226\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Reference length for appendage Reynolds number:** mean chord length $c_m$ in metres (not $L_{PP}$)
> - **Standard KTU reference velocity:** $V$ in knots or $\text{m/s}$
> - **Form-factor symbol:** $(1+k)$ — dimensionless

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decomposition Logic of Appendage Resistance

Each appendage, being a fully wetted (or partially wetted) body with finite thickness and flow separation, develops its own **boundary layer** and **viscous pressure** contribution. The KTU / ITTC 1978 approach handles this by treating each appendage as a *mini-hull* with its own form factor.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Identify the appendage** and isolate its wetted surface area $S_{APP}$ (in m²).
2. **Compute the appendage Reynolds number** using the *mean chord length* $c_m$ of the appendage (NOT the ship length):
   $$Re_{APP} = \frac{V \cdot c_m}{\nu}$$
3. **Obtain the frictional coefficient** $C_{F,APP}$ from the ITTC 1957 correlation line:
   $$C_{F,APP} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_{APP} - 2)^2}$$
4. **Select the appropriate form factor** $(1+k)_{APP}$ from standard tables (Section 2.3 below).
5. **Compute the viscous resistance** of the appendage:
   $$R_{V,APP} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S_{APP} \cdot (1+k)_{APP} \cdot C_{F,APP}$$
6. **Apply roughness allowance** $\Delta C_F$ if the surface is not hydraulically smooth.
7. **Add to bare-hull resistance** to obtain the total ship resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind using $c_m$ and not $L_{PP}$:** Boundary-layer theory shows that the Reynolds number controls the frictional drag of a *flat plate of equivalent chord*. Using $L_{PP}$ would dramatically over-estimate friction for a small appendage, because the boundary layer never has the chance to grow to ship-scale thickness.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you will need for appendage-resistance numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. **Memorise this table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | ITTC-1957 frictional coefficient (ship) | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless | $Re = V L_{PP}/\nu$ |
| 2 | ITTC-1957 frictional coefficient (appendage) | $C_{F,APP} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_{APP} - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless | $Re_{APP} = V c_m/\nu$ |
| 3 | Appendage Reynolds number | $Re_{APP} = \dfrac{V \, c_m}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Use **mean chord** |
| 4 | Appendage viscous resistance | $R_{V,APP} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S_{APP} (1+k)_{APP} C_{F,APP}$ | **N (newtons)** | $(1+k)$ from table |
| 5 | Air (aerodynamic) resistance | $R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} V_A^2 S_{AA} C_{D,AA}$ | **N** | $V_A$ = wind speed |
| 6 | Roughness allowance | $\Delta C_F = 0.044 \left[ \dfrac{k_s}{L} \right]^{1/3} - 10^{-3.4 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot Re \cdot 10^{-4} + 0.539}$ | dimensionless | For $Re > 10^9$ |
| 7 | Correlation allowance (ITTC-1978) | $C_A = 0.0004$ | dimensionless | Bare-hull form |
| 8 | ITTC-2017 frictional coefficient | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(log_{10} Re - 2)^2 + 2 - 0.0056 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless | Newer exam favourite |
| 9 | Appendage resistance increment (proportion) | $\dfrac{R_{APP}}{R_{F,hull}} = \dfrac{S_{APP} (1+k)_{APP} C_{F,APP}}{S_{hull} \, C_{F,hull}}$ | dimensionless | Common Part-A question |
| 10 | Speed in knots to m/s | $V [\text{m/s}] = 0.5144 \cdot V [\text{kts}]$ | m/s | Required in all numerics |

> **Critical LaTeX note for students:** Whenever you see the **absolute-value** symbol $ \vert x \vert $ in a problem, the **form factor** is always a *positive* number $\vert (1+k) \vert$ (i.e. just $(1+k)$ itself). Don't get confused with the modulus notation in your KTU answer booklets.

## 2.3 Standard Form-Factor Values $(1+k)_{APP}$ for Common Appendages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most-tested table in the entire Module 1.** The KTU paper-setter expects you to reproduce these values *without* referring to any source. Practise writing them from memory.

| Appendage Type | Typical $(1+k)$ Range | Recommended Design Value |
|----------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|
| Rudder (single, behind propeller) | 1.3 – 1.7 | **1.5** |
| Rudder (twin, semi-spade) | 1.5 – 2.0 | **1.8** |
| Bilge keel (one side) | 1.8 – 2.5 | **2.0** |
| Bilge keel (both sides) | – | **2.0** (per keel) |
| Shaft bracket | 1.5 – 2.5 | **1.8** |
| Propeller hub (non-rotating) | 1.2 – 1.5 | **1.3** |
| Sonar dome | 1.2 – 1.5 | **1.3** |
| Stabilizer fin (retracted) | 1.5 – 2.0 | **1.8** |
| Stabilizer fin (deployed) | 2.0 – 3.0 | **2.5** |
| Hull bossing / Stern tube | 1.2 – 1.4 | **1.3** |

> **Why bilge keels have the highest form factor:** A bilge keel is a long, thin plate oriented *perpendicular* to the flow, mounted on the curved bilge of the hull. This produces massive flow separation, large wake, and a thick turbulent boundary layer — exactly the conditions that maximise form drag.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Appendage resistance directly impacts:

1. **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index) compliance** — every kW of appendage drag must be justified under IMO MARPOL Annex VI.
2. **Fuel-oil consumption (FOC) bills** — appendages cost operators approximately **USD 50,000–150,000 per year per ship** in wasted fuel.
3. **Shaft-line design** — the propeller must be sized to overcome the appendage drag in addition to hull drag.
4. **Operational decisions** — e.g. retracting stabiliser fins at low speed, or fairing shaft brackets on racing yachts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How the Appendage Resistance Formula Emerges from Boundary-Layer Theory

The classic KTU / ITTC formulation of appendage viscous resistance is **not** a definition but a *derivation*. Let us reconstruct it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Begin with the standard viscous-drag definition for any body

For a fully wetted body moving at velocity $V$ through a fluid of density $\rho$, the viscous resistance is:

$$R_V = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S \, C_V$$

where $C_V$ is the *viscous drag coefficient* (combining friction and form pressure).

### Step 2 — Express $C_V$ as the product of friction and form factor

By the **Froude hypothesis** of 1868 (later refined by Prohaska and Hughes), the viscous coefficient can be factored into a *frictional* part (depending on Reynolds number) and a *form* part (depending on body geometry):

$$C_V = (1+k) \cdot C_F$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$R_V = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S \, (1+k) \, C_F$$

This is the foundational equation. Everything that follows is refinement.

### Step 3 — Specialise to an appendage

The appendage is a *separate* body with its own Reynolds number built on its mean chord $c_m$:

$$Re_{APP} = \frac{V \cdot c_m}{\nu}$$

Hence the appendage frictional coefficient is:

$$C_{F,APP} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_{APP} - 2)^2}$$

### Step 4 — Combine the expressions

The viscous resistance of *one* appendage becomes:

$$R_{V,APP} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S_{APP} \, (1+k)_{APP} \, C_{F,APP}$$

### Step 5 — Sum over all appendages

For a ship with $N$ appendages, the total appendage viscous resistance is:

$$R_{V,APP}^{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S_{APP,i} \, (1+k)_{APP,i} \, C_{F,APP,i}$$

In practice, KTU numericals group appendages into 2–3 categories (e.g. "rudder + bilge keels + shaft bracket") and assume a representative $(1+k)$ per category.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Model Examination Style)

> **Problem (KTU Module-1 typical):** A bulk carrier of $L_{PP} = 180$ m travels at $V = 15$ knots in seawater ($\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$). The appendage data is:
>
> - Twin semi-spade rudders, each with mean chord $c_m = 2.4$ m and wetted area $S = 14\ \text{m}^2$, $(1+k) = 1.8$.
> - Two bilge keels, each with $c_m = 22$ m and $S = 18\ \text{m}^2$, $(1+k) = 2.0$.
>
> Compute the total appendage viscous resistance.

### Solution — Full Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert speed**

$$V = 15\ \text{kts} \times 0.5144 = 7.716\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute appendage Reynolds numbers**

For the rudders:

$$Re_{rudder} = \frac{7.716 \times 2.4}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.559 \times 10^{7}$$

For the bilge keels:

$$Re_{bilge} = \frac{7.716 \times 22}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.429 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3: Compute ITTC-1957 frictional coefficients**

For the rudder:

$$C_{F, rudder} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.559 \times 10^{7}) - 2)^2}$$

$$\log_{10}(1.559 \times 10^{7}) = 7.1929$$

$$C_{F, rudder} = \frac{0.075}{(7.1929 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{26.984} = 2.780 \times 10^{-3}$$

For the bilge keel:

$$\log_{10}(1.429 \times 10^{8}) = 8.1550$$

$$C_{F, bilge} = \frac{0.075}{(8.1550 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{37.861} = 1.981 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Compute viscous resistance of each rudder**

$$R_{V, rudder} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 7.716^2 \times 14 \times 1.8 \times 2.780 \times 10^{-3}$$

Working through the intermediate product:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 7.716^2 = 30{,}514\ \text{N/m}^2 \text{ per unit of } (C_F S (1+k))$$

$$R_{V, rudder} = 30{,}514 \times 14 \times 1.8 \times 2.780 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_{V, rudder} = 30{,}514 \times 0.0700 = 2{,}136\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5: Viscous resistance of one bilge keel**

$$R_{V, bilge} = 30{,}514 \times 18 \times 2.0 \times 1.981 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_{V, bilge} = 30{,}514 \times 0.0713 = 2{,}176\ \text{N}$$

**Step 6: Total appendage viscous resistance (2 rudders + 2 bilge keels)**

$$R_{V,APP}^{total} = 2 \times 2{,}136 + 2 \times 2{,}176$$

$$R_{V,APP}^{total} = 4{,}272 + 4{,}352 = 8{,}624\ \text{N} \approx 8.62\ \text{kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check for sanity:** At 15 knots, the bare-hull frictional resistance of a 180 m bulk carrier is roughly **600–800 kN**. Our calculated appendage resistance of 8.6 kN is approximately **1.0–1.4 %** of the bare-hull frictional resistance — well within the typical 5–15 % band once *all* appendages (shaft brackets, hub, sonar dome) are included. The order-of-magnitude is correct.

## 3.3 Air Resistance Derivation

For appendages exposed to air (mast, funnel, bridge wing, radar mast), the resistance is computed using air density:

$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_{air} \, V_A^2 \, S_{AA} \, C_{D,AA}$$

Where $V_A$ is the **relative wind speed** (ship speed + true wind component) and $C_{D,AA} \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ for typical superstructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The default KTU numerical assumes $V_A = V$ (i.e. no wind). If wind data is given, you must use the vector sum: $V_A^2 = V_{ship}^2 + V_{wind}^2$ for **head-wind** (worst case) and a cosine law otherwise. The examiner's mark scheme **always** tests this if wind is given.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ITTC-1978 vs ITTC-2017 for Appendages

| Aspect | ITTC-1978 | ITTC-2017 |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Friction line | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log Re - 2)^2$ | $C_F = 0.075 / [(\log Re - 2)^2 + 2 - 0.0056/(\log Re - 2)^2]$ |
| Correlation allowance | $C_A = 0.0004$ | $C_A = 0.001$ |
| Form factor $(1+k)$ | Standard tables | Same tables, but with CFD validation |
| Speed-effect | $F_n$-independent | Slight $F_n$-dependence acknowledged |
| Use in KTU 2024 paper | **Most common** (≈80 % of past papers) | Increasingly common in 2024 Scheme |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: How Appendage Resistance Enters the Total Resistance Budget

The following Mermaid block maps the **calculation pipeline** from appendage geometry all the way to total ship resistance. It is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow that every KTU student should be able to reproduce from memory during the viva / oral component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Appendage Geometry Data] --> B[Collect: c_m, S_APP, type]
    B --> C{Is appendage submerged?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use seawater density and viscosity]
    C -- No --> E[Use air density and C_D]
    D --> F[Compute Re_APP = V c_m / nu]
    F --> G[Compute C_F,APP via ITTC-1957]
    G --> H[Look up 1+k from standard table]
    H --> I[R_V,APP = 0.5 rho V^2 S_APP 1+k C_F,APP]
    E --> J[R_AA = 0.5 rho_air V_A^2 S_AA C_D,AA]
    I --> K[Sum over all N appendages]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Total Appendage Resistance R_APP,total]
    L --> M[Add Bare-Hull R_F and R_W]
    M --> N[Add Correlation Allowance C_A term]
    N --> O[Total Calm-Water Resistance R_T]
    O --> P[Effective Power P_E = R_T V]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Appendage Categories on a Typical Ship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WET["Fully Wetted Appendages (rho = 1025 kg/m3)"]
        W1[Rudder - 1+k = 1.5 to 1.8]
        W2[Bilge Keel - 1+k = 2.0]
        W3[Shaft Bracket - 1+k = 1.8]
        W4[Sonar Dome - 1+k = 1.3]
        W5[Stabiliser Fin deployed - 1+k = 2.5]
    end
    subgraph AIR["Aerodynamic Appendages (rho_air = 1.226 kg/m3)"]
        A1[Mast and Radar]
        A2[Funnel]
        A3[Bridge Wing]
    end
    WET --> SUM[Sum all R_V,APP]
    AIR --> SUM2[Sum all R_AA]
    SUM --> TOTAL[R_T total resistance]
    SUM2 --> TOTAL
```

## 4.3 Logical Decision Tree: When to Apply Which Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the appendage surface smooth?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Reynolds number above 10^9?}
    Q1 -- No --> QR[Apply roughness allowance Delta_C_F]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Does KTU paper specify ITTC 2017?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use ITTC 1957 friction line]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5[Use ITTC 2017 formula]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 --> OUT[Compute R_V,APP]
    Q5 --> OUT
    QR --> Q2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical hull sketch?** A Mermaid environment cannot render a side-profile photograph or a CAD drawing of a ship. The block-level architecture flows above convey *exactly* the same procedural knowledge that the KTU examiner tests, while staying within the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]`
**Define appendage resistance. Why is it treated separately from the bare-hull frictional resistance in the ITTC-1978 method?**

**Model Answer:**

Appendage resistance is the additional viscous and pressure resistance arising from fittings (rudders, bilge keels, shaft brackets, sonar domes, etc.) attached to the bare hull. It is treated separately because:

1. Appendages develop their own **independent boundary layers** built on their own characteristic length (mean chord $c_m$, not $L_{PP}$).
2. The form factor $(1+k)$ for an appendage is generally **larger** than for the bare hull (often 1.5–2.5) because of sharper leading edges, flow separation, and higher thickness ratios.
3. Separating the appendage drag allows the designer to **trade-off** appendage geometry (e.g. fairing a shaft bracket) without re-computing hull resistance.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three justifications.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**With a neat sketch / table, list typical $(1+k)$ form factors for: (i) a single spade rudder, (ii) a pair of bilge keels, (iii) a sonar dome.**

**Model Answer:**

| Appendage | $(1+k)$ Design Value | Reason for the Value |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Single spade rudder | **1.5** | Thick aerofoil-like section; some flow separation at high angles |
| Pair of bilge keels | **2.0** (per keel) | Plate perpendicular to flow on bilge; maximum separation |
| Sonar dome | **1.3** | Streamlined ellipsoidal cap; minimal separation |

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark per correct value, 1 mark for reasoning on any one.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

> The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a Module-wise **internal choice**. Below are the **two alternative questions**, each carrying 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Full Quantification of Appendage Resistance

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Set A]`

**(a)** [7 marks] A 25,000 DWT chemical tanker of $L_{PP} = 160$ m, wetted hull area $S = 5800\ \text{m}^2$, operates at $V = 14$ knots in seawater. The appendages consist of:
- One spade rudder: $c_m = 2.2$ m, $S_{APP} = 12\ \text{m}^2$, $(1+k) = 1.5$
- Two bilge keels: each $c_m = 18$ m, $S_{APP} = 15\ \text{m}^2$, $(1+k) = 2.0$
- Shaft bracket: $c_m = 1.8$ m, $S_{APP} = 6\ \text{m}^2$, $(1+k) = 1.8$

Compute the total appendage viscous resistance and express it as a percentage of the bare-hull frictional resistance (use ITTC-1957, $C_A = 0.0004$).

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss, with justification, **two** design modifications that the naval architect could implement to reduce the appendage resistance by at least 20 %, and explain the trade-offs involved.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Speed conversion**

$$V = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number for the bare hull**

$$Re_{hull} = \frac{7.2016 \times 160}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.69 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — Bare-hull frictional coefficient**

$$\log_{10}(9.69 \times 10^8) = 8.9863$$

$$C_{F,hull} = \frac{0.075}{(8.9863 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{48.804} = 1.537 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Bare-hull frictional resistance**

$$R_{F,hull} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 7.2016^2 \times 5800 \times 1.537 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 7.2016^2 = 26{,}584\ \text{N/m}^2 \text{ per unit of } C_F S$$

$$R_{F,hull} = 26{,}584 \times 5800 \times 1.537 \times 10^{-3} = 236{,}945\ \text{N} \approx 237\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Appendage Reynolds numbers**

- Rudder: $Re = (7.2016 \times 2.2) / (1.1883 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.333 \times 10^{7}$
- Bilge keel: $Re = (7.2016 \times 18) / (1.1883 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.091 \times 10^{8}$
- Shaft bracket: $Re = (7.2016 \times 1.8) / (1.1883 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.091 \times 10^{7}$

**Step 6 — Appendage frictional coefficients**

- Rudder: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10}(1.333 \times 10^7) - 2)^2 = 0.075 / (5.1249 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 9.764 = 7.682 \times 10^{-3}$

  Wait, recompute: $\log_{10}(1.333 \times 10^7) = 7.1249$, so $(\log Re - 2) = 5.1249$, $(5.1249)^2 = 26.265$, $C_F = 0.075/26.265 = 2.856 \times 10^{-3}$.

- Bilge keel: $\log_{10}(1.091 \times 10^8) = 8.0378$, $(\log Re - 2) = 6.0378$, square $= 36.456$, $C_F = 0.075/36.456 = 2.057 \times 10^{-3}$.

- Shaft bracket: identical $Re$ to rudder, $C_F = 2.856 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 7 — Resistance of each appendage**

- Rudder: $R = 26{,}584 \times 12 \times 1.5 \times 2.856 \times 10^{-3} = 26{,}584 \times 0.0514 = 1{,}367\ \text{N}$
- Bilge keel (each): $R = 26{,}584 \times 15 \times 2.0 \times 2.057 \times 10^{-3} = 26{,}584 \times 0.0617 = 1{,}641\ \text{N}$
- Shaft bracket: $R = 26{,}584 \times 6 \times 1.8 \times 2.856 \times 10^{-3} = 26{,}584 \times 0.0308 = 820\ \text{N}$

**Step 8 — Total appendage resistance**

$$R_{APP} = 1{,}367 + 2 \times 1{,}641 + 820 = 5{,}469\ \text{N} \approx 5.47\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 9 — Percentage of bare-hull friction**

$$\frac{R_{APP}}{R_{F,hull}} \times 100\% = \frac{5{,}469}{236{,}945} \times 100\% \approx 2.31\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The calculated 2.31 % is at the *lower* end of the typical 5–15 % band because we have only accounted for the rudder, bilge keels, and shaft bracket. Adding the propeller hub, sonar dome, sea chests, and stabiliser fins would lift the proportion to roughly 6–8 %, well within the engineering norm.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Two valid design modifications are:

1. **Fair the shaft bracket into an elliptical / streamlined cross-section** (e.g. change from a flat plate to a NACA-shaped strut). This reduces $(1+k)$ from 1.8 to roughly 1.3, a ~28 % reduction in shaft-bracket drag, contributing ~0.3 kN savings. *Trade-off:* increased fabrication cost and slightly weaker structural support for the shaft.

2. **Re-design the bilge keels as end-bulb or "wing" sections** instead of flat plates. Modern bulb-ended bilge keels (used in-service on some KVLCC2 designs) reduce $(1+k)$ to about 1.4 — a 30 % per-keel reduction, contributing ~1 kN total. *Trade-off:* the bulb adds displacement and weight, and may interfere with port-entry in shallow water if too long.

Any equivalent modification (e.g. retractable stabiliser fins, rudder bulbs, propeller-hub cap) is acceptable. *(Each modification: 3 marks for the change, 1 mark for the quantitative saving, 3 marks for the trade-off discussion — total 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Form-Factor Theory and Air Resistance

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Define the **form factor $(1+k)$** and explain, with sketches, why bilge keels have a *much higher* form factor than a sonar dome. Quote typical numerical values from the KTU / ITTC tables.

**(b)** [7 marks] The bridge wing and foremast of a ship have a combined transverse projected area above water of $35\ \text{m}^2$. The ship sails at 18 knots into a 20-knot true head wind. Assuming $C_{D,AA} = 0.8$ and air density $\rho_{air} = 1.226\ \text{kg/m}^3$, compute the aerodynamic resistance.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Form factor definition:**

> The form factor $(1+k)$ is the ratio of the *total* viscous resistance coefficient of a body to its *frictional* resistance coefficient, i.e.
> $$1+k = \frac{C_V}{C_F} \ge 1$$
> It quantifies the additional pressure drag arising from the body's shape, thickness, and flow-separation tendency.

**Why bilge keels have higher $(1+k)$ than sonar domes:**

- A **bilge keel** is a long flat plate mounted on the curved bilge, oriented nearly perpendicular to the flow. The flow impinges on the leading edge, separates, and creates a large low-pressure wake behind. Geometry: thickness ratio $t/c$ is small but the *angle of attack* on the curved bilge is high. Typical $(1+k) = 2.0$.

- A **sonar dome** is a streamlined ellipsoidal cap with a rounded leading edge, designed to minimise separation. The flow remains attached over most of the surface. Typical $(1+k) = 1.3$.

Therefore, bilge keels experience roughly 50 % *more* form drag per unit frictional area than sonar domes.

*(Marks: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for physical reasoning, 1 mark for the typical numerical values, 1 mark for the quantitative comparison, 1 mark for sketch description.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to m/s**

$$V_{ship} = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.259\ \text{m/s}$$

$$V_{wind} = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute relative head-wind speed**

For a head-wind, both vectors oppose the ship, so the relative speed is the *sum*:

$$V_A = 9.259 + 10.288 = 19.547\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute aerodynamic resistance**

$$R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.226 \times 19.547^2 \times 35 \times 0.8$$

Working through the product:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.226 = 0.613$$

$$19.547^2 = 382.06$$

$$0.613 \times 382.06 = 234.20\ \text{N per unit of } S \cdot C_D$$

$$R_{AA} = 234.20 \times 35 \times 0.8 = 6{,}557.7\ \text{N} \approx 6.56\ \text{kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At 18 knots the bare-hull frictional resistance is ~500 kN, so 6.56 kN of air drag is ~1.3 % of hull friction. This is *consistent* with the typical 1–2 % aerodynamic contribution for a modern merchant ship.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **The five most common ways KTU students lose marks on appendage-resistance problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong characteristic length.** Using $L_{PP}$ instead of $c_m$ when computing $Re_{APP}$. This alone wipes out 2–3 marks. **Always re-read the question** for "mean chord" or "appendage chord length."
> 2. **Forgetting the form factor.** Writing $R_{APP} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_F$ instead of the full $(1+k) C_F$. This costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing fluid properties.** Using seawater density for an aerodynamic appendage (mast, funnel). Cost: 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the head-wind sum.** When a wind speed is given, students often use $V_A = V_{ship}$ alone, missing the wind vector. Cost: 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Wrong speed units.** Writing $V = 15$ (knots) directly into $V$ (m/s) formulas. Always convert. Cost: 1 mark per sub-part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Appendage resistance** = viscous (friction + form) drag of fittings outside the bare hull.
- [ ] Total appendage resistance contributes **5–15 %** of bare-hull frictional resistance for a fully-fitted ship.
- [ ] **Use mean chord $c_m$** — never $L_{PP}$ — for the appendage Reynolds number.
- [ ] The ITTC-1957 friction line: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$.
- [ ] The ITTC-2017 friction line: $C_F = 0.075 / [(\log Re - 2)^2 + 2 - 0.0056/(\log Re - 2)^2]$.
- [ ] The fundamental equation: $R_{V,APP} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S_{APP} (1+k)_{APP} C_{F,APP}$.
- [ ] **Bilge keels have the highest** $(1+k) \approx 2.0$.
- [ ] **Sonar domes have among the lowest** $(1+k) \approx 1.3$.
- [ ] **Single spade rudder** $(1+k) \approx 1.5$; **twin semi-spade rudders** $(1+k) \approx 1.8$.
- [ ] **Shaft brackets** $(1+k) \approx 1.8$.
- [ ] **Propeller hub (towing)** $(1+k) \approx 1.3$.
- [ ] Air resistance uses $\rho_{air} = 1.226\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and a drag coefficient $C_D \approx 0.5$–$1.0$.
- [ ] Head-wind speed: $V_A = V_{ship} + V_{wind}$ (worst case, opposing directions).
- [ ] 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s** — always convert.
- [ ] ITTC-1978 correlation allowance: $C_A = 0.0004$ (older, default in KTU papers).
- [ ] ITTC-2017 correlation allowance: $C_A = 0.001$ (newer 2024 Scheme).
- [ ] The **mean chord** $c_m$ of an appendage is its average fore-aft dimension perpendicular to flow.
- [ ] Reynolds number threshold for ITTC 1978 friction-line validity: $10^6 \le Re \le 10^9$.
- [ ] Appendage drag *adds* to hull drag — it never *replaces* it in the ITTC-1978 method.
- [ ] Always check: is the appendage **fully wetted** (use seawater $\rho$) or **in air** (use $\rho_{air}$)?
- [ ] Appendage resistance scales with $V^2$ — doubling speed quadruples the drag.
- [ ] Units check: $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $V$ in $\text{m/s}$, $S$ in $\text{m}^2$ → $R$ in **newtons**.
- [ ] Practical design levers: streamlined fairings, retractable fins, end-bulb bilge keels, propeller-hub caps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensional Analysis, Laws of Comparison - Geometrical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dimensional Analysis, Laws of Comparison - Geometrical # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Dimensional Analysis — Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Dimensional Analysis (DA)** is a mathematical technique used in ship hydrodynamics to study physical phenomena (such as resistance, wave-making, or viscous flow) by expressing the relationships between physical quantities in terms of their fundamental dimensions — **Mass [M]**, **Length [L]**, and **Time [T]**. In ship resistance, it is the gateway to the **Laws of Comparison** that allow engineers to predict full-scale ship performance from small-scale model experiments. For ship resistance, the governing variables are typically: resistance force ($R$), fluid density ($\rho$), kinematic viscosity ($\nu$), gravitational acceleration ($g$), ship speed ($V$), length ($L$), and free-surface properties (e.g., surface tension, $\sigma$). The **functional relationship** is written as: $$f(R, \rho, \nu, g, V, L, \sigma) = 0$$ By using the **Buckingham Pi Theorem**, this reduces to a set of **dimensionless groups** (Pi terms), the most famous of which is the **Reynolds Number ($R_n$)**, **Froude Number ($F_n$)**, and **Weber Number ($W_e$)**. ## 1.2 Laws of Comparison — Definitions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Laws of Comparison** are the engineering rules derived from dimensional analysis that define the conditions under which a **model** of a ship faithfully represents its **prototype** (full-scale ship). They are classified into three hierarchical levels: > > 1. **Geometrical Similarity** — Shape similarity (form) > 2. **Kinematical Similarity** — Motion similarity (velocity ratios) > 3. **Dynamical Similarity** — Force similarity (force ratios) ## 1.3 Geometrical Similarity — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Geometrical Similarity** exists between a model and its prototype when **all corresponding linear dimensions are in a constant ratio** and **all corresponding angles are identical**. In ship model testing, the model is a *scaled, geometrically faithful copy* of the full-scale ship. Mathematically, if a model and prototype share a *common origin* and *common orientation*, then every corresponding point $(x, y, z)$ on the prototype maps to $(\lambda x, \lambda y, \lambda z)$ on the model, where $\lambda$ is the **Scale Ratio** (also called *Linear Scale Factor* or *Scale Ratio of Length*). $$\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_p}$$ Where subscripts $m$ = model and $p$ = prototype. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Toy Ship in the Towing Tank" Imagine you are a naval architect in the 19th century, and you cannot afford to build a 100-meter long ship just to test if its hull shape is efficient. So, you build a **1-meter long wooden replica** of the ship and tow it through a long narrow water channel called a **towing tank**. For your little wooden replica to *truly* tell you how the real ship will behave, it must look **exactly like the real ship** in shape — just smaller. Every hull curvature, every propeller blade angle, every bulbous bow must be a perfect scaled-down copy. This is **Geometrical Similarity**. If your toy ship had a longer bow or wider stern than the real ship, your resistance test results would be useless. Geometric similarity is the **"shape contract"** that the model signs with the prototype before the experiment begins. ## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Geometrical Similarity The following ship form coefficients are **dimensionless** and therefore must remain **identical** in model and prototype for geometrical similarity: - **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** — Volume displacement indicator - **Midship Coefficient ($C_M$)** — Cross-section fullness - **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)** — Longitudinal distribution of volume - **Waterplane Coefficient ($C_W$)** — Waterplane area fullness - **Longitudinal Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{LP}$)** — Afterbody distribution > [!IMPORTANT] > Since these coefficients are ratios of like dimensions, they are **invariant** under scaling. Their equality between model and prototype is a *necessary and sufficient* signature of perfect geometrical similarity. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Geometric Similarity For a ship and its model to be geometrically similar, three conditions must hold **simultaneously**: 1. **Constant Linear Scale Ratio ($\lambda$)** — Every length scales by the same factor. 2. **Identical Form Coefficients** — $C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P$, $C_W$ are equal. 3. **Identical Angular Geometry** — Flare angles, deadrise angles, buttock angles, stem profile angles are preserved. ## 2.2 Scale-Dependent Quantities vs. Scale-Invariant Quantities | Category | Quantities | Scaling Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Linear Dimensions** | Length $L$, Beam $B$, Draft $T$, Depth $D$ | Scale as $\lambda$ | | **Area Quantities** | Wetted Surface Area (WSA), Waterplane Area ($A_W$), Midship Area ($A_M$) | Scale as $\lambda^2$ | | **Volume Quantities** | Displacement Volume ($\nabla$), Volume of Hull, Wetted Volume | Scale as $\lambda^3$ | | **Form Coefficients** | $C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P$, $C_W$, $C_{LP}$ | **Invariant (equal to 1:1)** | > [!NOTE] > **Why areas scale as $\lambda^2$**: Area has dimension $[L^2]$. If length is multiplied by $\lambda$, area is multiplied by $\lambda \times \lambda = \lambda^2$. > > **Why volumes scale as $\lambda^3$**: Volume has dimension $[L^3]$. Under linear scaling, volume is multiplied by $\lambda \times \lambda \times \lambda = \lambda^3$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following table — these are the formulas examiners expect you to reproduce without hesitation.** | # | Quantity | Symbol | Model-to-Prototype Ratio | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear Scale Ratio | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_m}{L_p}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Length Ratio | $L$ | $\dfrac{L_m}{L_p} = \lambda$ | m or m | | 3 | Beam Ratio | $B$ | $\dfrac{B_m}{B_p} = \lambda$ | m | | 4 | Draft Ratio | $T$ | $\dfrac{T_m}{T_p} = \lambda$ | m | | 5 | Wetted Surface Area Ratio | $\dfrac{S_m}{S_p}$ | $\lambda^2$ | m² | | 6 | Waterplane Area Ratio | $\dfrac{A_{W,m}}{A_{W,p}}$ | $\lambda^2$ | m² | | 7 | Midship Section Area Ratio | $\dfrac{A_{M,m}}{A_{M,p}}$ | $\lambda^2$ | m² | | 8 | Displacement Volume Ratio | $\dfrac{\nabla_m}{\nabla_p}$ | $\lambda^3$ | m³ | | 9 | Displacement Mass (Weight) Ratio | $\dfrac{\Delta_m}{\Delta_p}$ | $\lambda^3 \cdot \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_p}$ | tonnes | | 10 | Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | $C_{B,m} = C_{B,p}$ | dimensionless | | 11 | Midship Coefficient | $C_M$ | $C_{M,m} = C_{M,p}$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P$ | $C_{P,m} = C_{P,p}$ | dimensionless | | 13 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_W$ | $C_{W,m} = C_{W,p}$ | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Density Caveat**: In standard fresh/salt water model tests, the fluid (water) is the *same* on both sides, so $\rho_m = \rho_p$ and the mass ratio simplifies to $\lambda^3$. However, if you compare a **wax model tested in a salt-water tank** with a full-scale ship in the ocean, the density ratio **must** be included. ## 2.4 The Displacement Relationship The displacement volume of a ship is: $$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$ Scaling this from prototype to model: $$\frac{\nabla_m}{\nabla_p} = \frac{C_{B,m} \cdot L_m \cdot B_m \cdot T_m}{C_{B,p} \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p}$$ Since $C_{B,m} = C_{B,p}$ (geometric similarity) and each linear dimension scales by $\lambda$: $$\frac{\nabla_m}{\nabla_p} = \lambda \cdot \lambda \cdot \lambda = \lambda^3$$ ## 2.5 The Wetted Surface Area Relationship The wetted surface area at the design waterline scales as: $$\frac{S_m}{S_p} = \lambda^2$$ This relationship is **critical** because the **frictional resistance component** in ship resistance (computed via the ITTC 1957 or 1978 friction lines) is directly proportional to wetted surface area. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Geometrical similarity is the **foundation** of: - **Towing Tank Testing** at MARIN (Netherlands), HSVA (Germany), KRISO (Korea), and Indian test facilities. - **CFD Validation** — Numerical simulations of ships are validated against model test data; geometric similarity ensures apples-to-apples comparison. - **Contract Speed Trials** — Sea trials are corrected back to "trial conditions" using scaling laws. - **Design of Hull Forms** — Form coefficients preserved during parametric studies. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Area Scales as the Square of the Linear Scale Ratio **Statement**: If a model and prototype are geometrically similar with linear scale ratio $\lambda = L_m / L_p$, then any corresponding area scales as $\lambda^2$. **Proof**: Consider a rectangular panel on the hull surface with prototype dimensions $L_p \times B_p$. The prototype area is: $$A_p = L_p \times B_p$$ The corresponding panel on the model has dimensions: $$L_m = \lambda L_p \quad \text{and} \quad B_m = \lambda B_p$$ The model area is: $$A_m = L_m \times B_m = (\lambda L_p) \times (\lambda B_p)$$ $$\therefore A_m = \lambda^2 \cdot L_p \cdot B_p = \lambda^2 \cdot A_p$$ Therefore: $$\frac{A_m}{A_p} = \lambda^2 \quad \blacksquare$$ This result is general — it holds for **any** area, because area is a *bilinear* function of two linear dimensions. The same argument applies to the wetted surface area $S$, waterplane area $A_W$, and midship area $A_M$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume Scales as the Cube of the Linear Scale Ratio **Statement**: If a model and prototype are geometrically similar with linear scale ratio $\lambda$, then any corresponding volume scales as $\lambda^3$. **Proof**: Consider a rectangular parallelepiped (a basic volume element of the hull form) with prototype dimensions $L_p \times B_p \times T_p$. The prototype volume is: $$V_p = L_p \times B_p \times T_p$$ The corresponding volume on the model has dimensions: $$L_m = \lambda L_p, \quad B_m = \lambda B_p, \quad T_m = \lambda T_p$$ The model volume is: $$V_m = L_m \times B_m \times T_m = (\lambda L_p) \times (\lambda B_p) \times (\lambda T_p)$$ $$\therefore V_m = \lambda^3 \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p = \lambda^3 \cdot V_p$$ Therefore: $$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \lambda^3 \quad \blacksquare$$ This holds for **any** volume, including the displacement volume $\nabla$, the volume of the bare hull, and the volume of a propeller disc. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Form Coefficients are Scale-Invariant **Statement**: The block coefficient $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ is identical in model and prototype under geometric similarity. **Proof**: The block coefficient of the prototype is: $$C_{B,p} = \frac{\nabla_p}{L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p}$$ The block coefficient of the model is: $$C_{B,m} = \frac{\nabla_m}{L_m \cdot B_m \cdot T_m} = \frac{\lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p}{(\lambda L_p) \cdot (\lambda B_p) \cdot (\lambda T_p)}$$ $$C_{B,m} = \frac{\lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p}{\lambda^3 \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p} = \frac{\nabla_p}{L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p}$$ $$\therefore C_{B,m} = C_{B,p} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > The $\lambda^3$ in the numerator and the $\lambda^3$ in the denominator **cancel exactly**. This is why *all* form coefficients — being ratios of like-dimensioned quantities — are dimensionless and scale-invariant. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Displacement Mass (Weight) Relationship **Statement**: The displacement mass of the model is $\Delta_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \Delta_p \cdot (\rho_m / \rho_p)$ when fluid densities differ. **Proof**: The displacement mass is the product of displaced volume and fluid density: $$\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho$$ Applying the volume scaling result $\nabla_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p$: $$\Delta_m = \nabla_m \cdot \rho_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p \cdot \rho_m$$ Dividing both sides by the prototype displacement $\Delta_p = \nabla_p \cdot \rho_p$: $$\frac{\Delta_m}{\Delta_p} = \frac{\lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p \cdot \rho_m}{\nabla_p \cdot \rho_p} = \lambda^3 \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Special Case**: For the same water (fresh or salt), $\rho_m = \rho_p$, giving $\Delta_m / \Delta_p = \lambda^3$. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem**: A ship of length $L_p = 200$ m, beam $B_p = 30$ m, draft $T_p = 12$ m, with block coefficient $C_B = 0.65$, is to be modeled at a scale ratio of $\lambda = 1/40$. Find the model dimensions, model wetted surface area (assuming $S_p = 12{,}000$ m²), and model displacement mass (salt water, $\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³). **Solution**: **Step 1**: Model linear dimensions $$L_m = \lambda \cdot L_p = \frac{1}{40} \times 200 = 5.00 \text{ m}$$ $$B_m = \lambda \cdot B_p = \frac{1}{40} \times 30 = 0.75 \text{ m}$$ $$T_m = \lambda \cdot T_p = \frac{1}{40} \times 12 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2**: Model wetted surface area $$\frac{S_m}{S_p} = \lambda^2 = \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{1600}$$ $$S_m = \frac{12{,}000}{1600} = 7.50 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3**: Model displacement volume $$\nabla_p = C_B \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p = 0.65 \times 200 \times 30 \times 12 = 46{,}800 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\nabla_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p = \frac{1}{64{,}000} \times 46{,}800 = 0.73125 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 4**: Model displacement mass (same fluid) $$\Delta_m = \nabla_m \cdot \rho_p = 0.73125 \times 1025 = 749.53 \text{ kg}$$ **Verification**: $\Delta_p = 46{,}800 \times 1025 = 4.797 \times 10^7$ kg. Ratio check: $\Delta_m / \Delta_p = 749.53 / (4.797 \times 10^7) = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5} = (1/40)^3$. ✓ ## 3.6 Reverse Problem — Finding Scale Ratio from Displacement If we are given that the model displacement is 0.5% of prototype displacement, find $\lambda$: $$\frac{\nabla_m}{\nabla_p} = \lambda^3 = 0.005$$ $$\lambda = (0.005)^{1/3} = 0.1710$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Similarity (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Ship Resistance Modeling"] --> B["Laws of Comparison"] B --> C["1. Geometrical Similarity"] B --> D["2. Kinematical Similarity"] B --> E["3. Dynamical Similarity"] C --> C1["Shape Match: lambda = Lm divided by Lp"] C --> C2["Form Coefficients: C_B, C_M, C_P equal"] C --> C3["Angles Preserved: Flare, Deadrise, Buttock"] D --> D1["Velocity Ratios Constant"] D --> D2["Streamlines Geometrically Similar"] D --> D3["Froude Number Equality"] E --> E1["Force Ratios Constant"] E --> E2["Pressure Distribution Similar"] E --> E3["Reynolds and Froude Equality"] C1 --> F["Foundation Block"] F --> D F --> E ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Geometric Scaling of Ship Quantities ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Prototype Ship Lp, Bp, Tp"] -->|"Multiply by lambda"| B["Model Ship Lm, Bm, Tm"] A2["Prototype WSA Sp"] -->|"Multiply by lambda squared"| B2["Model WSA Sm"] A3["Prototype Volume Nabla_p"] -->|"Multiply by lambda cubed"| B3["Model Volume Nabla_m"] A4["Prototype C_B value"] -->|"Invariant ratio"| B4["Model C_B value equal"] B --> C["Towing Tank Test"] B2 --> C B3 --> C B4 --> C C --> D["Resistance Measurement Rm"] D --> E["Froude Scaling: Rp = Rm divided by lambda cubed"] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Dimensional Analysis Path ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Identify Physical Variables:
R, rho, nu, g, V, L, sigma"] --> Q["Select Repeating Variables:
rho, V, L"] Q --> R["Apply Buckingham Pi Theorem"] R --> S["Form Dimensionless Groups"] S --> T["Pi1 = R divided by rho V squared L squared"] S --> U["Pi2 = V divided by sqrt of gL = Froude Number"] S --> V["Pi3 = VL divided by nu = Reynolds Number"] S --> W["Pi4 = rho V squared L divided by sigma = Weber Number"] T --> X["Functional Relationship:
f of Pi1, Pi2, Pi3, Pi4 = 0"] U --> X V --> X W --> X X --> Y["Model-Prototype Similarity Conditions"] ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Section Comparison Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PROTO["PROTOTYPE - Full Scale Ship"] P1["Lpp = 200 m"] P2["B = 30 m"] P3["T = 12 m"] P4["Nabla_p = 46,800 m cubed"] P5["S_p = 12,000 m squared"] end subgraph MODEL["MODEL at scale 1 to 40"] M1["Lpp = 5.00 m"] M2["B = 0.75 m"] M3["T = 0.30 m"] M4["Nabla_m = 0.731 m cubed"] M5["S_m = 7.50 m squared"] end P1 -->|"divide by 40"| M1 P2 -->|"divide by 40"| M2 P3 -->|"divide by 40"| M3 P4 -->|"divide by 64,000"| M4 P5 -->|"divide by 1,600"| M5 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define geometrical similarity in the context of ship model testing. Why is it considered the foundation of the laws of comparison?** **Model Answer** (3 marks): Geometrical similarity between a ship and its model exists when all corresponding linear dimensions are in a constant ratio $\lambda = L_m / L_p$, and all corresponding angles (flare, deadrise, buttock, stem profile) are identical. As a consequence, all **form coefficients** — $C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P$, $C_W$ — are also identical, since these are dimensionless ratios. Geometrical similarity is the **foundation** of the laws of comparison because the higher levels — *kinematical* (motion) and *dynamical* (force) similarity — are built upon it. Without an identical shape, there is no meaningful way to define corresponding streamlines, velocities, or forces. In towing tank testing, geometrical similarity is achieved by machining the model hull to the exact scaled lines plan of the prototype. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write *only* "linear dimensions are in constant ratio" and forget to mention the preservation of **angles**. The angle condition is equally important — a stretched bow would alter wave patterns even if lengths matched. **(–1 mark deduction)** --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: If the linear scale ratio of a ship model is $\lambda = 1/50$, find the ratios of (a) wetted surface area, (b) displacement volume, and (c) block coefficient between model and prototype.** **Model Answer** (3 marks): **(a) Wetted Surface Area Ratio** (1 mark): $$\frac{S_m}{S_p} = \lambda^2 = \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2500}$$ **(b) Displacement Volume Ratio** (1 mark): $$\frac{\nabla_m}{\nabla_p} = \lambda^3 = \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)^3 = \frac{1}{125{,}000}$$ **(c) Block Coefficient Ratio** (1 mark): $$C_{B,m} = C_{B,p} \quad \text{(dimensionless, invariant under scaling)}$$ The ratio is **1:1** (i.e., $C_{B,m} / C_{B,p} = 1$). --- ## 📝 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: A full-scale tanker has the following particulars:** - Length between perpendiculars: $L_{pp} = 250$ m - Beam: $B = 40$ m - Draft: $T = 15$ m - Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.78$ - Wetted surface area: $S_p = 18{,}000$ m² - Cruising speed: $V_p = 14$ knots A model is built at a scale of $\lambda = 1/50$ and tested in a towing tank using salt water (same as prototype, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). **(a)** Calculate the model dimensions ($L_m$, $B_m$, $T_m$), model wetted surface area, and model displacement mass. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the model is towed at a speed corresponding to **Froude similarity**, calculate the model speed $V_m$ and discuss whether Reynolds similarity is achievable at this scale. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Part A** #### Part (a) — Model Dimensions and Displacement (7 marks) **Step 1: Model linear dimensions** (2 marks) $$L_m = \lambda \cdot L_{pp} = \frac{1}{50} \times 250 = 5.00 \text{ m}$$ $$B_m = \lambda \cdot B = \frac{1}{50} \times 40 = 0.80 \text{ m}$$ $$T_m = \lambda \cdot T = \frac{1}{50} \times 15 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Model wetted surface area** (2 marks) $$\frac{S_m}{S_p} = \lambda^2 = \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2500}$$ $$S_m = \frac{18{,}000}{2500} = 7.20 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3: Model displacement volume** (1 mark) $$\nabla_p = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.78 \times 250 \times 40 \times 15 = 117{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\nabla_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p = \frac{1}{125{,}000} \times 117{,}000 = 0.936 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 4: Model displacement mass** (2 marks) Since $\rho_m = \rho_p$: $$\Delta_m = \nabla_m \cdot \rho = 0.936 \times 1025 = 959.4 \text{ kg}$$ Equivalently: $$\Delta_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \Delta_p = \frac{1}{125{,}000} \times (117{,}000 \times 1025) = 959.4 \text{ kg} \quad ✓$$ --- #### Part (b) — Model Speed and Reynolds Similarity Discussion (7 marks) **Step 1: Froude Similarity Condition** (2 marks) Froude similarity requires: $$F_{n,m} = F_{n,p} \implies \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g L_{pp}}}$$ This simplifies to: $$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{50}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} = 0.1414$$ **Step 2: Convert prototype speed to m/s** (1 mark) $$V_p = 14 \text{ knots} = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.202 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3: Calculate model speed** (2 marks) $$V_m = \sqrt{\lambda} \cdot V_p = 0.1414 \times 7.202 = 1.018 \text{ m/s} \approx 1.02 \text{ m/s}$$ In knots: $V_m = 1.018 / 0.5144 \approx 1.98$ knots. **Step 4: Discussion of Reynolds Similarity** (2 marks) For Reynolds similarity: $$R_{n,m} = R_{n,p} \implies \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p L_{pp}}{\nu_p}$$ This would require $V_m / V_p = 1/\lambda = 50$, giving $V_m = 50 \times 7.202 = 360$ m/s — clearly **unrealistic** in a towing tank. In practice, the test water is the same ($\nu_m = \nu_p$), so: $$\frac{R_{n,m}}{R_{n,p}} = \frac{V_m L_m}{V_p L_{pp}} = \sqrt{\lambda} \cdot \lambda = \lambda^{3/2} = \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)^{3/2} = \frac{1}{353.55}$$ The model Reynolds number is only **~0.28%** of the prototype Reynolds number. Therefore, **Reynolds similarity cannot be achieved** at this scale. This is the classical **"Froude-Reynolds conflict"** in ship model testing, and is the reason why **form factors** and **ITTC 1978 extrapolation** methods are used to correct for the Reynolds number mismatch. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students wrongly assume that setting $F_n$ equal also satisfies Reynolds similarity. This is **incorrect** — Froude scaling gives $V \propto \sqrt{L}$, but Reynolds scaling demands $V \propto 1/L$. The two are fundamentally incompatible at small scales. **(–2 marks deduction if not mentioned)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a) Explain Buckingham's Pi theorem and apply it to ship resistance to derive the functional relationship:** $$f\!\left(\frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2},\ \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}},\ \frac{VL}{\nu}\right) = 0$$ **Identify each Pi term and state its physical significance.** **(7 marks)** **(b) A geometrically similar model of a cargo ship is tested at $\lambda = 1/30$. The prototype has $L_p = 180$ m, $B_p = 28$ m, $T_p = 10.5$ m, $C_B = 0.68$, and $S_p = 8500$ m². Determine:** - (i) Model displacement in kg - (ii) Model wetted surface area - (iii) Model speed if the prototype is to operate at 15 knots, using Froude similarity - (iv) Verify that the form coefficients are preserved. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Part B** #### Part (a) — Buckingham Pi Theorem Application (7 marks) **Statement of the Theorem** (1 mark): Buckingham's Pi Theorem states that if a physical phenomenon involves $n$ physical variables and $k$ fundamental dimensions, then the relationship can be expressed in terms of $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups called **Pi terms**. **Identify Variables and Dimensions** (1 mark): For ship resistance, the relevant variables and their dimensions in the $[M, L, T]$ system are: | Variable | Symbol | Dimensions | |---|---|---| | Resistance | $R$ | $[M L T^{-2}]$ | | Fluid Density | $\rho$ | $[M L^{-3}]$ | | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $[L^2 T^{-1}]$ | | Gravity | $g$ | $[L T^{-2}]$ | | Ship Speed | $V$ | $[L T^{-1}]$ | | Ship Length | $L$ | $[L]$ | So $n = 6$ variables and $k = 3$ dimensions, giving $n - k = 3$ dimensionless Pi groups. **Select Repeating Variables** (1 mark): Choose $\rho$, $V$, and $L$ as the repeating variables (they collectively span all three dimensions $[M, L, T]$). **Form Pi 1 (resistance group)** (1 mark): $$\Pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho^a V^b L^c}$$ Setting dimensions equal: $$[M L T^{-2}] = [M L^{-3}]^a [L T^{-1}]^b [L]^c = [M^a \cdot L^{-3a+b+c} \cdot T^{-b}]$$ Solving: $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $c = 2$. $$\Pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \text{(Resistance Coefficient, } C_R \text{)}$$ **Form Pi 2 (Froude group)** (1 mark): $$\Pi_2 = \frac{g^a V^b L^c}{1} \quad \text{(constructing from } g, V, L\text{)}$$ For Froude number, the standard form is $V / \sqrt{gL}$: $$\Pi_2 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = F_n \quad \text{(Froude Number — wave gravity effects)}$$ **Form Pi 3 (Reynolds group)** (1 mark): $$\Pi_3 = \frac{V L}{\nu} = R_n \quad \text{(Reynolds Number — viscous effects)}$$ **Final Functional Relationship** (1 mark): $$f\!\left(\frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2},\ \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}},\ \frac{VL}{\nu}\right) = 0$$ | Pi Term | Name | Physical Significance | |---|---|---| | $\Pi_1 = R / (\rho V^2 L^2)$ | Resistance Coefficient $C_R$ | Non-dimensionalized total resistance force | | $\Pi_2 = V / \sqrt{gL}$ | Froude Number $F_n$ | Ratio of inertial to gravitational forces; governs wave-making | | $\Pi_3 = VL/\nu$ | Reynolds Number $R_n$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces; governs frictional resistance | --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 marks) **Step 1: Model dimensions** (1 mark) $$L_m = \frac{180}{30} = 6.00 \text{ m}, \quad B_m = \frac{28}{30} = 0.933 \text{ m}, \quad T_m = \frac{10.5}{30} = 0.350 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: (i) Model displacement in kg** (2 marks) Prototype displacement volume: $$\nabla_p = C_B \cdot L_p \cdot B_p \cdot T_p = 0.68 \times 180 \times 28 \times 10.5 = 35{,}942.4 \text{ m}^3$$ Model volume: $$\nabla_m = \lambda^3 \cdot \nabla_p = \frac{1}{27{,}000} \times 35{,}942.4 = 1.331 \text{ m}^3$$ Model displacement mass ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ for salt water): $$\Delta_m = 1.331 \times 1025 = 1364.3 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3: (ii) Model wetted surface area** (1 mark) $$S_m = \lambda^2 \cdot S_p = \frac{1}{900} \times 8500 = 9.444 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4: (iii) Model speed under Froude similarity** (2 marks) $$V_m = \sqrt{\lambda} \cdot V_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{30}} \times 15 \text{ knots}$$ $$\sqrt{30} = 5.477 \implies V_m = \frac{15}{5.477} = 2.738 \text{ knots}$$ In m/s: $V_m = 2.738 \times 0.5144 = 1.408$ m/s. **Step 5: (iv) Verification of form coefficient preservation** (1 mark) Block coefficient (model): $$C_{B,m} = \frac{\nabla_m}{L_m \cdot B_m \cdot T_m} = \frac{1.331}{6.00 \times 0.933 \times 0.350} = \frac{1.331}{1.960} = 0.679 \approx 0.68 \quad ✓$$ This equals $C_{B,p} = 0.68$, confirming form coefficient preservation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall**: For the model speed, students often write $V_m = \lambda \cdot V_p = 0.5$ knots (wrong — that's linear scaling). Froude scaling gives $V_m = \sqrt{\lambda} \cdot V_p = 2.74$ knots. Using the wrong scaling exponent is the most common error. **(–2 marks deduction)** --- ## 🔥 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Geometrical Similarity & Dimensional Analysis** - ☐ **Geometrical similarity** requires constant linear scale ratio $\lambda$ **AND** identical corresponding angles. - ☐ **Length scales as $\lambda$**, **areas as $\lambda^2$**, **volumes (and mass in same fluid) as $\lambda^3$**. - ☐ **All form coefficients are dimensionless** and therefore **scale-invariant** — they must match exactly. - ☐ **Displacement mass ratio**: $\Delta_m / \Delta_p = \lambda^3 \cdot (\rho_m / \rho_p)$. For same water, this is $\lambda^3$. - ☐ **Wetted Surface Area** is the **only** area that scales as $\lambda^2$ — it determines frictional resistance. - ☐ **Block coefficient** $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$ is the most-tested form coefficient in KTU exams. - ☐ **Buckingham's Pi Theorem**: $n$ variables, $k$ dimensions $\Rightarrow$ $(n - k)$ dimensionless Pi groups. - ☐ **Three classical Pi terms in ship resistance**: Resistance Coefficient $C_R$, Froude Number $F_n$, Reynolds Number $R_n$. - ☐ **Froude scaling** gives $V_m = \sqrt{\lambda} \cdot V_p$ (not $\lambda \cdot V_p$). - ☐ **Reynolds scaling** would require $V_m = V_p / \lambda$ — **incompatible with Froude scaling** at model scales. - ☐ **The Froude-Reynolds conflict** is the central problem in ship model testing, resolved via ITTC form factor methods. - ☐ **Laws of comparison hierarchy**: Geometrical (shape) $\rightarrow$ Kinematical (motion) $\rightarrow$ Dynamical (force). Each level assumes the previous. - ☐ In equations, **always specify the scale ratio $\lambda$** and indicate whether fluid densities are equal.

topics.title: Kinematical and Dynamical Similarity
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematical and Dynamical Similarity

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Naval Architecture** and **Marine Hydrodynamics**, *similarity* is the foundational principle that allows engineers to predict the hydrodynamic performance of a full-scale ship (the **prototype**) by testing a geometrically scaled-down model in a towing tank or a circulating water channel. Without proper similitude, model test results are scientifically meaningless and cannot be extrapolated to the real ship.

The concept of similarity is hierarchically divided into three types:

1. **Geometric Similarity (GS):** The model and prototype have the same shape but different sizes. Every linear dimension in the model is a constant multiple of the corresponding dimension in the prototype. This constant is the **scale ratio** ($\lambda$).

2. **Kinematic Similarity (KS):** The model and prototype have geometrically similar streamlines, and the ratio of velocities at corresponding points is constant throughout the flow field. The motion (kinematics) of the fluid around the two bodies is similar.

3. **Dynamic Similarity (DS):** Geometrically similar bodies with kinematically similar flows where the ratios of all forces (viscous, inertial, gravitational, surface tension) acting on corresponding fluid elements and bodies are the same. In other words, the dimensionless force coefficients are identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Rule of Similarity:**
> Dynamic Similarity $\Rightarrow$ Kinematic Similarity $\Rightarrow$ Geometric Similarity
>
> Establishing Geometric Similarity is the **prerequisite**, but it alone is **insufficient**. To have KS, we need GS plus similarity of the flow pattern (velocity field). To have DS, we need KS plus similarity of all the force ratios (dynamic equilibrium of fluid particles).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a **miniature remote-controlled toy car** to test a new aerodynamic design before building the full-size race car.

- **Geometric Similarity:** The toy is a perfect, scaled-down *shape* clone of the race car — every curve, every angle, just smaller. If the race car is 4 m long and the toy is 40 cm long, the scale ratio $\lambda = 0.1$.
- **Kinematic Similarity:** The *motion* of the air around both cars is identical. Air particles speeding over the hood, swirling around the spoiler, and trailing behind the bumper behave in the *same pattern* in both cases, just at different speeds. If the toy is tested at $V_m$, the race car should be driven at $V_p$ such that the velocity ratios match.
- **Dynamic Similarity:** The *forces* (inertia, viscous drag, lift, weight) are in the same proportions. The ratio of "inertia force" to "viscous force" must be identical for both. If this is not met, the air behaves differently — for example, flow may be turbulent on the race car but laminar on the toy, making the test useless.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Ship Resistance**, the two dominant forces are **gravity** (causing wave-making resistance) and **viscous shear** (causing frictional resistance). To match both simultaneously is **mathematically impossible** at a single scale. This fundamental conflict — known as the **Conflict of Similitude** — is the central problem of ship model testing. The KTU syllabus expects students to understand that the **Froude number** is used to match wave effects, and the **Reynolds number** is used to match viscous effects, leading to a compromise known as the **Froude Scaling with Reynolds Correction**.

The standard physical constants in this topic are:

- Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at 20 °C: $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Kinematic viscosity of sea water at 20 °C: $\nu \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m}/\text{s}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Similarity — The Foundation

If a model and prototype are geometrically similar with linear scale ratio $\lambda$, then for every dimension $L$:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_p} = \frac{B_m}{B_p} = \frac{T_m}{T_p} = \frac{\nabla_m^{1/3}}{\nabla_p^{1/3}}$$
