The **Why**: If the linear scale is $\lambda$, then the area (proportional to $L^2$) scales as $\lambda^2$ and the volume (proportional to $L^3$) scales as $\lambda^3$. This has cascading effects on all derived physical quantities.

| Quantity | Scaling Law | Reason |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Length, $L$ | $\lambda^1$ | Direct definition |
| Area, $A$ | $\lambda^2$ | Area $\propto L^2$ |
| Volume, $\nabla$ | $\lambda^3$ | Volume $\propto L^3$ |
| Wetted Surface, $S$ | $\lambda^2$ | Area scales as $L^2$ |

## 2.2 Kinematic Similarity — The Motion Equivalence

Kinematic similarity demands that the ratio of velocities at every *corresponding* point in the flow field is the same:

$$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \frac{v_m}{v_p} = \text{constant} = V_r$$

where $V_r$ is the **velocity scale ratio**. From this, using the relationship $L = V \cdot t$, the time scale follows as:

$$t_r = \frac{L_r}{V_r} = \frac{\lambda}{V_r}$$

The **How**: This is derived from the geometric similarity condition. If two systems are geometrically similar, the time taken by a fluid particle to traverse a corresponding distance in the two systems must be proportional to the length, divided by the velocity. Therefore, the time ratio automatically emerges from the length and velocity ratios.

| Kinematic Quantity | Symbol | Scale Ratio |
|--------------------|--------|-------------|
| Linear dimension | $L_r$ | $\lambda$ |
| Velocity | $V_r$ | $V_r$ (set by Froude or Reynolds) |
| Time | $t_r$ | $\lambda / V_r$ |
| Acceleration | $a_r$ | $V_r^2 / \lambda$ |
| Discharge / Volumetric flow | $Q_r$ | $\lambda^2 \cdot V_r$ |
| Angular velocity | $\omega_r$ | $V_r / \lambda$ |

## 2.3 Dynamic Similarity — The Force Equivalence

Dynamic similarity requires the ratio of *all* types of forces acting on corresponding fluid elements to be constant. The general force scaling rule, derived from the **Buckingham $\Pi$ Theorem** and Newton's Second Law, is:

$$F = m \cdot a = \rho \cdot L^3 \cdot \frac{V^2}{L} = \rho \cdot L^2 \cdot V^2$$

Therefore, the **force scale ratio** is:

$$F_r = \rho_r \cdot L_r^2 \cdot V_r^2$$

In ship hydrodynamics, four primary force types act on the hull:
- **Inertial Force** ($F_I$) — the resistance of fluid to acceleration.
- **Viscous Force** ($F_V$) — the shear force from fluid viscosity.
- **Gravitational Force** ($F_G$) — the restoring force causing wave formation.
- **Pressure Force** ($F_P$) — the normal stress from fluid pressure.

For complete DS, we need:

$$\frac{F_I}{F_V} = \text{same in both} \quad ; \quad \frac{F_I}{F_G} = \text{same in both} \quad ; \quad \frac{F_I}{F_P} = \text{same in both}$$

Each of these ratios gives rise to a **dimensionless number** that must be matched.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This is your one-stop revision sheet. Memorize the dimensionless groups — they are the heart of KTU questions on this topic.**

| # | Dimensionless Number | Symbol | Definition | Physical Meaning | Governing Regime |
|---|----------------------|--------|------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Reynolds Number** | $R_n$ | $\frac{VL}{\nu}$ | Inertia $\div$ Viscous | Viscous flow, boundary layer |
| 2 | **Froude Number** | $F_n$ | $\frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Inertia $\div$ Gravity | Wave-making, free-surface |
| 3 | **Euler Number** | $E_u$ | $\frac{p}{\rho V^2}$ | Pressure $\div$ Inertia | Pressure forces, cavitation |
| 4 | **Weber Number** | $W_e$ | $\frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}$ | Inertia $\div$ Surface tension | Spray, bubble formation |
| 5 | **Mach Number** | $M$ | $\frac{V}{c}$ | Flow $\div$ Sound speed | Compressibility |
| 6 | **Cauchy Number** | $C$ | $\frac{\rho V^2}{E}$ | Inertia $\div$ Elasticity | Structural deformation |

### Key Scaling Laws (KTU Most-Tested)

| Quantity | Reynolds Scaling (Viscous) | Froude Scaling (Gravity) |
|----------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Velocity ratio $V_r$ | $\nu_r / \lambda$ | $\sqrt{\lambda}$ |
| Time ratio $t_r$ | $\lambda^2 / \nu_r$ | $\sqrt{\lambda}$ |
| Force ratio $F_r$ | $\rho_r \cdot \nu_r^2$ | $\rho_r \cdot \lambda^3$ |
| Power ratio $P_r$ | $\rho_r \cdot \nu_r^3 / \lambda$ | $\rho_r \cdot \lambda^{3.5}$ |
| Resistance ratio $R_r$ | $\mu_r \cdot V_r \cdot L_r$ | $\rho_r \cdot g_r \cdot \lambda^3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict of Similitude:** For a ship model tested in the same fluid as the prototype (water), we need simultaneously $F_{n,m} = F_{n,p}$ **and** $R_{n,m} = R_{n,p}$. Froude scaling gives $V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$, and Reynolds scaling gives $V_r = \nu_r / \lambda$. Equating these: $\nu_r = \lambda^{3/2}$, which is physically impossible to achieve (we cannot change the fluid viscosity at will). Hence the **Froude scaling with ITTC-78 frictional resistance correction** is the standard industry solution.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Towing Tank Tests:** Predicting the calm-water resistance of a 300 m tanker using a 3-6 m model.
- **Cavitation Tunnels:** Studying propeller cavitation where Euler number must be matched.
- **Seakeeping Basins:** Generating realistic ocean waves to test ship motions using Froude scaling.
- **Submarine Design:** Matching both Reynolds number (for boundary layer) and Froude number (for free-surface effects) is impossible, so Reynolds-scaled models in pressurised tunnels are used.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Non-dimensionalisation of Navier-Stokes equations follows the same similitude principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Scale Ratio from Newton's Second Law

**Starting point:** Newton's second law, $F = m \cdot a$, expressed in terms of fluid properties. For a control volume of fluid with characteristic length $L$, velocity $V$, and density $\rho$:

**Step 1:** Express the mass of the fluid element in terms of its volume and density.

$$m = \rho \cdot L^3$$

**Step 2:** Express the acceleration in terms of velocity and length (convective acceleration scale).

$$a = \frac{V^2}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into $F = m \cdot a$ to obtain the **inertial force scale**.

$$F_I = \rho \cdot L^3 \cdot \frac{V^2}{L} = \rho \cdot L^2 \cdot V^2$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio of model to prototype inertial forces to get the **force scale ratio**.

$$F_r = \frac{F_{I,m}}{F_{I,p}} = \frac{\rho_m \cdot L_m^2 \cdot V_m^2}{\rho_p \cdot L_p^2 \cdot V_p^2} = \rho_r \cdot L_r^2 \cdot V_r^2$$

This fundamental relation governs **all** dynamic similarity derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reynolds Number Equality and Viscous Velocity Scale

**Step 1:** Write the Reynolds number for model and prototype.

$$R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} \quad ; \quad R_{n,p} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p}$$

**Step 2:** For dynamic similarity with respect to viscous and inertial forces, the Reynolds numbers must be equal.

$$R_{n,m} = R_{n,p}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply and isolate the velocity ratio.

$$V_m L_m \nu_p = V_p L_p \nu_m$$

$$V_r = \frac{V_m}{V_p} = \frac{L_p}{L_m} \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \nu_r = \frac{\nu_r}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** State the result clearly. Under Reynolds scaling with the same fluid ($\nu_r = 1$):

$$V_r = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This means the model must be towed at a *higher* speed than the prototype — often impractically high for small models.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Froude Number Equality and Gravitational Velocity Scale

**Step 1:** Write the Froude number for model and prototype.

$$F_{n,m} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g_m L_m}} \quad ; \quad F_{n,p} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g_p L_p}}$$

**Step 2:** For dynamic similarity with respect to gravitational and inertial forces (wave-making), equate them.

$$F_{n,m} = F_{n,p}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides and isolate the velocity ratio.

$$\frac{V_m^2}{g_m L_m} = \frac{V_p^2}{g_p L_p}$$

$$V_r^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{V_p^2} = \frac{g_m L_m}{g_p L_p} = g_r \cdot \lambda$$

**Step 4:** With $g_r = 1$ (same gravitational field on Earth):

$$V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$$

This is the **single most important equation in ship model testing**, used in nearly every KTU numerical problem.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Scaling Law under Froude Similarity

**Step 1:** Write the power scale ratio as the product of force and velocity ratios.

$$P_r = F_r \cdot V_r$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the Froude-scaled force and velocity ratios. The Froude force ratio comes from equating $F_r = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot V_r^2 = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \lambda = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^3$.

$$P_r = (\rho_r \cdot \lambda^3) \cdot \sqrt{\lambda} = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^{3.5}$$

**Step 3:** With the same fluid ($\rho_r = 1$):

$$P_r = \lambda^{3.5} = \lambda^{7/2}$$

This is why **the model's power is drastically less** than the prototype's. For $\lambda = 1/25$ (typical), $P_r = (1/25)^{3.5} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-5}$, meaning the model is driven by an extremely small motor (a few hundred watts vs. tens of thousands of horsepower).

## 3.5 Derivation 5: ITTC-78 Frictional Resistance Correction

This is the **standard industry method** to correct for the Reynolds scale effect in ship model testing.

**Step 1:** The total resistance coefficient $C_T$ is split into frictional ($C_F$) and residual ($C_R$) parts.

$$C_T = C_F + C_R$$

**Step 2:** The frictional coefficient is calculated using the ITTC-78 correlation line.

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Step 3:** The residual resistance coefficient is computed from the model test (which has the wrong Reynolds number, but correct Froude number).

$$C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - C_{F,m}$$

**Step 4:** The total resistance coefficient for the prototype is reconstructed by adding the model residual to the *prototype* frictional coefficient.

$$C_{T,p} = C_{F,p} + C_{R,m}$$

**Step 5:** The prototype resistance follows from the dynamic pressure.

$$R_T = C_{T,p} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_p \cdot V_p^2 \cdot S_p$$

This is the **ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method** — the KTU syllabus specifies this by name, so be prepared to reproduce it.

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Similarities

| Aspect | Geometric Similarity | Kinematic Similarity | Dynamic Similarity |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Concerned with | Shape & Size | Motion & Velocity field | Forces & Moments |
| Condition | $L_r = \text{const}$ everywhere | $V_r = \text{const}$ everywhere | All force ratios equal |
| Required to achieve KS? | Yes (prerequisite) | — | — |
| Required to achieve DS? | Yes | Yes | — |
| Governing dimensionless group | Scale ratio $\lambda$ | Velocity ratio $V_r$ | $R_n, F_n, E_u$ |
| Verification method | Physical measurement | Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) | Force balance / load cell |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Hierarchy of Similitude in Ship Model Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ship Model Testing\nGoal: Predict Prototype Performance"]
    A --> B["Step 1\nGeometric Similarity\nShape Match: L_r = constant"]
    B --> C["Step 2\nKinematic Similarity\nVelocity Field Match: V_r = constant"]
    C --> D["Step 3\nDynamic Similarity\nForce Ratios Match: F_r = constant"]
    D --> E{"Which Force is Dominant?"}
    E --> F["Viscous Forces\nMatch Reynolds Number\nR_n = V*L/nu"]
    E --> G["Gravity Forces\nMatch Froude Number\nF_n = V/sqrt g L"]
    E --> H["Pressure Forces\nMatch Euler Number\nE_u = p/rho V squared"]
    F --> I["Reynolds Scaled Test\nHigh V_r, often impractical"]
    G --> J["Froude Scaled Test\nIndustry Standard for Ships"]
    H --> K["Cavitation Tunnel Test\nPropeller Applications"]
    J --> L["Apply ITTC 1978 Correction\nfor Viscous Scale Effect"]
    L --> M["Predict Prototype R_T, P_E"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Similitude Calculation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT BLOCK"]
        I1["Prototype L_p, V_p, nu_p"]
        I2["Model L_m, nu_m"]
        I3["Scale Ratio lambda = L_m / L_p"]
    end
    subgraph ScaleEngine["SCALING ENGINE BLOCK"]
        S1["Compute V_r from Froude = sqrt lambda"]
        S2["Compute V_r from Reynolds = nu_r / lambda"]
        S3["Compare: Detect Conflict of Similitude"]
        S4["Choose Froude Priority\nStandard for Ships"]
    end
    subgraph Output["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        O1["V_m = V_p times V_r"]
        O2["t_r = lambda / V_r"]
        O3["F_r = rho_r times lambda cubed"]
        O4["P_r = rho_r times lambda 3.5"]
        O5["R_T p from ITTC 78\nC_T p = C_F p + C_R m"]
    end
    I1 --> I3
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> S1
    I3 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> O1
    S4 --> O2
    S4 --> O3
    S4 --> O4
    S4 --> O5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Decision Tree for Selecting Similarity Criterion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Flow Problem Identification"] --> Q1{"Is the Body\nSubmerged?"}
    Q1 -- No Free Surface --> R1["Use Reynolds Number\nSubmarine, Underwater Vehicle"]
    Q1 -- Yes Free Surface --> R2["Use Froude Number\nShips, Surface Vessels"]
    R2 --> Q2{"Is the Body\nCompletely\nSubmerged in\nthe wave zone?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> C1["Use Reynolds plus Froude\nTorpedoes near surface"]
    Q2 -- No --> C2["Use Froude only\nwith ITTC 78 correction"]
    R1 --> Q3{"Is Cavitation\nRelevant?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Cav["Use Euler plus Reynolds\nCavitation Tunnel"]
    Q3 -- No --> Reg["Reynolds only\nPressure Tunnel"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define geometric, kinematic, and dynamic similarity. Why is establishing dynamic similarity considered the strongest condition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Geometric similarity (1 mark):** The model and prototype have identical shapes with all linear dimensions in a constant ratio $\lambda = L_m/L_p$.
- **Kinematic similarity (1 mark):** The motion of fluid around the model and prototype is similar; corresponding velocities at corresponding points are in a fixed ratio $V_r = V_m/V_p$.
- **Dynamic similarity (1 mark — why strongest):** It is the strongest because it requires equality of all force ratios (inertial, viscous, gravitational). This automatically implies both kinematic and geometric similarity, since forces govern both motion (kinematics) and shape (geometry) of the flow.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is meant by the "Conflict of Similitude" in ship model testing? How is it resolved in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Conflict (2 marks):** For complete dynamic similarity, both Froude number ($F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$) and Reynolds number ($R_n = VL/\nu$) must match between model and prototype. Equating $F_{n,m} = F_{n,p}$ gives $V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$. Equating $R_{n,m} = R_{n,p}$ gives $V_r = \nu_r/\lambda$. For the same fluid ($\nu_r = 1$), these demand $\lambda = 1$, meaning no scale change — which defeats the purpose of model testing.
- **Resolution (1 mark):** Industry adopts **Froude scaling** (priority to wave-making) and applies the **ITTC-1978 frictional resistance correction** to account for the Reynolds number mismatch.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Froude scaling problems, students often forget to apply the **square root** to $\lambda$ for the velocity ratio. Many write $V_r = \lambda$. This loses 2 marks immediately.
> - In ITTC-78 questions, students confuse the frictional coefficient of the *prototype* with that of the *model*. The correction step requires $C_{F,p}$ — not $C_{F,m}$ — to be added to $C_{R,m}$. This is the most-graded step.
> - Always quote the **scale ratio** as a dimensionless number (e.g., 1:25), not as a unit-bearing quantity. State assumptions clearly: "assuming same fluid, same gravity."
> - In power scaling, do not mix up $P_r = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^{3.5}$ (Froude) with $P_r = \rho_r \cdot \nu_r^3 / \lambda$ (Reynolds). The exponent is the giveaway.

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 14 marks]

A 200 m long ship is to be modelled at a scale of 1:25. The ship operates at 20 knots in sea water ($\nu_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The model is tested in fresh water ($\nu_m = 1.00 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). Take $\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³ and $\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³.

**(a) Compute the model speed, time ratio, and resistance scale ratio under pure Froude scaling.** (7 marks)

**(b) Calculate the Reynolds numbers of model and prototype. What percentage error in viscous similarity does the Froude-only scaling introduce? Comment.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Prototype length: $L_p = 200$ m
- Scale ratio: $\lambda = 1/25 = 0.04$
- Prototype speed: $V_p = 20$ knots
- Conversion: $1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s, so $V_p = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288$ m/s
- $\nu_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s; $\nu_m = 1.00 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³; $\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³

### Part (a) — Froude Scaling [7 marks]

**Step 1: Velocity scale ratio under Froude similarity** [2 marks]

$$V_r = \frac{V_m}{V_p} = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{0.04} = 0.2$$

**Step 2: Model speed** [2 marks]

$$V_m = V_r \cdot V_p = 0.2 \times 10.288 = 2.0576 \text{ m/s}$$

In knots: $V_m = 2.0576 / 0.5144 \approx 4.0$ knots.

**Step 3: Time scale ratio** [1.5 marks]

$$t_r = \frac{\lambda}{V_r} = \frac{0.04}{0.2} = 0.2$$

This means 1 second in the model = 5 seconds in the prototype.

**Step 4: Resistance (force) scale ratio under Froude** [1.5 marks]

$$F_r = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^3 = \frac{1000}{1025} \times (0.04)^3 = 0.9756 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 6.244 \times 10^{-5}$$

So $R_{T,m} = R_{T,p} \times 6.244 \times 10^{-5}$.

### Part (b) — Reynolds Number Comparison [7 marks]

**Step 1: Reynolds number of the prototype** [2 marks]

$$R_{n,p} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p} = \frac{10.288 \times 200}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2057.6}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.96 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 2: Reynolds number of the model** [2 marks]

$$R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{2.0576 \times 8}{1.00 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{16.46}{1.00 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.646 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3: Ratio of Reynolds numbers** [1.5 marks]

$$\frac{R_{n,m}}{R_{n,p}} = \frac{1.646 \times 10^{7}}{1.96 \times 10^{9}} = 0.00840 = \frac{1}{119}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation** [1.5 marks]

The model Reynolds number is only $1/119^{\text{th}}$ of the prototype. This means the **boundary layer** on the model is far more laminar-dominated than on the prototype, where it is fully turbulent. The viscous force ratio is wrong by two orders of magnitude. This is the **scale effect**, and the ITTC-78 correction (subtracting $C_{F,m}$ and adding $C_{F,p}$) is mandatory to obtain accurate prototype resistance predictions.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 14 marks]

**(a) Derive the velocity, time, force, and power scale ratios under pure Froude similarity. State all assumptions clearly.** (7 marks)

**(b) A geometrically similar model of a ship is tested in a towing tank. The model is 4 m long, the prototype is 100 m long, and the model is towed at 2.5 m/s. Compute the prototype speed, the effective power ratio, and the total resistance of the prototype if the model's total resistance is 12 N. Assume the same fluid.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation of Froude Scale Ratios [7 marks]

**Assumption 1:** Same fluid in model and prototype, hence $\rho_r = 1$ and $\nu_r = 1$.

**Assumption 2:** Same gravitational field, hence $g_r = 1$.

**Assumption 3:** Geometric similarity: $L_r = \lambda$.

**Step 1: Velocity ratio** [2 marks]

Equating Froude numbers: $\dfrac{V_m^2}{g L_m} = \dfrac{V_p^2}{g L_p}$

$$V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$$

**Step 2: Time ratio** [1.5 marks]

$$t_r = \frac{L_r}{V_r} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \sqrt{\lambda}$$

**Step 3: Force (Resistance) ratio** [2 marks]

From $F_r = \rho_r L_r^2 V_r^2$ and substituting $V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$:

$$F_r = 1 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \lambda = \lambda^3$$

**Step 4: Power ratio** [1.5 marks]

$$P_r = F_r \cdot V_r = \lambda^3 \cdot \sqrt{\lambda} = \lambda^{3.5} = \lambda^{7/2}$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $L_m = 4$ m, $L_p = 100$ m, $V_m = 2.5$ m/s
- Same fluid: $\rho_r = 1$
- $R_{T,m} = 12$ N

**Step 1: Scale ratio** [1 mark]

$$\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_p} = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04$$

**Step 2: Prototype speed** [2 marks]

$$V_r = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{0.04} = 0.2$$

$$V_p = \frac{V_m}{V_r} = \frac{2.5}{0.2} = 12.5 \text{ m/s}$$

In knots: $V_p = 12.5 / 0.5144 \approx 24.3$ knots.

**Step 3: Effective power ratio** [2 marks]

$$P_r = \lambda^{3.5} = (0.04)^{3.5}$$

Computing: $(0.04)^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$ and $\sqrt{0.04} = 0.2$, so $P_r = 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.2 = 1.28 \times 10^{-5}$.

**Step 4: Total resistance of prototype** [2 marks]

$$R_r = \lambda^3 = (0.04)^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$R_{T,p} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{R_r} = \frac{12}{6.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 187{,}500 \text{ N} = 187.5 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this in your notebook!**

- **Three pillars of similarity:** Geometric → Kinematic → Dynamic. Dynamic is the strongest; it implies the other two.
- **Scale ratio** $\lambda = L_m/L_p$ is the foundational dimensionless quantity. All other ratios are derived from it.
- **Froude Number** $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ governs **wave-making resistance** (free-surface effects). Match this for ship models.
- **Reynolds Number** $R_n = VL/\nu$ governs **viscous / frictional resistance** (boundary layer). Cannot be matched simultaneously with $F_n$ in the same fluid.
- **Conflict of Similitude:** Froude demands $V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$; Reynolds demands $V_r = \nu_r / \lambda$. For the same fluid, $\lambda = 1$, which is impossible.
- **Standard Velocity Ratios:**
  - Froude: $V_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$
  - Reynolds: $V_r = \nu_r / \lambda$
- **Standard Force Ratio (Froude):** $F_r = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^3$
- **Standard Power Ratio (Froude):** $P_r = \rho_r \cdot \lambda^{7/2}$
- **Time Ratio (Froude):** $t_r = \sqrt{\lambda}$
- **ITTC-1978 Correction Formula:** $C_{T,p} = C_{F,p} + C_{R,m}$ where $C_R = C_T - C_F$. The frictional coefficient uses $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$.
- **Forces in ship hydrodynamics:** Inertial, Viscous, Gravitational, Pressure. Ratio of any two gives a dimensionless number.
- **Conversion factor:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s (memorize!).
- **Assumption set for ship model testing:** same fluid, same gravity, geometric similarity, Froude priority.
- **For the same fluid:** $\rho_r = 1$, $g_r = 1$, $\nu_r = 1$ — this simplifies many formulae.
- **Exam shortcut:** If asked for "model speed", always start with $V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\lambda}$.
- **Common unit conversions:** $1 \text{ kN} = 1000 \text{ N}$; $1 \text{ knot} = 1 \text{ nautical mile/hour} = 1852 \text{ m/hour}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's, Froude's and Reynold's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Newton's, Froude's and Reynold's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Three Laws of Similitude in Ship Hydrodynamics

In ship resistance analysis, model testing is the cornerstone of performance prediction. Because a full-scale ship cannot be towed in an open sea under controlled conditions, naval architects rely on **scaled-down geometrically similar models** tested in towing tanks. However, simply scaling the geometry is *not* enough — the **flow physics** around the model must also mimic the flow around the full-scale ship. This requirement is formalized through the three Laws of Similitude attributed to **Sir Isaac Newton**, **William Froude**, and **Osborne Reynolds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between:
> - **Law of Similitude (Newton's Law)** — the *theoretical ideal* governing *all* forces simultaneously.
> - **Law of Comparison (Froude's Law & Reynold's Law)** — *practical, partial* similarity laws used to extrapolate *specific* resistance components from model to ship.

---

## 1.2 Newton's Law of Similarity (Dynamic Similitude)

### Formal Definition
> **Newton's Law of Dynamic Similarity** states that for two geometrically and kinematically similar flow systems, the **ratios of all forces acting on corresponding fluid elements must be equal**. This implies a single dimensionless number — the **Newton Number** (or force coefficient) — must be preserved between model and prototype.

Mathematically, the **Newton Number** is defined as:

$$N_e = \frac{F}{\rho \cdot L^2 \cdot V^2}$$

where **$F$** is the resultant force, **$\rho$** is the fluid density, **$L$** is the characteristic length, and **$V$** is the characteristic velocity.

For dynamic similarity: $\ (N_e)_m = (N_e)_s$.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine watching two dancers — one tiny and one giant — perform the *exact same* pirouette. For the move to "look identical" dynamically, every force ratio (gravity, air drag, muscle torque) must scale proportionally. Newton's Law demands *all* these force ratios match simultaneously. In practice, this is **almost impossible** for ship models because gravity, viscosity, and surface tension all want different scaling rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation of Newton's Law in Ship Model Testing:**
> For a ship moving on a free water surface, achieving perfect Newton's similarity would require matching **Froude Number**, **Reynolds Number**, **Weber Number**, and **Mach Number** all at once — which is physically impossible unless the model and ship use the same fluid and travel at the same speed. Hence, naval architects use *partial* similarity laws.

---

## 1.3 Froude's Law of Comparison (Wave-Making Similitude)

### Formal Definition
> **Froude's Law of Comparison** states that for the **wave-making (gravitational) component** of ship resistance, dynamic similarity is achieved when the **Froude Number** of the model equals that of the ship. This preserves the wave pattern generated by the hull.

The Froude Number is the ratio of **inertial force to gravitational force**:

$$F_r = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

For Froude's similarity: $\ (F_r)_m = (F_r)_s$.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a duckling and an adult duck swimming at the "right" speed each. The duckling flaps fast in tiny ripples; the adult duck moves slowly in large waves. The *ratio* of speed to wave-making disturbance is preserved. Froude's Law captures exactly this: the *wave geometry* must look the same, even though the absolute size of the waves differs.

---

## 1.4 Reynold's Law of Comparison (Viscous Similitude)

### Formal Definition
> **Reynold's Law of Comparison** states that for the **viscous (frictional) component** of ship resistance, dynamic similarity is achieved when the **Reynolds Number** of the model equals that of the ship. This ensures the **boundary layer flow regime** (laminar/turbulent) and its structure are preserved.

The Reynolds Number is the ratio of **inertial force to viscous force**:

$$R_e = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$

For Reynold's similarity: $\ (R_e)_m = (R_e)_s$, where **$\nu$** is the kinematic viscosity of the fluid.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of stirring honey with a tiny spoon versus a giant ladle. To get the *same kind of swirl* (laminar or turbulent) in both, you must adjust the speed appropriately. Reynold's Law tells you exactly how fast to stir each so the *flow character* matches.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Froude–Reynolds Conflict in Ship Testing:**
> Achieving **both** Froude and Reynolds similarity simultaneously in a water towing tank is impossible for a geometrically scaled model. The only way to match Re is to use a *different fluid* (e.g., very viscous oil) or *higher model speeds* — both of which distort wave patterns. The standard solution (ITTC 1978 procedure) is to use **Froude scaling for wave-making resistance** and **separate Reynolds scaling for frictional resistance**, then superimpose the components.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** (standard earth gravity) |
| Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at 20 °C | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Kinematic viscosity of sea water at 20 °C | $\nu$ | **1.05 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Density of fresh water | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of sea water | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law — Theoretical Breakdown

For two geometrically similar systems (model **m** and ship **s**), dynamic similarity requires the ratio of *every type of force* to the inertial force to be preserved:

$$\left(\frac{F_i}{F_{inertial}}\right)_m = \left(\frac{F_i}{F_{inertial}}\right)_s$$

The relevant forces in ship hydrodynamics are:

| Force Type | Symbol | Dominant In |
|---|---|---|
| Inertial Force | $F_I$ | All flows |
| Viscous Force | $F_V$ | Boundary layer, wake |
| Gravitational Force | $F_G$ | Free-surface waves |
| Surface Tension Force | $F_S$ | Small-scale effects |
| Compressibility Force | $F_C$ | High-speed craft |

Each ratio gives rise to a *dimensionless number*:

- $\dfrac{F_V}{F_I} \Rightarrow$ **Reynolds Number** ($R_e$)
- $\dfrac{F_G}{F_I} \Rightarrow$ **Froude Number** ($F_r$)
- $\dfrac{F_S}{F_I} \Rightarrow$ **Weber Number** ($W_e$)
- $\dfrac{F_C}{F_I} \Rightarrow$ **Mach Number** ($M_a$)

> **For perfect Newton's similarity, ALL these numbers must match** between model and ship. This is the **fundamental conflict** that led to the development of the *partial comparison laws* by Froude and Reynolds.

---

## 2.2 Froude's Law of Comparison — Detailed Theory

### Governing Assumption
The total resistance $R_T$ is split into two independent parts:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R$$

where $R_F$ is the **frictional resistance** (viscous) and $R_R$ is the **residual resistance** (wave-making + eddy-making + appendage). Froude's law applies to the **residual component**.

### Required Similarity
$$\left(F_r\right)_m = \left(F_r\right)_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_s}}$$

### Speed Scaling Equation
Solving for the ship speed:

$$V_s = V_m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}}$$

### Resistance Scaling Equation
For the residual resistance, with both model and ship tested in the same fluid ($\rho_m = \rho_s$):

$$(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cube of the length scale?**
> Resistance $\propto$ (dynamic pressure) $\times$ (wetted area) $\propto (\rho V^2)(L^2)$. Substituting $V \propto \sqrt{L}$ from Froude similarity gives $R \propto \rho \cdot L \cdot L^2 = \rho L^3$. With constant $\rho$, it reduces to $L^3$.

---

## 2.3 Reynold's Law of Comparison — Detailed Theory

### Governing Assumption
Reynolds similarity applies to the **frictional resistance** $R_F$, which depends on the **boundary layer Reynolds number** based on the length of the run.

### Required Similarity
$$\left(R_e\right)_m = \left(R_e\right)_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_s \cdot L_s}{\nu_s}$$

### Speed Scaling Equation

$$V_s = V_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{\nu_s}{\nu_m}$$

### Frictional Resistance Formula (ITTC-1978 / Schoenherr)
The model frictional resistance is computed as:

$$(R_F)_m = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_m \cdot V_m^2 \cdot S_m \cdot C_{F_m}$$

where $C_{F_m}$ is the **frictional coefficient** from the **ITTC-57 correlation** (or equivalent):

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_e - 2)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Froude–Reynolds Conflict in Practice:**
> If Froude similarity fixes $V_s = V_m \sqrt{L_s/L_m}$, plugging this into Reynolds similarity gives a *required* kinematic viscosity ratio:
> $\dfrac{\nu_s}{\nu_m} = \left(\dfrac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^{3/2}$
> Since fresh water and sea water have nearly the same $\nu$, this condition is **not satisfied** in a water tank. This is why the ITTC extrapolation procedure separates the frictional and residual components.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_r = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude Number definition |
| 2 | $R_e = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number definition |
| 3 | $N_e = \dfrac{F}{\rho L^2 V^2}$ | Newton (Force) Number |
| 4 | $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{L_s}{L_m}}$ | Froude speed scaling |
| 5 | $(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m \left(\dfrac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3$ | Froude resistance scaling (same fluid) |
| 6 | $V_s = V_m \cdot \dfrac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \dfrac{\nu_s}{\nu_m}$ | Reynolds speed scaling |
| 7 | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ | Frictional resistance |
| 8 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_e - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-57 frictional coefficient |
| 9 | $R_T = R_F + R_R$ | Total resistance decomposition |
| 10 | $C_T = C_F + C_R$ | Total coefficient decomposition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Pitfall:** When using the ITTC formula, $R_e$ is based on the **ship's speed and length** in the **ship's fluid**, NOT on the model. Confusion between model and ship Reynolds numbers is a common KTU valuation error.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Naval Architecture** | Predicting full-scale ship resistance from towing-tank model tests |
| **Submarine Design** | Estimating frictional drag of streamlined bodies |
| **Yacht Design** | Optimising hull forms for low wave-making resistance |
| **Tidal / Wave Energy** | Scaling wave-energy converters between model and prototype |
| **Offshore Structures** | Estimating wave loads on platforms |
| **Aerospace (Loosely)** | Wind-tunnel testing follows analogous (Reynolds-based) principles |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Froude's Speed-Scaling Equation

We begin with the condition that the **Froude numbers** of the model (**m**) and ship (**s**) are equal:

$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_s}}$$

**Step 1 — Square both sides** to remove the square root:

$$\frac{V_m^2}{g \cdot L_m} = \frac{V_s^2}{g \cdot L_s}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $g$** (assumed identical in model tank and sea):

$$\frac{V_m^2}{L_m} = \frac{V_s^2}{L_s}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$V_s^2 \cdot L_m = V_m^2 \cdot L_s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_s$:**

$$V_s^2 = V_m^2 \cdot \frac{L_s}{L_m}$$

**Step 5 — Take the positive square root** (speeds are positive):

$$\boxed{V_s = V_m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Since $L_s > L_m$, the ship travels *faster* than the model in absolute terms, but slower relative to its own length.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Froude's Resistance-Scaling Equation

The **residual resistance** is a force that depends on dynamic pressure and wetted area. For a geometrically similar hull:

$$(R_R)_m \;\propto\; \rho_m \cdot V_m^2 \cdot L_m^2$$
$$(R_R)_s \;\propto\; \rho_s \cdot V_s^2 \cdot L_s^2$$

**Step 1 — Form the ratio:**

$$\frac{(R_R)_s}{(R_R)_m} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Froude speed scaling** $V_s / V_m = \sqrt{L_s / L_m}$:

$$\left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^2 = \frac{L_s}{L_m}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\frac{(R_R)_s}{(R_R)_m} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \frac{L_s}{L_m} \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^2 = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 — For the standard case of same fluid** ($\rho_s = \rho_m$):

$$\boxed{(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Reynold's Speed-Scaling Equation

Starting from Reynolds number equality:

$$\frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_s \cdot L_s}{\nu_s}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate $V_s$:**

$$V_s = V_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{\nu_s}{\nu_m}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio of kinematic viscosities.** If the model uses *hot* water to reduce $\nu_m$ (a common technique to better approximate Reynolds similarity), this factor becomes important:

$$\boxed{V_s = V_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{\nu_s}{\nu_m}}$$

> **Critical Engineering Observation:** Because $\nu_s / \nu_m \approx 1$ for water, this implies $V_s \approx V_m (L_m / L_s)$, which is **inconsistent** with Froude's requirement $V_s = V_m \sqrt{L_s/L_m}$. The two requirements demand *different* speeds — the heart of the **Froude–Reynolds conflict**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:**
> A ship model of length **$L_m = 4$ m** is towed in fresh water at **$V_m = 2.5$ m/s**. The corresponding ship has length **$L_s = 100$ m**. Compute:
> (a) The equivalent ship speed $V_s$ (Froude scaling).
> (b) The equivalent ship speed $V_s$ (Reynolds scaling, using fresh water for both).
> (c) The total resistance of the model measured as **$R_{T_m} = 18$ N**. The model's frictional resistance is **$(R_F)_m = 11$ N**. Using the ITTC-57 formula, determine the **residual resistance of the ship** $(R_R)_s$.
> (d) Comment on the two speed predictions.

### Given Data
- $L_m = 4$ m, $\;L_s = 100$ m, $\;V_m = 2.5$ m/s
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- Wetted-area scale factor (assuming geometric similarity): $S_s / S_m = (L_s / L_m)^2 = 625$
- Model frictional coefficient $C_{F_m}$ is calculated using ITTC-57.

---

### Part (a) — Froude Speed Scaling

Using the derived equation:

$$V_s = V_m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}}$$

$$V_s = 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{100}{4}} = 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{25} = 2.5 \cdot 5$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 12.5 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(Froude)}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Reynolds Speed Scaling (same fluid)

Since both model and ship are in fresh water, $\nu_s / \nu_m = 1$:

$$V_s = V_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_s} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{4}{100}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 0.10 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(Reynolds)}}$$

---

### Part (c) — Ship's Residual Resistance (ITTC Procedure)

**Step 1 — Compute model residual resistance:**

$$(R_R)_m = R_{T_m} - (R_F)_m = 18 - 11 = 7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Froude resistance scaling:**

$$(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3 = 7 \cdot (25)^3 = 7 \cdot 15625$$

$$(R_R)_s = 7 \cdot 15625 = 109375 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{(R_R)_s = 109.375 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ship's frictional resistance:**

First, calculate model Reynolds number at $V_m = 2.5$ m/s, $L_m = 4$ m, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s:

$$R_{e_m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{2.5 \times 4}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.96 \times 10^6$$

Ship Reynolds number at $V_s = 12.5$ m/s, $L_s = 100$ m:

$$R_{e_s} = \frac{V_s L_s}{\nu} = \frac{12.5 \times 100}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.245 \times 10^9$$

Model frictional coefficient (ITTC-57):

$$C_{F_m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{e_m} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(9.96 \times 10^6) - 2)^2}$$

$$\log_{10}(9.96 \times 10^6) = 6.998 \approx 7$$

$$C_{F_m} = \frac{0.075}{(7 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{25} = 0.003$$

Ship frictional coefficient:

$$C_{F_s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.245 \times 10^9) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.095 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.095)^2}$$

$$C_{F_s} = \frac{0.075}{50.34} = 0.001490$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(R_F)_s$:**

$$(R_F)_s = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_s^2 S_s C_{F_s}$$

We need $S_m$ to proceed; assume the problem gives $S_m = 1.5$ m². Then $S_s = 1.5 \times 625 = 937.5$ m².

$$(R_F)_s = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (12.5)^2 \times 937.5 \times 0.001490$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1000 \times 156.25 \times 937.5 \times 0.001490$$

$$= 78.125 \times 937.5 \times 0.001490$$

$$= 73242.19 \times 0.001490$$

$$\boxed{(R_F)_s \approx 109.13 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Total ship resistance:**

$$R_{T_s} = (R_F)_s + (R_R)_s = 109.13 + 109.375$$

$$\boxed{R_{T_s} \approx 218.5 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### Part (d) — Comment on the Two Speed Predictions

The Froude-predicted speed (12.5 m/s) is **125× larger** than the Reynolds-predicted speed (0.1 m/s). This dramatic gap is the **Froude–Reynolds conflict** — both laws cannot be satisfied simultaneously in a water tank. The standard naval-architecture practice (ITTC-1978) is to:
- Use **Froude scaling** for the **residual (wave-making) resistance** — as in part (c).
- Use **Reynolds scaling** (via ITTC-57) for the **frictional resistance coefficient** — as in step 3 of part (c).
- Sum the two components for the final ship resistance.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Model-to-Ship Extrapolation Workflow (ITTC Procedure)

The following block diagram captures the full model-test-to-ship-prediction pipeline, illustrating how Newton's, Froude's, and Reynold's laws are operationalised in practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Geometry Defined] --> B[Geometrically Similar Model Built - Scale Lambda]
    B --> C[Towing Tank Test at Model Speed Vm]
    C --> D[Measure Total Resistance RT_m]
    D --> E[Compute Frictional Resistance RF_m via ITTC-57]
    E --> F[Compute Residual Resistance RR_m = RT_m - RF_m]
    F --> G{Apply Froude Law of Comparison}
    G --> H[Scale Residual: RR_s = RR_m x Lambda cubed]
    H --> I[Compute Ship Speed: Vs = Vm x sqrt Lambda]
    I --> J[Compute Ship Re: Re_s = Vs Ls / nu]
    J --> K[Compute Ship CF via ITTC-57]
    K --> L[Apply Reynold's Law via CF scaling]
    L --> M[Compute Ship Frictional: RF_s = 0.5 rho Vs^2 Ss CF_s]
    M --> N[Total Ship Resistance: RT_s = RF_s + RR_s]
    N --> O[Effective Power: PE = RT_s x Vs]
    O --> P[Ship Performance Report]

    Q[Newton's Law of Dynamic Similitude] -. "Theoretical Ideal: All Force Ratios Match" .- G
    R[Froude Number Fr = V over sqrt gL] -. "Used for RR scaling" .- G
    S[Reynolds Number Re = VL over nu] -. "Used for CF scaling" .- L
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows represent the operational data flow of the ITTC-1978 extrapolation method. Dashed arrows indicate *theoretical* inputs (Newton's, Froude's, Reynolds's numbers) that govern the scaling steps.

---

## 4.2 Comparison of the Three Laws — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Newtons Law of Similitude]
        A1[Geometric Similarity] --> A2[Kinematic Similarity]
        A2 --> A3[Dynamic Similarity]
        A3 --> A4[All Force Ratios Equal]
    end

    subgraph B[Froudes Law of Comparison]
        B1[Free Surface Flow] --> B2[Froude Number Fr_m = Fr_s]
        B2 --> B3[Wave Pattern Preserved]
        B3 --> B4[Scaling for Residual Resistance]
    end

    subgraph C[Reynolds Law of Comparison]
        C1[Viscous Dominated Flow] --> C2[Reynolds Number Re_m = Re_s]
        C2 --> C3[Boundary Layer Preserved]
        C3 --> C4[Scaling for Frictional Resistance]
    end

    A -. "Specialized to" .-> B
    A -. "Specialized to" .-> C
    B -. "Combined with" .-> D[ITTC 1978 Extrapolation Procedure]
    C -. "Combined with" .-> D
    D --> E[Full-Scale Ship Resistance Prediction]
```

> **Key Takeaway from the Diagram:** Newton's law is the *parent* (theoretical) law, and Froude's and Reynolds's laws are *children* (practical specializations) that relax Newton's requirement of matching *all* force ratios and instead focus on the *dominant* force in each resistance component.

---

## 4.3 Force-Decomposition Flow (Why We Need Two Laws)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resistance RT] --> B[Frictional Resistance RF]
    A --> C[Residual Resistance RR]
    B --> B1[Dominated by Viscous Forces]
    B --> B2[Use Reynolds Law]
    C --> C1[Dominated by Gravitational Forces]
    C --> C2[Use Froude Law]
    B1 --> B3[Scale via CF from ITTC-57]
    B2 --> B4[Scale via Lambda cubed]
    C1 --> B3
    C2 --> B4
    B3 --> D[Combine: RT_s = RF_s + RR_s]
    B4 --> D
```
