# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. State Newton's Law of Dynamic Similarity. Why is it impractical to satisfy in ship model testing?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Newton's Law of Dynamic Similarity states that for two geometrically and kinematically similar systems, the **ratios of all types of forces** (inertial, viscous, gravitational, surface tension) acting on corresponding fluid elements must be equal. Equivalently, all relevant dimensionless numbers (Froude, Reynolds, Weber, Mach) must match between model and prototype.
>
> In ship model testing it is **impractical** because:
> 1. The model and ship use the same fluid (water), so viscosity cannot be independently scaled.
> 2. To match Froude number, the model speed must satisfy $V_m = V_s \sqrt{L_m/L_s}$. To *simultaneously* match Reynolds number, the same condition would require $\nu_s/\nu_m = (L_s/L_m)^{3/2}$, which is impossible in fresh/sea water.
> 3. Surface tension and other minor forces cannot be matched at typical model scales.
>
> Hence, only **partial similarity** is achievable.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Froude's Law and Reynolds' Law of Comparison. State the key dimensionless number preserved in each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Froude's Law | Reynolds' Law |
> |---|---|---|
> | Dominant force | Gravitational (wave-making) | Viscous (frictional) |
> | Preserved number | Froude number $\;F_r = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Reynolds number $\;R_e = VL/\nu$ |
> | Applied to | Residual resistance $R_R$ | Frictional resistance $R_F$ |
> | Speed scaling | $V_s = V_m \sqrt{L_s/L_m}$ | $V_s = V_m (L_m/L_s)(\nu_s/\nu_m)$ |
> | Use | Free-surface (ship) flows | Fully submerged / boundary-layer flows |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 (Module 2 Internal Choice A)

> **(a)** Derive the **Froude speed-scaling** and **residual resistance scaling** equations for a geometrically similar ship-model pair tested in the same fluid. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A ship model of length **3 m** is tested in a towing tank at a speed of **2.0 m/s**. The measured total resistance is **12 N** and the model's frictional resistance (by ITTC-57) is **7 N**. The ship is **75 m** long. Calculate:
> 1. The corresponding ship speed.
> 2. The residual resistance of the ship.
> 3. The total frictional resistance of the ship (assume $S_m = 1.2$ m²).
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Froude similarity condition:**
$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply and cancel $g$:**
$$V_s^2 L_m = V_m^2 L_s$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_s$:**
$$\boxed{V_s = V_m \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}}}$$

**Step 4 — Resistance proportionality:**
For a residual resistance dominated by inertial and gravitational effects, $R_R \propto \rho V^2 L^2$.

**Step 5 — Form the ratio:**
$$\frac{(R_R)_s}{(R_R)_m} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Froude speed relation $(V_s/V_m)^2 = L_s/L_m$:**
$$\frac{(R_R)_s}{(R_R)_m} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3$$

**Step 7 — For the same fluid** ($\rho_s = \rho_m$):
$$\boxed{(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^3}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $L_m = 3$ m, $\;L_s = 75$ m, $\;V_m = 2.0$ m/s, $\;R_{T_m} = 12$ N, $\;(R_F)_m = 7$ N, $\;S_m = 1.2$ m², $\;\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\;\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

**Step 1 — Model residual resistance:**
$$(R_R)_m = R_{T_m} - (R_F)_m = 12 - 7 = 5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Scale ratio $\lambda = L_s / L_m = 75 / 3 = 25$**

**Step 3 — Ship speed (Froude):**
$$V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda} = 2.0 \times \sqrt{25} = 2.0 \times 5 = \boxed{10.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Ship residual resistance:**
$$(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m \cdot \lambda^3 = 5 \times (25)^3 = 5 \times 15625$$
$$\boxed{(R_R)_s = 78\,125 \text{ N} = 78.125 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Model Reynolds number:**
$$R_{e_m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{2.0 \times 3}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.976 \times 10^6$$

**Step 6 — Model frictional coefficient (ITTC-57):**
$$C_{F_m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(5.976 \times 10^6) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(5.976) + 6 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(0.7765 + 4)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.7765)^2}$$
$$C_{F_m} = \frac{0.075}{22.815} = 0.003288$$

**Step 7 — Ship Reynolds number:**
$$R_{e_s} = \frac{V_s L_s}{\nu} = \frac{10 \times 75}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.47 \times 10^8$$

**Step 8 — Ship frictional coefficient:**
$$C_{F_s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(7.47 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(7.47) + 8 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(0.8733 + 6)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.8733)^2}$$
$$C_{F_s} = \frac{0.075}{47.243} = 0.001588$$

**Step 9 — Ship wetted area:**
$$S_s = S_m \cdot \lambda^2 = 1.2 \times (25)^2 = 1.2 \times 625 = 750 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 10 — Ship frictional resistance:**
$$(R_F)_s = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_s^2 S_s C_{F_s} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (10)^2 \times 750 \times 0.001588$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1000 \times 100 \times 750 \times 0.001588$$
$$= 50\,000 \times 750 \times 0.001588$$
$$= 3.75 \times 10^7 \times 0.001588$$
$$\boxed{(R_F)_s = 59\,550 \text{ N} \approx 59.55 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (Module 2 Internal Choice B)

> **(a)** State and explain **Reynolds' Law of Comparison**. Derive the corresponding **speed-scaling** relation. Discuss why Reynolds' law alone is insufficient for ship model testing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A model of length **5 m** is towed at **3.0 m/s** in fresh water ($\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The ship has length **125 m** and is to operate in sea water ($\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s).
> 1. Calculate the model Reynolds number.
> 2. Determine the equivalent ship speed for **Reynolds similarity**.
> 3. If Froude similarity were imposed instead, what would the ship speed be?
> 4. Calculate the percentage difference between the two speed predictions.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Statement:** Reynolds' Law of Comparison states that for flows dominated by **viscous forces**, dynamic similarity is achieved when the **Reynolds number** of the model equals that of the prototype:

$$(R_e)_m = (R_e)_s \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_s L_s}{\nu_s}$$

**Derivation of speed scaling:**

Rearranging the equality:

$$V_s = V_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{\nu_s}{\nu_m}$$

**Why insufficient for ship testing:**
1. Ships operate at a free surface — the **Froude number** governs wave-making, not Reynolds.
2. A geometrically scaled model run in water at Reynolds-similar speed produces *wrong wave patterns* (the wave-resistance component is misrepresented).
3. Hence, **both** Froude (for residual) and Reynolds (for frictional) laws must be applied in a *combined* procedure (ITTC-1978).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $L_m = 5$ m, $\;L_s = 125$ m, $\;V_m = 3.0$ m/s, $\;\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\;\nu_s = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

**Step 1 — Model Reynolds number:**
$$R_{e_m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{3.0 \times 5}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5 \times 10^7$$

$$\boxed{R_{e_m} = 1.5 \times 10^7}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds-similar ship speed:**
$$V_s^{Re} = V_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{\nu_s}{\nu_m} = 3.0 \times \frac{5}{125} \times \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= 3.0 \times 0.04 \times 1.05$$

$$\boxed{V_s^{Re} = 0.126 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Froude-similar ship speed:**
$$V_s^{Fr} = V_m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}} = 3.0 \times \sqrt{\frac{125}{5}} = 3.0 \times \sqrt{25} = 3.0 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{V_s^{Fr} = 15.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage difference:**

$$\Delta V = V_s^{Fr} - V_s^{Re} = 15.0 - 0.126 = 14.874 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\%\,\text{Difference} = \frac{14.874}{15.0} \times 100 \approx 99.16\%$$

$$\boxed{\%\,\text{Difference} \approx 99.16\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The Froude and Reynolds predictions differ by nearly **two orders of magnitude**. This vividly illustrates the **Froude–Reynolds conflict** and why a *combined* ITTC procedure is essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
>
> 1. **Mixing Model & Ship Reynolds Numbers:** Using $R_{e_m}$ in the ITTC-57 formula to get $(R_F)_s$. Correct: use $R_{e_s}$ (the *ship's* Reynolds number). *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 2. **Forgetting the $\rho_s/\rho_m$ Factor:** When model and ship use *different* fluids, the residual resistance scales as $\rho_s/\rho_m \cdot \lambda^3$, NOT just $\lambda^3$. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Wetted Area Confusion:** Wetted area scales as $L^2$, not $L^3$. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 4. **Skipping the $g$-Cancellation Step:** Examiners want to see the explicit cancellation of $g$ in Froude-speed derivations. *(Lose 0.5 mark for brevity.)*
> 5. **Stating "Reynolds Law is not applicable to ships":** It *is* applicable — just to the frictional component, not the total resistance. *(Lose 1 mark for over-generalisation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **Newton's Law** = *theoretical* law demanding ALL force ratios (Re, Fr, We, Ma) match — practically impossible for ships.
- ✅ **Froude's Law** applies to **wave-making / residual resistance**; preserved quantity is the **Froude Number** $F_r = V/\sqrt{gL}$.
- ✅ **Reynolds' Law** applies to **frictional resistance**; preserved quantity is the **Reynolds Number** $R_e = VL/\nu$.
- ✅ **Speed scaling (Froude):** $V_s = V_m \sqrt{L_s/L_m}$ — ship goes faster in absolute terms.
- ✅ **Speed scaling (Reynolds):** $V_s = V_m (L_m/L_s)(\nu_s/\nu_m)$ — ship goes slower in absolute terms.
- ✅ **Residual resistance scaling:** $(R_R)_s = (R_R)_m (\rho_s/\rho_m)(L_s/L_m)^3$.
- ✅ **Frictional resistance** uses the **ITTC-57** formula: $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_e - 2)^2$.
- ✅ **Wetted area** scales as $L^2$; **resistance** as $L^3$ (with Froude) or $L^2$ (with velocity held).
- ✅ **Total resistance** = frictional + residual: $R_T = R_F + R_R$.
- ✅ **ITTC-1978 Procedure** combines both laws: Froude for $R_R$, Reynolds-based $C_F$ (ITTC-57) for $R_F$.
- ✅ **Froude–Reynolds conflict** is the fundamental reason partial-similarity laws exist.
- ✅ For a **same-fluid** test, $\rho_s/\rho_m = 1$ and the resistance scaling reduces to the pure $\lambda^3$ form.
- ✅ **Kinetics first, then kinematics** — always start with force ratio analysis, then derive geometric and kinematic consequences.

> **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a question says *"in the same fluid"*, immediately set $\rho_s/\rho_m = 1$ and $\nu_s/\nu_m = 1$ to simplify the algebra. This is the most common exam scenario and a guaranteed way to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model-Ship Correlation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model-Ship Correlation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model-Ship Correlation (MSC)** is the systematic engineering procedure used to predict the resistance, powering, and propulsive performance of a full-scale ship from the measured resistance of a geometrically similar model towed in a towing tank or similar facility. It is fundamentally a *scaling problem* — because no single dimensionless group (such as Froude number $F_n$ alone) governs ship resistance, the Reynolds number $R_n$ and Froude number cannot be matched simultaneously between model and ship. Hence, the model data must be *correlated* (i.e., adjusted, corrected, and extrapolated) using empirically derived and theoretically grounded correction factors before it can represent the ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Model-Ship Correlation is the *bridge* between model basin experiments and full-scale ship design. Without correlation, raw model data is meaningless for predicting ship powering. The ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method is the universally accepted benchmark.

Mathematically, the correlation is expressed through the **residual resistance coefficient** or the **total resistance coefficient decomposition**:

$$
C_T = C_F + C_R + C_{APP} + C_{AA}
$$

where each term represents a physically distinct contribution to total drag.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Twin Cities Problem"

Imagine you have a miniature exact replica of a city (the *model*) and the real city (the *ship*). You measure how much fuel a tiny toy bus uses on a tiny road in the miniature. Clearly, you cannot *directly* scale the fuel consumption linearly — because in the toy city, air behaves as a thick syrup (low Reynolds number, viscous-dominated), while in the real city, air flows turbulently around the bus (high Reynolds number, inertia-dominated). 

The solution? You must **separate** the friction (viscous) part — which scales with Reynolds number — from the shape/wave part — which scales with Froude number — and recombine them at the ship scale using different scaling laws.

This is exactly what Model-Ship Correlation does:
- **Friction** → scaled by **Reynolds Number** ($R_n$)
- **Waves & Shape** → scaled by **Froude Number** ($F_n$)
- **Air & Appendages** → applied as **additive corrections**

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of fresh water (tank):** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of sea water (ship):** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water:** $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea water:** $\nu \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Standard ITTC form factor benchmark:** $k = 0.15$ (for modern slender forms)
- **ITTC 1978 line constant:** $C = 0.075$ (in $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-scale problem:** Because gravity (wave-making) and viscosity (friction) cannot be matched simultaneously, the correlation procedure *separates* the total resistance into viscous and wave components — each scaled by its own governing dimensionless number.

## 1.4 Why Model-Ship Correlation is Indispensable

A ship is too large, too expensive, and too slow to iterate designs on. Towing tanks let engineers test ~3–10 m long models cheaply and rapidly. However, a model test conducted at model Reynolds number would give *grossly incorrect* friction resistance if directly scaled to ship length. **MSC converts model data into ship data** with engineering-grade accuracy (typically ±5–10% for calm-water resistance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Reynolds & Froude Independence

Ship total resistance $R_T$ consists of two dominant physical components:
1. **Viscous resistance** $R_V$ (frictional + form/viscous-pressure) → governed by **Reynolds number** $R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu}$
2. **Wave-making resistance** $R_W$ → governed by **Froude number** $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$

Because gravitational and viscous forces have different scaling laws, the **ITTC assumes additive decomposition** (an approximation, but excellent in engineering practice):

$$
R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP} + R_{AA}
$$

where:
- $R_F$ = frictional resistance (purely frictional, on an equivalent flat plate)
- $R_R$ = residual resistance (wave-making + viscous-pressure form effects)
- $R_{APP}$ = appendage resistance (rudder, bilge keels, shaft brackets)
- $R_{AA}$ = air resistance (above-water hull, superstructure)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of the Correlation Procedure

**Step 1 — Model test** is conducted at the same Froude number as the ship ($F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$). This ensures dynamic similarity of the wave pattern.

**Step 2 — Total model resistance** $R_{T,m}$ is measured and non-dimensionalized to $C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^2 S_m}$.

**Step 3 — Frictional component** of model is computed using the **ITTC 1978 friction line** for the *model's* Reynolds number $R_{n,m}$:

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,m} - 2)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Form factor (k)** is determined from a separate *provisional form factor test* (Prohaska method or standard value $k = 0.15$). The viscous resistance coefficient of the model is:

$$
C_{V,m} = (1 + k) \cdot C_{F,m}
$$

**Step 5 — Residual resistance coefficient** of model is calculated as:

$$
C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - (1+k) C_{F,m}
$$

**Step 6 — Froude scaling assumption** states that residual resistance coefficient (wave-dominated) transfers directly from model to ship:

$$
C_{R,s} = C_{R,m}
$$

**Step 7 — Ship frictional coefficient** is computed at the *ship's* Reynolds number $R_{n,s}$:

$$
C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,s} - 2)^2}
$$

**Step 8 — Ship total resistance coefficient** is reconstructed:

$$
C_{T,s} = C_{F,s} + C_R + C_{AA} + C_{APP}
$$

**Step 9 — Air and appendage corrections** are added as percentage allowances or empirical correlations.

**Step 10 — Effective power** is finally computed as:

$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V_s
$$

## 2.3 The ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method (Detailed)

The ITTC 1978 method is the international standard. It assumes:
- Froude similarity ($F_n$ matched)
- Friction line is a flat-plate turbulent boundary-layer line (Schönherr or ITTC)
- Form factor is constant between model and ship
- Air and appendage resistance is additive

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Key Units |
|---|---------------------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number (wave scaling) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_n = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (viscous scaling) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Total resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 1978 friction line | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_V = (1+k)\,C_F$ | Viscous resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_R = C_T - C_V$ | Residual resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{T,s} = (1+k)\,C_{F,s} + C_R + \Delta C_F + C_{AA} + C_{APP}$ | Ship total resistance (ITTC 1978) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta C_F$ | Roughness allowance (correl. factor) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\lambda = L_s / L_m$ | Geometric scale ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Ship speed from Froude similarity | m/s |
| 11 | $R_{T,s} = C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$ | Ship total resistance | N |
| 12 | $P_E = R_{T,s} \cdot V_s$ | Effective power | W |
| 13 | $P_{APP} = (1.5 - 2.0)\% \text{ of } P_E$ | Appendage power allowance | W |
| 14 | $P_{AA} = 5\% \text{ of } P_E$ | Air resistance power allowance | W |
| 15 | $k = 0.15$ (typical slender form) | Standard form factor | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the $(1+k)$ multiplier when reconstructing $C_{T,s}$. The *form factor* must be applied on the **ship's** $C_{F,s}$, not the model's $C_{F,m}$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World Fields

Model-Ship Correlation is used by:
- **Naval architects** for powering predictions during ship design
- **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) for contract speed guarantees
- **Propulsion engineers** for selecting engines, gearboxes, propellers
- **Research institutions** for validating CFD codes against benchmark data
- **Model basins** (HSVA Hamburg, MARIN Wageningen, IIHR Iowa, NMRI Tokyo) for producing performance data

Without MSC, the entire modern ship design workflow (design → contract → build → sea trial) would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Model Test to Ship Resistance — Full ITTC 1978 Chain

### Given (Sample KTU-style Problem Setup):

A 6 m long model of a 180 m ship is tested in fresh water at 2.4 m/s. The measured model resistance is 24.5 N. The wetted surface area of the model is 1.42 m². Form factor $k = 0.15$. Compute the ship's effective power at the corresponding ship speed. Assume:
- Ship wetted surface (with appendages): $S_s = 5800 \text{ m}^2$
- $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (fresh water)
- $\nu_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (sea water)
- $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Appendage allowance: $C_{APP} = 0.0004$
- Air allowance: $C_{AA} = 0.0006$

### Step 1: Compute the Scale Ratio

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_s}{L_m} = \frac{180}{6} = 30
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Ship Speed (Froude Similarity)

$$
V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda} = 2.4 \times \sqrt{30} = 2.4 \times 5.477 = 13.146 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Model Reynolds Number

$$
R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{2.4 \times 6}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.44 \times 10^{7}
$$

### Step 4: Compute ITTC 1978 Friction Coefficient for Model

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.44 \times 10^{7}) - 2)^2}
$$

Now, $\log_{10}(1.44 \times 10^7) = 7.1584$, so:

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(7.1584 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(5.1584)^2} = \frac{0.075}{26.609} = 2.819 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Model Total Resistance Coefficient

$$
C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{24.5}{0.5 \times 1000 \times (2.4)^2 \times 1.42}
$$

$$
C_{T,m} = \frac{24.5}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 5.76 \times 1.42} = \frac{24.5}{4089.6} = 5.991 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 6: Compute Model Viscous Resistance Coefficient

$$
C_{V,m} = (1+k)\,C_{F,m} = (1.15) \times 2.819 \times 10^{-3} = 3.242 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 7: Extract Model Residual Resistance Coefficient

$$
C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - C_{V,m} = 5.991 \times 10^{-3} - 3.242 \times 10^{-3} = 2.749 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 8: Assume Froude Similarity Transfers $C_R$ to Ship

$$
C_{R,s} = C_{R,m} = 2.749 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 9: Compute Ship Reynolds Number

$$
R_{n,s} = \frac{V_s L_s}{\nu_s} = \frac{13.146 \times 180}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.973 \times 10^{9}
$$

### Step 10: Compute ITTC 1978 Friction Coefficient for Ship

$$
C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.973 \times 10^{9}) - 2)^2}
$$

Now, $\log_{10}(1.973 \times 10^9) = 9.2952$, so:

$$
C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(9.2952 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.2952)^2} = \frac{0.075}{53.219} = 1.409 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 11: Add Roughness Allowance $\Delta C_F$

For a ship with typical paint roughness and service life (~1 year), the standard ITTC roughness allowance is:

$$
\Delta C_F = \left[ \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,s} - 2)^2} - \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,s} - 2)^2} \right]_{\text{roughness}}
$$

A commonly used approximate value (from ITTC standard tables) for $R_{n,s} \approx 2 \times 10^9$:

$$
\Delta C_F \approx 0.40 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 12: Reconstruct Ship Total Resistance Coefficient

$$
C_{T,s} = (1+k)\,C_{F,s} + C_R + \Delta C_F + C_{AA} + C_{APP}
$$

$$
C_{T,s} = (1.15)(1.409 \times 10^{-3}) + 2.749 \times 10^{-3} + 0.40 \times 10^{-3} + 0.6 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_{T,s} = 1.620 \times 10^{-3} + 2.749 \times 10^{-3} + 0.40 \times 10^{-3} + 0.60 \times 10^{-3} + 0.40 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_{T,s} = 5.769 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 13: Compute Ship Total Resistance

$$
R_{T,s} = C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s
$$

$$
R_{T,s} = 5.769 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times (13.146)^2 \times 5800
$$

$$
R_{T,s} = 5.769 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 172.82 \times 5800
$$

$$
R_{T,s} = 5.769 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.137 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
R_{T,s} \approx 2.964 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

### Step 14: Compute Effective Power

$$
P_E = R_{T,s} \cdot V_s = 2.964 \times 10^{6} \times 13.146 \approx 3.897 \times 10^{7} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_E \approx 38.97 \text{ MW} \approx 52{,}250 \text{ HP (metric)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Form Factor $k$ via Prohaska Method

The Prohaska method derives $k$ from two model tests at the **same Froude number** but different speeds. It is the classical *slope method*:

Plot $C_T$ vs $C_F$. The slope of the resulting straight line (at constant $F_n$) yields $1+k$:

$$
\frac{C_{T}}{C_F} = (1+k) + \frac{C_R}{C_F}
$$

If two speeds are tested, the line slope gives $1+k$ directly. The ITTC recommends running tests at multiple speeds and performing least-squares regression.

## 3.3 Summary of the Analytical Matrix

| Stage | Quantity | Equation |
|-------|----------|----------|
| Input | $V_m, L_m, L_s, R_{T,m}, S_m, k$ | — |
| Scale | $V_s = V_m\sqrt{\lambda}$ | Froude similarity |
| Model friction | $C_{F,m}$ | ITTC 1978 line |
| Model viscous | $C_{V,m} = (1+k)C_{F,m}$ | Form factor |
| Model residual | $C_R = C_{T,m} - C_{V,m}$ | Residual extraction |
| Ship friction | $C_{F,s}$ | ITTC 1978 at $R_{n,s}$ |
| Ship total | $C_{T,s}$ | ITTC 1978 reconstruction |
| Ship resistance | $R_{T,s}$ | $C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$ |
| Effective power | $P_E$ | $R_{T,s} \cdot V_s$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Model-Ship Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Model Test Data V_m, L_m, R_Tm, S_m] --> B[Compute Scale Ratio lambda = L_s divided by L_m]
    B --> C[Compute Ship Speed V_s = V_m times sqrt of lambda]
    C --> D[Compute Model Reynolds Number R_nm]
    D --> E[Compute ITTC 1978 Model Friction C_Fm]
    E --> F[Apply Form Factor C_Vm = 1+k times C_Fm]
    F --> G[Extract Residual Coefficient C_R = C_Tm minus C_Vm]
    G --> H[Froude Similarity Assumption: C_Rs = C_Rm]
    H --> I[Compute Ship Reynolds Number R_ns]
    I --> J[Compute ITTC 1978 Ship Friction C_Fs]
    J --> K[Add Roughness Allowance deltaC_F]
    K --> L[Add Air Allowance C_AA]
    L --> M[Add Appendage Allowance C_APP]
    M --> N[Reconstruct Ship Total C_Ts]
    N --> O[Compute Ship Total Resistance R_Ts]
    O --> P[Compute Effective Power P_E]
```

## 4.2 Resistance Decomposition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RT[Total Resistance R_T] --> RF[Frictional R_F]
    RT --> RR[Residual R_R]
    RT --> RAPP[Appendage R_APP]
    RT --> RAA[Air R_AA]
    RF --> CFSHIP[C_F at Ship R_n]
    RF --> K[Form Factor 1+k]
    RR --> FR[Froude Scaling F_n constant]
    RAPP --> SUM[Add at Ship Scale]
    RAA --> SUM
    CFSHIP --> SUM
    K --> SUM
    FR --> SUM
    SUM --> CT_S[Ship C_T]
    CT_S --> POW[Effective Power]
```

## 4.3 Iterative Procedure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Receive Model Test Data]) --> step1[Step 1: Match F_n Model equals Ship]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Compute Model C_Fm]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Compute Model C_Vm = 1+k times C_Fm]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Compute Model C_R]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Apply Froude Sim: C_Rs equals C_Rm]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Compute Ship C_Fs]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Add Roughness Air Appendage]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Reconstruct C_Ts]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Compute R_Ts and P_E]
    step9 --> end([End: Ship Powering Prediction])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Explain in brief why Model-Ship Correlation is necessary. Mention the standard method recommended by ITTC.**

**Model Answer:**

Model-Ship Correlation (MSC) is necessary because the model and ship cannot be tested at the same Reynolds number (since the same fluid at the same kinematic viscosity is used), so viscous similarity cannot be achieved. Only Froude number (wave) similarity is maintained. Hence, the model test data must be *correlated* to ship conditions using separate Reynolds number scaling for friction and Froude scaling for waves.

The standard method recommended by ITTC is the **ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method**, which uses the formula:

$$
C_T = (1+k) \cdot C_F + C_R + \Delta C_F + C_{AA} + C_{APP}
$$

The form factor $k$ is typically taken as **0.15** for modern slender hull forms.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the significance of the ITTC 1978 friction line? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The ITTC 1978 friction line is an empirically derived equation representing the frictional resistance coefficient $C_F$ of a **turbulent flat plate** as a function of Reynolds number $R_n$. It serves as the reference baseline for separating frictional from residual (wave-making) resistance in model-ship correlation.

The formula is:

$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}
$$

It allows the engineer to compute the friction resistance of both model and ship using the *same empirical line* but at their respective Reynolds numbers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the ITTC 1978 model-ship correlation procedure step-by-step. List all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5 m model of a 100 m ship is tested at 2.0 m/s in fresh water. The measured resistance is 14.8 N. Model wetted surface is 1.20 m². Form factor $k = 0.16$. The ship wetted surface (with appendages) is 3200 m². Use:
- $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $\nu_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta C_F = 0.35 \times 10^{-3}$, $C_{AA} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$, $C_{APP} = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$

Calculate the ship's effective power. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Step-by-step derivation of ITTC 1978 Method:**

The ITTC 1978 method decomposes total ship resistance as:

$$
R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP} + R_{AA}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Froude similarity between model and ship ($F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$).
2. Viscous resistance scales with Reynolds number.
3. Residual resistance (waves + viscous-pressure) transfers with constant $C_R$.
4. Form factor $k$ is the same for model and ship.
5. Air and appendage resistance are additive.

**Steps:**
1. Run model test at $F_{n,s}$.
2. Compute $C_{T,m} = R_{T,m} / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^2 S_m)$.
3. Compute $C_{F,m}$ from ITTC 1978 line.
4. Compute $C_{V,m} = (1+k) C_{F,m}$.
5. Extract $C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - C_{V,m}$.
6. Compute $C_{F,s}$ from ITTC 1978 at ship $R_n$.
7. Reconstruct $C_{T,s} = (1+k)C_{F,s} + C_{R,m} + \Delta C_F + C_{AA} + C_{APP}$.
8. Compute $R_{T,s} = C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$.
9. Compute $P_E = R_{T,s} \cdot V_s$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Scale ratio $\lambda = 100/5 = 20$.

**Step 2:** Ship speed $V_s = 2.0 \sqrt{20} = 8.944 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 3:** Model Reynolds $R_{n,m} = 2.0 \times 5 / (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.0 \times 10^{7}$.

**Step 4:** Model friction $C_{F,m} = 0.075/(\log 10^7 - 2)^2 = 0.075/(5)^2 = 0.075/25 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 5:** Model viscous $C_{V,m} = 1.16 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 3.48 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 6:** Model $C_T = 14.8 / (0.5 \times 1000 \times 4 \times 1.2) = 14.8/2400 = 6.167 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 7:** Residual $C_R = 6.167 \times 10^{-3} - 3.48 \times 10^{-3} = 2.687 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 8:** Ship Reynolds $R_{n,s} = 8.944 \times 100 / (1.2 \times 10^{-6}) = 7.453 \times 10^{8}$.

**Step 9:** Ship friction $C_{F,s} = 0.075/(\log(7.453 \times 10^8) - 2)^2 = 0.075/(8.872 - 2)^2 = 0.075/(47.23) = 1.588 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 10:** $C_{T,s} = 1.16 \times 1.588 \times 10^{-3} + 2.687 \times 10^{-3} + 0.35 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$

$C_{T,s} = 1.842 \times 10^{-3} + 2.687 \times 10^{-3} + 0.35 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} = 5.779 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 11:** $R_{T,s} = 5.779 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times (8.944)^2 \times 3200$

$R_{T,s} = 5.779 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 79.99 \times 3200 = 5.779 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.312 \times 10^{8}$

$R_{T,s} \approx 7.585 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$

**Step 12:** $P_E = 7.585 \times 10^5 \times 8.944 \approx 6.784 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 6.78 \text{ MW}$.

---

### QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the role of Froude and Reynolds numbers in model-ship correlation. Why can't both be matched simultaneously? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A model of length 4 m representing a 200 m ship is tested at 1.8 m/s in fresh water. Model resistance is 9.2 N, wetted surface 0.95 m². Take $k = 0.15$, $\nu_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $S_s = 6500 \text{ m}^2$. Use $\Delta C_F = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$, $C_{AA} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$, $C_{APP} = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$. Find the effective power of the ship. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** Froude number $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ governs wave-making resistance (gravitational). Reynolds number $R_n = VL/\nu$ governs viscous resistance (frictional).

Since gravity and viscosity cannot both be matched using the same fluid in two scales:
- If we set $F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$, then $V_m = V_s/\sqrt{\lambda}$. For typical $\lambda = 30$, $V_m \approx V_s/5.5$.
- This forces $R_{n,m} \ll R_{n,s}$ (model Reynolds is much lower).
- Hence viscous similarity is sacrificed. The viscous (frictional) part must be *separated* and re-scaled.

The ITTC 1978 method reconciles this by assuming additive decomposition and applying Reynolds scaling only to the friction part.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$\lambda = 200/4 = 50$; $V_s = 1.8\sqrt{50} = 12.728 \text{ m/s}$.

$R_{n,m} = 1.8 \times 4 / 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^6$.

$C_{F,m} = 0.075/(\log(7.2\times 10^6)-2)^2 = 0.075/(4.857)^2 = 0.075/23.59 = 3.179 \times 10^{-3}$.

$C_V = 1.15 \times 3.179 \times 10^{-3} = 3.656 \times 10^{-3}$.

$C_{T,m} = 9.2/(0.5 \times 1000 \times 3.24 \times 0.95) = 9.2/1539 = 5.978 \times 10^{-3}$.

$C_R = 5.978 \times 10^{-3} - 3.656 \times 10^{-3} = 2.322 \times 10^{-3}$.

$R_{n,s} = 12.728 \times 200 / (1.2 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.121 \times 10^9$.

$C_{F,s} = 0.075/(\log(2.121 \times 10^9) - 2)^2 = 0.075/(9.327-2)^2 = 0.075/(53.62) = 1.399 \times 10^{-3}$.

$C_{T,s} = 1.15 \times 1.399 \times 10^{-3} + 2.322 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$

$= 1.609 \times 10^{-3} + 2.322 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} = 5.231 \times 10^{-3}$.

$R_{T,s} = 5.231 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times (12.728)^2 \times 6500$

$= 5.231 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 161.99 \times 6500$

$= 5.231 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.397 \times 10^{8} = 2.823 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$.

$P_E = 2.823 \times 10^6 \times 12.728 = 3.594 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \approx 35.94 \text{ MW}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the form factor multiplier $(1+k)$** on the ship's $C_F,s$ — leading to under-prediction of resistance.
> 2. **Wrong density assignment** — students sometimes use $\rho_m$ instead of $\rho_s$ when computing ship resistance. Always use **sea water density (1025 kg/m³)** for ship.
> 3. **Mixing up kinematic viscosity** — model uses **fresh water** ($\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$), ship uses **sea water** ($\nu \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$).
> 4. **Wrong formula for $V_s$** — speed conversion is $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda}$, not $V_s = V_m \cdot \lambda$.
> 5. **Forgetting roughness/appendage/air allowances** — these are mandatory in ITTC 1978 reconstruction.
> 6. **Unit errors** in final power: $P_E$ is in Watts if $R$ is in N and $V$ in m/s; convert to MW or HP if asked.
> 7. **Logarithm base confusion** — ITTC 1978 uses **base-10 logarithm** of $R_n$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Model-Ship Correlation** = bridge between model test and full-scale ship performance.
- **ITTC 1978 Method** is the international standard for MSC.
- **Froude number** governs **wave-making resistance** → matched between model and ship.
- **Reynolds number** governs **frictional resistance** → scaled separately using ITTC friction line.
- **ITTC 1978 friction line**: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$
- **Form factor** $k$ (typically 0.15) accounts for viscous-pressure form effects: $C_V = (1+k) C_F$.
- **Residual resistance** $C_R$ transfers via Froude similarity: $C_{R,s} = C_{R,m}$.
- **Ship reconstruction formula**: $C_{T,s} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_R + \Delta C_F + C_{AA} + C_{APP}$.
- **Speed conversion**: $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda}$.
- **Scale ratio**: $\lambda = L_s / L_m$.
- **Ship resistance**: $R_{T,s} = C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$.
- **Effective power**: $P_E = R_{T,s} \cdot V_s$.
- **Standard allowances**: roughness $\Delta C_F \approx 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$, air $C_{AA} \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$, appendage $C_{APP} \approx 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$.
- **Fresh water vs sea water**: $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\nu_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Why both $F_n$ and $R_n$ can't be matched**: because gravity and viscosity scale differently with length, and the same fluid must be used.
- **Limitations**: ITTC 1978 is an engineering approximation; advanced methods (CFD, EFD validation, hybrid RANS) refine accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Testing - Tank Testing Facilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Model Testing - Tank Testing Facilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Model Testing** is the experimental determination of hydrodynamic performance characteristics (resistance, propulsion, seakeeping, and maneuvering) of a ship using a geometrically similar, dynamically scaled model tested in a controlled water facility under conditions that satisfy the laws of similitude.

**Tank Testing Facilities** are the specialized hydrodynamic laboratories equipped with towing tanks, circulating water channels, seakeeping basins, and cavitation tunnels that enable accurate simulation of full-scale ship behavior using small-scale models.

The principal facility for ship model testing is the **Towing Tank** — a long, narrow, rectangular basin filled with fresh or salt water in which a ship model is towed (or self-propelled) by a carriage system while its hydrodynamic forces and motions are precisely measured.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Tank Testing Facilities

| Facility Type | Primary Application |
|---------------|---------------------|
| **Conventional Towing Tank** | Resistance, propulsion, wake survey |
| **Deep Water Towing Tank** | Deep-water resistance, slamming, wave breaking |
| **Shallow Water Towing Tank** | Bank-effect, squat, shallow-water resistance |
| **Circulating Water Channel** | Cavitation, propeller open-water, boundary layer |
| **Seakeeping / Maneuvering Basin** | Wave-induced motions, course stability |
| **Ice Tank** | Ice-resistance, icebreaking performance |
| **Cavitation Tunnel** | Cavitation observation on propellers |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wind Tunnel for Ships"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine you want to know whether a new aircraft design will fly safely before building it. You build a scaled-down model and place it inside a **wind tunnel** where wind simulates flight conditions. The ship's towing tank works on the same principle — but with water and gravity-driven waves instead of air.

A towing tank is essentially the **"wind tunnel of naval architecture."** Instead of generating artificial wind, the tank uses gravity and a precision towing carriage to drag a model ship through otherwise still water. The carriage moves at a programmed speed while onboard load cells measure the drag (resistance) the model experiences.

### 1.3 Why Ship Model Testing is Indispensable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Justification**: Despite advances in **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** and numerical methods like **RANS (Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes)** simulations, physical model testing remains the **gold standard** for ship resistance prediction because:
> - Captures real **turbulent boundary layer** physics
> - Accounts for **wave–wave interactions** at the free surface
> - Validates numerical predictions
> - Required for **class society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV)

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Density of fresh water at 20 °C: ρ = 998.2 kg/m³**
- **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at 20 °C: ν ≈ 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- **Standard gravitational acceleration: g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard towing tank width: 4 m to 12 m**
- **Standard tank length: 100 m to 400 m**
- **Standard test water depth: 2 m to 10 m**

> [!NOTE]
> Most KTU textbook problems (e.g., from *Papanikolaou's "Ship Design"*, *Larsen's "Principles of Naval Architecture"*) assume **fresh water at 15 °C–20 °C** unless specified otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Laws of Similitude — The Foundation of Model Testing

For the model to faithfully represent the full-scale ship, **three types of similarity** must be enforced:

1. **Geometric Similarity** — All linear dimensions scaled by ratio $\lambda = L_m/L_s$.
2. **Kinematic Similarity** — Streamline patterns and velocity ratios identical at corresponding points.
3. **Dynamic Similarity** — All relevant force ratios identical.

### 2.1.1 Froude's Law of Comparison (Gravity Dominance)

Ship wave-making resistance is governed primarily by **gravity**, so **Froude number similarity** must hold:

$$F_{nm} = F_{ns}$$

This gives the critical **model-to-ship ratios** for all kinematic and dynamic quantities:

- **Length ratio:** $\lambda = L_m / L_s$
- **Velocity ratio:** $V_m / V_s = \sqrt{\lambda}$
- **Time ratio:** $t_m / t_s = \sqrt{\lambda}$
- **Displacement ratio:** $\nabla_m / \nabla_s = \lambda^3$
- **Force ratio (same fluid):** $R_m / R_s = \lambda^3$
- **Power ratio (same fluid):** $P_m / P_s = \lambda^{3.5}$

### 2.1.2 Reynolds Number Scaling

For the same fluid (water), Reynolds number similarity **cannot be preserved simultaneously** with Froude similarity. The Reynolds number scales as:

$$\frac{R_{nm}}{R_{ns}} = \lambda^{1.5}$$

This is why **frictional resistance correction** (using the ITTC 1957 or ITTC 1978 correlation line) is mandatory.

## 2.2 The 3-Tank Resistance Approach (Holtrop-Mennen Outline)

> [!NOTE]
> Total ship resistance is decomposed into physically meaningful components, each estimated using a dedicated formula:
>
> 1. **Frictional Resistance ($R_F$)** — ITTC 1957 line: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$
> 2. **Form (Viscous Pressure) Resistance ($R_{PV}$)** — via the **form factor** $k$ derived from model testing
> 3. **Wave-Making Resistance ($R_W$)** — ITTC 1978 method or thin-ship theory

The resulting equation is:

$$R_T = R_F (1 + k) + R_W$$

where the form factor $k$ is **experimentally determined** from the model test by performing a **progressive resistance test** at varying Froude numbers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every formula** you need for Module 3 tank-testing numerical problems. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU exam paper.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $F_n = V / \sqrt{g L}$ | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ | Length scale ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Model velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $R_m / R_s = \lambda^3 \cdot (\rho_m / \rho_s)$ | Force ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_T = R / (0.5 \rho V^2 S)$ | Resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ | ITTC 1957 frictional line | dimensionless |
| 7 | $k = (C_T - C_F) / C_F$ | Form factor (extrapolated) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_T = C_F (1+k) + C_W + C_{AA}$ | ITTC 1978 total resistance | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{n} = V L / \nu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_P = R_{F,m}/(0.5\rho_m V_m^2 S_m)$ | Pressure (frictional) coeff. | dimensionless |
| 11 | $S_m = \lambda^2 S_s$ | Wetted area scaling | m² |
| 12 | $P_E = R_T \cdot V_s$ | Effective power | W (Watts) |
| 13 | $R_{F,s} = (C_{F,s} / C_{F,m}) \cdot R_{F,m} \cdot (\rho_s/\rho_m)$ | Frictional resistance scaling | N |
| 14 | $t_m = t_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Time scale | s |
| 15 | $\nabla_m = \lambda^3 \nabla_s$ | Volume displacement scaling | m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Trap**: When the model and ship use **different fluids** (e.g., paraffin wax model in fresh water), always include the **density ratio** $\rho_m / \rho_s$ in the force scaling. Most KTU problems assume **same-fluid (fresh water) testing** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The knowledge gained from tank testing facilities underpins:

- **Concept ship design** — prediction of EHP and required BHP before contract
- **Hull-form optimization** — testing parent / derivative geometries
- **Propeller design validation** — open-water and cavitation testing
- **Regulatory compliance** — class approval of novel hulls (e.g., WIG craft, SWATH, trimarans)
- **Slamming and green-water studies** — critical for naval combatants

## 2.5 Components of a Modern Towing Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Tank Infrastructure:**
> - **Main tank basin** — reinforced concrete, lined with epoxy or tile
> - **Towing carriage** — computer-controlled, span-wise and heave adjustable
> - **Secondary carriage** — for free-running models
> - **Sub-carriage** — for self-propulsion tests with rotating dynamometer
> - **Wave generator** — hinged flap or piston type, computer-actuated
> - **Wave absorber** — beach-type or active-absorption
> - **Force dynamometer** — strain-gauge type, 6-component capable
> - **Wake-survey probe** — 5-hole Pitot tube on traversing mechanism
> - **Data acquisition system** — high-speed DAQ, typically 100–1000 Hz

## 2.6 Major Towing Tank Facilities Worldwide

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Facilities** (KTU students should know at least 3–4):
> - **MARIN (Wageningen, Netherlands):** Deep-water tank 252 m × 4.2 m, 4–10 m depth
> - **HSVA (Hamburg, Germany):** 300 m long, 18 m wide, 6 m deep
> - **NSWCCD (USA):** David Taylor Model Basin — 968 ft long
> - **SRC (Sweden):** 400 m tank at Gothenburg
> - **NMRI (Japan):** 400 m tank at Nagasaki
> - **IIT Madras & IISc (India):** smaller 90–110 m tanks for academic use

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Froude's Law of Comparison from Dimensional Homogeneity

We begin with the **wave-making resistance** $R_W$ which depends on:

$$R_W = f(\rho, V, L, g)$$

Four physical variables in three fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) → by Buckingham's π theorem, one independent dimensionless group exists. We seek it.

**Step 1:** Set up the dimensional matrix.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_W &: [M L T^{-2}] \\
\rho &: [M L^{-3}] \\
V &: [L T^{-1}] \\
L &: [L] \\
g &: [L T^{-2}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Choose repeating variables $\rho$, $V$, $L$. Solve for the π-group containing $R_W$ and $g$ separately.

For $R_W$ (using only $\rho$, $V$, $L$ as repeating):
$$\pi_1 = R_W / (\rho V^2 L^2)$$

For $g$:
$$\pi_2 = g L / V^2$$

**Step 3:** Functional relation:
$$\pi_1 = \phi(\pi_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_W}{\rho V^2 L^2} = \phi\!\left(\frac{gL}{V^2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Recognition. Note that $\sqrt{gL/V^2} = V / \sqrt{gL} = F_n$. So:
$$C_W = \frac{R_W}{0.5 \rho V^2 S} = f(F_n)$$

**Step 5:** Model–ship similarity requires $F_{nm} = F_{ns}$:

$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

Solving:
$$\frac{V_m}{V_s} = \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = \sqrt{\lambda}$$

> This establishes the **velocity scaling law** — Step 5 is what examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Derivation: Force Scaling Law

We require $C_{Tm} = C_{Ts}$ for the same Froude number. From the definition of total resistance coefficient:

$$C_T = \frac{R_T}{0.5 \rho V^2 S}$$

Equating model and ship:
$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{0.5 \rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{R_{Ts}}{0.5 \rho_s V_s^2 S_s}$$

Substitute $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ and $S_m = \lambda^2 S_s$:

$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{\rho_m V_s^2 \lambda \cdot \lambda^2 S_s} = \frac{R_{Ts}}{\rho_s V_s^2 S_s}$$

$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{\rho_m \lambda^3 S_s V_s^2} = \frac{R_{Ts}}{\rho_s S_s V_s^2}$$

Cancel common terms:

$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{\rho_m \lambda^3} = \frac{R_{Ts}}{\rho_s}$$

Therefore:
$$R_{Tm} = \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \lambda^3 R_{Ts}$$

For the **same fluid** ($\rho_m = \rho_s$):

$$R_{Tm} = \lambda^3 R_{Ts} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R_{Ts} = \frac{R_{Tm}}{\lambda^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic**: $\lambda^3$ reflects that resistance is a 3-D quantity scaling with volume. The force scale is cubic in the linear scale — a fundamental result that students must memorize.

## 3.3 Derivation: ITTC 1978 Extrapolation of Form Factor

The ITTC 1978 method is the **industry standard** for full-scale resistance prediction. The procedure:

**Step 1:** Run the model test over a range of speeds and obtain $R_{Tm}(V_m)$ values.

**Step 2:** Compute model frictional resistance using ITTC 1957:
$$R_{Fm} = 0.5 \rho_m V_m^2 S_m \cdot C_{Fm}, \quad C_{Fm} = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_{nm} - 2)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract frictional from total and plot $(R_{Tm} - R_{Fm})$ vs $F_n$.

**Step 4:** Extrapolate this residual curve to **F_n = 0**. At F_n = 0, there is no wave-making resistance, so the residual equals $R_{PV,m}$ (viscous-pressure resistance).

**Step 5:** Compute the form factor:
$$k = \frac{R_{PV,m}}{R_{Fm}} \quad \text{(at } F_n = 0 \text{ extrapolation)}$$

**Step 6:** Compute full-scale frictional resistance using ship Reynolds number:
$$C_{Fs} = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_{ns} - 2)^2}, \quad R_{Fs} = 0.5 \rho_s V_s^2 S_s C_{Fs}$$

**Step 7:** Compute full-scale viscous resistance:
$$R_{Vs} = R_{Fs}(1 + k)$$

**Step 8:** Estimate residual resistance coefficient from the model plot:
$$C_R = \frac{R_{Tm} - R_{Fm}}{0.5 \rho_m V_m^2 S_m}$$

**Step 9:** Apply at full scale with a roughness allowance $C_{AA} = 0.0004$:

$$C_{Ts} = \frac{C_{Fs}(1+k) + C_R + C_{AA}}{\lambda \cdot (\rho_m/\rho_s)^{-1}} \quad \text{or directly as forces using } \lambda^3$$

$$\boxed{R_{Ts} = \lambda^3 \cdot \left[ 0.5 \rho_m V_m^2 S_m \left( C_{Fm}(1+k) + C_R + C_{AA} \right) \right] \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic**: Step 9 is where density and roughness effects enter. The roughness allowance $C_{AA} = 0.0004$ accounts for the **hull's surface degradation** over its service life (typically 5–10 years of fouling).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem**: A ship of length $L_s = 200$ m is to be tested at 1:25 scale in fresh water at 20 °C. If the ship's design speed is $V_s = 20$ knots, find:
> (a) Model test speed
> (b) Expected model resistance if the full-scale resistance is $R_{Ts} = 2000$ kN
> (c) Effective power at full scale

**Given:**
- $L_s = 200$ m, $\lambda = 1/25$, $V_s = 20$ knots
- 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s → $V_s = 10.288$ m/s
- $R_{Ts} = 2000$ kN = $2 \times 10^6$ N

**Solution:**

**(a)** $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 10.288 \times \sqrt{1/25} = 10.288 \times 0.2 = 2.0576$ m/s

**(b)** $R_{Tm} = \lambda^3 R_{Ts} = (1/25)^3 \times 2 \times 10^6 = (1/15625) \times 2 \times 10^6 = 128$ N

**(c)** $P_E = R_{Ts} \cdot V_s = 2 \times 10^6 \times 10.288 = 20.576$ MW

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight**: Notice that the model experiences only **128 N** — about the weight of 13 kg — yet this tiny force must be measured with high-precision load cells (typically 50–500 N range, ±0.1% accuracy).

## 3.5 Self-Propulsion Test (SHP) — Brief Theoretical Note

In a self-propulsion test, the model is fitted with a **scaled propeller** driven by a rotating dynamometer mounted on the sub-carriage. The test yields:

- **Self-propulsion point (SPP):** $R_{Tm,SPP}$ at design $F_n$
- **Thrust deduction factor:** $t = (T - R_{Tm,SPP})/T$
- **Wake fraction:** $w = (V_s - V_{model})/V_s$ from wake survey

These are essential for **full-scale power prediction** using the admiralty coefficient / ITTC 1978 powering method.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Towing Tank Facility

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **interacting subsystems** of a typical towing tank. Each block represents a major functional unit; arrows show information or physical flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Model Preparation Block] --> B[Geometric Scaling Block 1 lambda]
    B --> C[Instrumentation Calibration Block]
    C --> D[Towing Carriage Control Block]
    D --> E[Main Towing Tank Basin Block]
    E --> F[Wave Generator Block]
    E --> G[Wave Absorber Beach Block]
    E --> H[Dynamometer and Force Balance Block]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition System Block]
    I --> J[Signal Conditioning and Amplifier Block]
    J --> K[Real Time Visualization Block]
    I --> L[Post Processing and Analysis Block]
    L --> M[Form Factor k Extraction Block]
    L --> N[ITTC 1978 Extrapolation Block]
    N --> O[Full Scale Resistance Prediction Block]
    M --> N
    L --> P[Self Propulsion Point Analysis Block]
    P --> O
    F --> Q[Towing Tank Water Body]
    G --> Q
    A --> Q
    D --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The flow moves from **left (model preparation)** to **right (full-scale prediction)**. The central node group [E, F, G, Q] represents the physical tank; surrounding blocks represent measurement, control, and analytical subsystems.

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Verify Model Geometry and Surface Finish] --> S2[Step 2 Measure Model Displacement and WSA]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Calibrate Dynamometer and Pressure Probes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Establish Tank Water Temperature and Density]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Conduct Resistance Test at Varying Froude]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compute Total Resistance Coefficients C sub T]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Compute Frictional Coefficients via ITTC 1957]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Plot Residual Curve and Extrapolate to F sub n 0]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Determine Form Factor k]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Perform Wake Survey Traverse]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Conduct Self Propulsion Test with Rotating Dynamometer]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Apply ITTC 1978 Extrapolation to Full Scale]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13 Compute Effective Power EHP]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14 Deliver Model Test Report]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of Towing Tank (Block-Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BASIN[Towing Tank Basin]
        WATER[Fresh Water Body]
        MODEL[Ship Model at Test Position]
        CARRIAGE[Top Towing Carriage with Dynamometer]
        WAVE[Wave Generator Flap]
        BEACH[Wave Absorber Beach at Far End]
    end
    CARRIAGE -.tows.-> MODEL
    MODEL -.resistance.-> CARRIAGE
    WAVE -.generates.-> WATER
    WATER -.attenuates.-> BEACH
```

## 4.4 Instrumentation Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    L1[Load Cell Strain Gauge] --> L2[Amplifier Block]
    L2 --> L3[Low Pass Filter Block]

    L5 --> L6[Storage SSD Block]
    L5 --> L7[Real Time Display Monitor]
    L5 --> L8[Post Processing Software MATLAB or Python]
    PITOT[5 Hole Pitot Probe] --> TRAV[Traversing Mechanism]
    TRAV --> L5
    ENCODER[Carriage Position Encoder] --> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: Modern tanks use a **sampling rate of 100–200 Hz** for resistance tests and **1000 Hz** for slamming/seakeeping tests. Signal filtering removes carriage-induced vibrations.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three main components of a towing tank facility and state the function of each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Towing Carriage (3 Marks total):** A computer-controlled bridge that spans the tank width and tows the model at programmed speeds. Function: Provides controlled, repeatable motion of the model at precise speeds.
2. **Wave Generator:** Hinged-flap or piston-type wavemaker installed at one end. Function: Generates regular or irregular waves for seakeeping tests.
3. **Wave Absorber Beach:** Sloped, energy-dissipating surface at the opposite end. Function: Prevents wave reflection that would otherwise contaminate the test section.
4. **Force Dynamometer / Load Cell:** Strain-gauge-based multi-axis force transducer mounted on the carriage. Function: Measures drag, side force, and vertical force on the towed model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning**: Students often write "to generate waves" for the **wave generator** but fail to mention its **location** (end of tank). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing location. Always mention **position** in tank descriptions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**State the principle of Froude's law of comparison. Why is it used in ship model testing?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Froude's law of comparison states that **dynamic similarity between model and ship is achieved when their Froude numbers are equal** ($F_{nm} = F_{ns}$), because gravity is the dominant force governing wave-making resistance.

It is used because:
1. **Wave-making resistance** depends primarily on gravity; hence matching $F_n$ ensures geometrically similar wave systems.
2. It provides the scaling laws for **velocity, time, force, and power** between model and ship.
3. Combined with the **ITTC 1957 friction line** (Reynolds correction), it allows prediction of full-scale resistance from model tests.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall**: Students frequently confuse Froude scaling with Reynolds scaling. Froude scaling applies to **wave-making resistance** only; Reynolds scaling applies to **frictional resistance**. Examiners will explicitly deduct 1 mark for conflating the two.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **main components of a towing tank facility** used for ship resistance testing. Highlight the role of the towing carriage and the wave absorber. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 1:30 scale model of a ship is to be tested in fresh water at 20 °C. The full-scale ship length is 180 m and design speed is 18 knots. If the total resistance at full scale is 1.8 MN, determine: (i) the model test speed, (ii) the expected model resistance, (iii) the effective power at design speed. (7 Marks)

### **Model Answer — Question A(a)**

**Components of a towing tank facility:**

1. **Main basin (3 Marks):** Reinforced concrete channel typically 100–400 m long, 5–12 m wide, and 2–10 m deep. Lined with smooth epoxy or tile to minimize parasitic currents.

2. **Towing carriage (2 Marks):** Spans the tank width on rails; tows the model through the water at programmed speeds from 0.1 m/s up to 10 m/s with ±0.1% accuracy. Hosts the dynamometer and DAQ electronics.

3. **Wave generator and absorber (2 Marks):** Wave generator (piston or flap type) at one end produces regular or irregular wave spectra. Wave-absorber beach at the opposite end (slope 1:8 to 1:20 with perforated plates) dissipates wave energy to prevent reflection.

**Role of towing carriage:**
- Maintains precise, constant speed of the model.
- Carries the dynamometer that measures resistance forces.
- Houses the data acquisition system for synchronized recording.

**Role of wave absorber:**
- Prevents standing waves and reflection that would otherwise corrupt resistance measurements.
- Maintains a "clean" wave field in the test section.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A(b)**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 1/30$, $L_s = 180$ m, $V_s = 18$ knots
- $V_s = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.259$ m/s
- $R_{Ts} = 1.8 \times 10^6$ N

**(i) Model test speed:**
$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 9.259 \times \sqrt{1/30} = 9.259 \times 0.1826 = 1.691 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Expected model resistance (same fluid):**
$$R_{Tm} = \lambda^3 R_{Ts} = (1/30)^3 \times 1.8 \times 10^6$$
$$R_{Tm} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{27000} = 66.67 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Effective power at design speed:**
$$P_E = R_{Ts} \cdot V_s = 1.8 \times 10^6 \times 9.259 = 1.6666 \times 10^7 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_E \approx 16.67 \text{ MW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout**: A common error is using the **knot-to-m/s factor** incorrectly (some students use 0.514 m/s, others 1.944 knots → m/s). **Always use 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.** Examiners allocate only 1 mark for unit conversion; get the conversion right or lose those marks instantly.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **ITTC 1978 method** of extrapolating model resistance to full scale. State clearly how the form factor $k$ is determined from the model test. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A ship model of length 4 m is tested in a towing tank at a speed of 2.5 m/s. The measured total resistance is 80 N. The wetted surface area of the model is 6.5 m². Calculate (i) the Froude number, (ii) the total resistance coefficient $C_T$, and (iii) the form factor $k$ if $R_n = 5.0 \times 10^6$ and the corresponding ship $R_n = 4.0 \times 10^8$. (7 Marks)

### **Model Answer — Question B(a)**

**ITTC 1978 Method — Outline (4 Marks):**
The ITTC 1978 procedure predicts full-scale resistance by separating total resistance into frictional, form (viscous-pressure), and wave-making components. The model test yields $R_{Tm}(F_n)$; this is decomposed using the ITTC 1957 friction line $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$. The residual $R_{Tm} - R_{Fm}$ plotted against $F_n$ is extrapolated to $F_n = 0$ to obtain the form (viscous-pressure) resistance component, $R_{PV,m}$. The **form factor** is then:
$$k = R_{PV,m}/R_{Fm}$$

Full-scale resistance is then computed:
$$R_{Ts} = R_{Fs}(1+k) + R_R + R_{AA}$$

where $R_R$ is the residual resistance (from $C_R$ at design $F_n$) and $R_{AA}$ is the roughness allowance.

**Form Factor Determination (3 Marks):**
1. Conduct resistance test over a range of speeds.
2. Compute $R_{Fm}$ using ITTC 1957 friction line for each speed.
3. Plot $(R_{Tm} - R_{Fm})$ against $F_n$ — the curve is approximately linear at low speeds.
4. Extrapolate linearly to $F_n = 0$; intercept = $R_{PV,m}$.
5. Compute $k = R_{PV,m} / R_{Fm}$ at the extrapolated point.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B(b)**

**Given:**
- $L_m = 4$ m, $V_m = 2.5$ m/s, $R_{Tm} = 80$ N
- $S_m = 6.5$ m², $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- $R_{nm} = 5.0 \times 10^6$

**(i) Froude number of the model:**
$$F_{nm} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 4}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{39.24}} = \frac{2.5}{6.264} = 0.399$$

$$\boxed{F_{nm} \approx 0.40}$$

**(ii) Total resistance coefficient:**
$$C_T = \frac{R_{Tm}}{0.5 \rho V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{80}{0.5 \times 998 \times (2.5)^2 \times 6.5}$$
$$C_T = \frac{80}{0.5 \times 998 \times 6.25 \times 6.5} = \frac{80}{20268.75}$$
$$\boxed{C_T \approx 3.947 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**(iii) Form factor $k$:**

Compute $C_{Fm}$ using ITTC 1957:
$$C_{Fm} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(5.0 \times 10^6) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.699 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.699)^2} = \frac{0.075}{22.08}$$
$$C_{Fm} = 3.397 \times 10^{-3}$$

Now form factor:
$$k = \frac{C_T - C_{Fm}}{C_{Fm}} = \frac{3.947 \times 10^{-3} - 3.397 \times 10^{-3}}{3.397 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.550 \times 10^{-3}}{3.397 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$\boxed{k \approx 0.162}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Logarithm Base**: A frequent mistake is computing $\log_{10}(5 \times 10^6)$ as 6.699 (correct) but then **forgetting to subtract 2**, yielding $C_F$ that is too small. Always subtract 2 — this is the ITTC 1957 convention that uses $\log R_n - 2$. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this single error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Revision Checklist for Tank Testing Facilities:**
>
> - [ ] Tank testing relies on **Froude similarity** ($F_{nm} = F_{ns}$) because gravity dominates wave-making resistance.
> - [ ] **Velocity scaling:** $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$.
> - [ ] **Force scaling (same fluid):** $R_m = \lambda^3 R_s$.
> - [ ] **Power scaling:** $P_m = \lambda^{3.5} P_s$.
> - [ ] **Form factor $k$** is determined by extrapolating residual resistance to $F_n = 0$.
> - [ ] **ITTC 1957 line:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$.
> - [ ] **Roughness allowance:** $C_{AA} = 0.0004$ (standard for service-aged hull).
> - [ ] Towing tank minimum length for **F_n = 0.40** at 6 m model: $V = 9.7$ m/s — requires ≥150 m of run.
> - [ ] **Standard test fluid:** Fresh water at 20 °C, $\rho = 998.2$ kg/m³.
> - [ ] Carriage speed accuracy: typically ±0.1% of commanded speed.
> - [ ] Force dynamometer resolution: typically 0.05–0.1% of full scale.
> - [ ] Major facilities: MARIN (Netherlands), HSVA (Germany), NSWCCD (USA).
> - [ ] Self-propulsion test uses a **rotating dynamometer** to absorb propeller torque.
> - [ ] **Wake survey** uses a 5-hole Pitot tube traversed behind the model propeller disk.
> - [ ] Reynolds number scales as $\lambda^{1.5}$ — this **mismatch** is why frictional correction is needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Tip**: In KTU 2024 scheme papers, **Part B questions of Module 3** almost always combine a **descriptive component** (e.g., describe the facility) with a **numerical component** (e.g., compute model speed/resistance). Practice both — descriptive answers earn content marks while numerical answers earn process marks. Aim for **balanced 50–50 time allocation** between them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST402 - SHIP RESISTANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model Testing for Ship Resistance** is the experimental procedure in which a geometrically similar scaled-down model of a ship is towed through calm water in a controlled towing tank at varying speeds, and the total resistance experienced by the model is measured using calibrated dynamometers. The measured model resistance is then extrapolated to the full-scale (prototype) ship using dimensional analysis and the **Froude Similarity Law**, since it is impossible to achieve both Froude and Reynolds similarity simultaneously between model and ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> **Resistance Testing** is the empirical determination of a ship's total resistance ($R_T$) through model-scale experiments in a towing tank, followed by the application of the **ITTC 1978 Correlation Line** (or equivalent method) to predict the full-scale effective power ($P_E$) of the prototype vessel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a small toy boat (1 meter long) and you want to know how much "push" a real cargo ship (200 meters long) will need to sail at 20 knots. You obviously cannot test the real ship during the design stage — it hasn't been built yet! So naval architects create a **miniature replica** of the hull, place it in a long water channel (towing tank), and pull it through the water while measuring the drag force.

However, water behaves differently around a tiny model compared to a giant ship — the waves look smaller, and the flow is more "slippery" (laminar) on the model. To compensate, engineers use **physical scaling laws** to "translate" the small model's measurements back to the real ship. This is exactly like using a small-scale architectural model of a skyscraper to predict how the real building will behave in wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "scaling" problem:** You cannot make the model test in real ocean water at real ship speed — the forces would be astronomical and the tank would need to be 10 km long! So you shrink the ship AND slow it down, then mathematically "undo" the shrinking and slowing using Froude's Law.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are used universally in KTU-validated ship resistance testing:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of fresh water (tank water):** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of seawater (prototype):** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at 20°C:** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Model scale ratio:** $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ (typically $1/20$ to $1/40$)
- **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ (dimensionless speed parameter)
- **Reynolds Number:** $R_n = VL / \nu$ (dimensionless viscous flow parameter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Froude Number matters most:** In ship resistance, gravity (wave-making) and inertia dominate, so **Froude similarity** is preserved between model and ship. Viscous similarity (Reynolds) cannot be matched simultaneously — this is the famous **ship model testing dilemma** resolved by Froude in 1870.

### 1.4 Why Model Testing Cannot Be Replaced by CFD Alone

Even with modern Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), full-scale Reynolds number simulation of a complete ship hull is computationally prohibitive. Model testing remains the **gold standard** for:
- Validating theoretical resistance predictions (Holtrop, Series 60, etc.)
- Determining the **form factor ($k$)** of novel hull forms
- Capturing real-world phenomena: **propeller-hull interaction**, **bilge vortex shedding**, and **transom stern effects**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Dimensional Analysis of Resistance

The total resistance of a ship depends on the following physical variables:
- Ship length: $L$ [m]
- Speed: $V$ [m/s]
- Gravity: $g$ [m/s²]
- Density of fluid: $\rho$ [kg/m³]
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu$ [m²/s]
- Surface roughness: $\epsilon$ [m]
- Free surface characteristics (wave effects)

Applying the **Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem**, the total resistance coefficient $C_T$ can be expressed as a function of three dimensionless groups:

$$C_T = f(F_n, R_n, \epsilon/L)$$

Where:
- $C_T$ is the **Total Resistance Coefficient**
- $F_n$ is the **Froude Number** (wave-making)
- $R_n$ is the **Reynolds Number** (viscous)
- $\epsilon/L$ is the **relative roughness**

### 2.2 The Froude Decomposition (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Resistance Split:** Froude postulated (1870) that the total model resistance $R_{TM}$ can be split into two independent components:
- **Frictional resistance** $R_F$ (depends on $R_n$)
- **Residuary resistance** $R_R$ (depends on $F_n$)

$$R_{TM} = R_F + R_R$$

**Step 2 — Why this split works:** Frictional resistance acts on a flat plate equivalent to the wetted surface, while residuary resistance is the "everything else" — primarily wave-making, eddy-making, and appendage drag.

**Step 3 — Frictional Resistance via ITTC 1957 Formula:**

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_F$$

The ITTC 1957 frictional line is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Form Factor (1+k):** Due to the ship's 3D hull form, the actual viscous resistance is higher than a flat plate. The empirical correction is:

$$R_V = (1 + k) \cdot R_F$$

**Step 5 — Effective Power:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_S \text{ [Watts]}$$

### 2.3 The ITTC 1978 Correlation Method (The Industry Standard)

This is the method you **must** memorize for KTU exams. The full-scale resistance is obtained by:

**Step A — Convert model total resistance to total resistance coefficient:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{R_{TM}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m}$$

**Step B — Compute frictional coefficient (ITTC 1957) for the model:**

$$C_{Fm} = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_{nm} - 2)^2}$$

**Step C — Determine Form Factor from the form factor test** (subsidiary test at low $F_n$, where $R_R \approx 0$):

$$(1 + k) = \frac{C_{Tm}}{C_{Fm}} \text{ at low } F_n$$

**Step D — Compute the residuary resistance coefficient of the model:**

$$C_R = C_{TM} - (1+k) C_{Fm}$$

**Step E — Add correlation allowance** $C_A$ to account for hull roughness, appendages not on model, weather, etc.:

$$C_{TS} = (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A$$

**Step F — Compute full-scale total resistance:**

$$R_{TS} = C_{TS} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|----------|--------|---------|-------|
| Scale Ratio | $\lambda$ | $L_m / L_s$ | dimensionless |
| Froude Number | $F_n$ | $V / \sqrt{gL}$ | dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number | $R_n$ | $VL / \nu$ | dimensionless |
| Total Resistance Coefficient | $C_T$ | $R_T / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S)$ | dimensionless |
| ITTC 1957 Frictional Line | $C_F$ | $\dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| Frictional Resistance | $R_F$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2 C_F$ | Newtons (N) |
| Form Factor (1+k) | $1+k$ | $C_{Tm(low F_n)} / C_{Fm}$ | dimensionless |
| Effective Power | $P_E$ | $R_{TS} \cdot V_S$ | Watts (W) |
| EHP (Effective Horsepower) | $EHP$ | $P_E / 745.7$ | Horsepower |
| Speed-Length Ratio (knots/ft) | $V / \sqrt{L}$ | Practical KTU formula | knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed-Length Ratio Conversion:** KTU examiners often use the practical form $V/\sqrt{L}$ in **knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$** (where $L$ is in feet). This is related to the SI Froude number by: $F_n = 0.298 \cdot (V/\sqrt{L})$. A ship of $L = 300$ ft at 18 knots has $V/\sqrt{L} = 1.039$ knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$, which is the **hull speed** range.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Froude scaling method is used in:
- **Naval architecture firms** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas) for power prediction during ship design
- **Classification societies** to verify contractual speed-power guarantees
- **Yacht design** to estimate sailing performance
- **Submarine design** for submerged resistance estimation
- **Towing tank facilities** worldwide: MARIN (Netherlands), HSVA (Germany), SSPA (Sweden), KRISO (Korea)

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Froude's Scaling Law from Dimensional Analysis

We start with the assumption that the total resistance $R_T$ depends on the variables: $R_T = f(L, V, \rho, g, \nu)$.

**Step 1 — List dimensions of each variable using [M, L, T] system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[R_T] &= M L T^{-2} \\
[L] &= L \\
[V] &= L T^{-1} \\
[\rho] &= M L^{-3} \\
[g] &= L T^{-2} \\
[\nu] &= L^2 T^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Buckingham $\pi$-Theorem to get 3 dimensionless $\pi$ groups:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= \frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \text{(resistance coefficient)} \\
\pi_2 &= \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \quad \text{(Froude number)} \\
\pi_3 &= \frac{VL}{\nu} \quad \text{(Reynolds number)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — The functional relationship is therefore:**

$$\pi_1 = \phi(\pi_2, \pi_3) \implies C_T = \phi(F_n, R_n)$$

**Step 4 — Froude's hypothesis:** Since the model and ship are geometrically similar and tested in the same fluid, $R_n$ scaling is incomplete but Froude scaling is exact. We equate $F_n$:

$$F_{nm} = F_{ns} \implies \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the model speed:**

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$$

This is the **golden rule** of ship model testing: if the ship is 25 times larger than the model, the model must be towed 5 times slower (proportionally).

### 3.2 Full Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
A ship of length $L_s = 200$ m is to be tested at a scale of $\lambda = 1/25$. At a ship speed of $V_s = 15$ knots, the measured model total resistance is $R_{TM} = 18.5$ N. Given: wetted surface $S_m = 4.5$ m², $S_s = 2812$ m², form factor $(1+k) = 1.18$, correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0004$. Compute the full-scale effective power in kW. Use seawater density $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, tank fresh water $\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert units:** $V_s = 15 \text{ knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s. $g = 9.81$ m/s², $L_s = 200$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute model speed using Froude similarity:**

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 7.716 \times \sqrt{0.04} = 7.716 \times 0.2 = 1.5432 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute model Froude and Reynolds numbers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{nm} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{1.5432}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 8}} = \frac{1.5432}{8.858} = 0.1742 \\
L_m &= \lambda L_s = 0.04 \times 200 = 8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{TM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{R_{TM}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{18.5}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 1.5432^2 \times 4.5}$$

**Step 4a — Evaluate denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 2.3815 \times 4.5 = 5358.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4b — Compute $C_{TM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{18.5}{5358.4} = 0.003452$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{Fm}$ using ITTC 1957 line:**

First, $R_{nm} = V_m L_m / \nu = 1.5432 \times 8 / (1.004 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.2296 \times 10^7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{nm}) &= \log_{10}(1.2296 \times 10^7) = 7.0897 \\
C_{Fm} &= \frac{0.075}{(7.0897 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{25.91} = 0.002895
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute residuary resistance coefficient:**

$$C_R = C_{TM} - (1+k) C_{Fm} = 0.003452 - 1.18 \times 0.002895 = 0.003452 - 0.003416 = 0.000036$$

**Step 7 — Compute full-scale frictional coefficient $C_{Fs}$:**

$R_{ns} = V_s L_s / \nu_s$ (using $\nu_s \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ for seawater at 15°C, or by using the scale law)

Using scale law: $R_{ns} = R_{nm} \times (V_s/V_m) \times (L_s/L_m) / (\nu_s/\nu_m)$

Simplification: $R_{ns} = R_{nm} \times (L_s/L_m)^{3/2} = 1.2296 \times 10^7 \times 125 = 1.537 \times 10^9$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{ns}) &= 9.1867 \\
C_{Fs} &= \frac{0.075}{(9.1867 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.62} = 0.001453
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply ITTC 1978 correlation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TS} &= (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A \\
C_{TS} &= 1.18 \times 0.001453 + 0.000036 + 0.0004 \\
C_{TS} &= 0.001714 + 0.000036 + 0.0004 = 0.002150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute full-scale total resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TS} &= C_{TS} \times \frac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s \\
\frac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 7.716^2 \times 2812 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 2812 \\
&= 85,800,000 \text{ N} = 85,800 \text{ kN} \\
R_{TS} &= 0.002150 \times 85,800,000 = 184,470 \text{ N} \approx 184.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Compute Effective Power:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \times V_s = 184,470 \times 7.716 = 1,423,400 \text{ W} \approx 1423.4 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $P_E \approx 1423$ kW (or equivalently 1908 shaft horsepower equivalent before propeller losses).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Test Speeds & Speed Coefficients

| Test Speed Point | $F_n$ Range | Dominant Resistance | Notes |
|------------------|-------------|--------------------|-------|
| Low Speed | $< 0.15$ | Frictional only | Used for form factor test |
| Cruise | $0.18 - 0.25$ | Mixed | Service speed region |
| High Speed | $0.28 - 0.40$ | Wave-making dominant | Slender vessels |
| Very High | $> 0.45$ | Wave-breaking | Planing / semi-displacement |
| Critical | $\approx 0.40$ | **Hump** (maximum resistance) | Often the design bottleneck |
