### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Resistance Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Design Geometry] --> B[Scale Model Fabrication]
    B --> C[Towing Tank Preparation]
    C --> D[Instrument Calibration]
    D --> E[Model Test Execution]
    E --> F[Raw Resistance Data Collection]
    F --> G[Form Factor Test Low Fn]
    G --> H[Compute Form Factor 1 plus k]
    F --> I[Main Test Cruise Speeds]
    I --> J[Compute CTM and CFm]
    H --> K[Compute CR Residuary Coefficient]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute CFs Full Scale]
    L --> M[Apply ITTC 1978 Correlation]
    M --> N[Add CA Correlation Allowance]
    N --> O[Full Scale Total Resistance RTS]
    O --> P[Effective Power PE Calculation]
    P --> Q[Report to Designer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Resistance Decomposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Resistance RT] --> B[Frictional RF]
    A --> C[Residuary RR]
    C --> D[Wave Making RW]
    C --> E[Eddy Making RE]
    C --> F[Appendage Drag RA]
    B --> G[ITTC 1957 CF Line]
    G --> H[1 plus k Correction]
    H --> I[Full Scale Viscous RV]
    D --> J[Froude Scaling at same Fn]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[RTS Full Scale Total]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Typical Towing Tank Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T[Towing Tank Installation]
        T1[Towing Carriage with Dynamometer]
        T2[Rails along tank length 100 to 400 m]
        T3[Model attached to strut]
        T4[Wave probes and load cells]
        T5[Variable speed drive system]
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T3 --> T1
    T4 --> T3
    T5 --> T1
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Textual Fallback)

A **towing tank** is essentially a long, narrow, deep water channel (typically 100–400 m long, 3–15 m wide, 3–8 m deep) filled with fresh or salt water. A motorized **carriage** runs on rails along the top of the tank, holding the model ship via a streamlined **strut** (force dynamometer) at the front. As the carriage moves, the model is "towed" through stationary water. The strut contains **strain gauges** that measure the resistance force in three axes (surge, sway, heave). **Wave probes** (capacitance-type) measure the wave pattern generated by the model. The **carriage speed** is precisely controlled (typically 0.1 to 10 m/s) via a closed-loop servo system.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Froude Number and explain why it is the governing similarity parameter in ship model testing.**

**Model Answer:**
The Froude Number $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces in a fluid flow. In ship model testing, gravity governs wave generation (wave-making resistance) and inertia governs the bulk flow response, so Froude similarity between model and ship must be maintained. Since it is physically impossible to match both Froude and Reynolds similarity simultaneously (Reynolds requires much higher model speeds that are unsafe or impossible in a tank), Froude is chosen as the primary scaling parameter.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the ITTC 1957 frictional resistance line? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The ITTC 1957 frictional resistance line is an empirical correlation obtained from flat-plate turbulent boundary layer experiments, used to estimate the frictional resistance of ship hulls as if they were flat plates of equivalent wetted surface area. The formula is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

This is used in the ITTC 1978 prediction method to estimate both model and full-scale frictional resistance coefficients.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the ITTC 1978 correlation method for predicting full-scale ship resistance from model test data. Clearly state all assumptions and the role of the form factor.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A model of scale $1/30$ is tested in fresh water ($\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³) at a speed of $V_m = 2.0$ m/s. The measured total resistance is $R_{TM} = 25$ N and the wetted surface area is $S_m = 5.0$ m². The form factor is $(1+k) = 1.20$ and the ITTC 1957 line gives $C_{Fm} = 0.0029$. Calculate the residuary resistance coefficient $C_R$ and the effective horsepower (EHP) at the corresponding full-scale speed of $V_s = 15$ knots in seawater ($\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, $S_s = 4500$ m², $C_{Fs} = 0.00146$, $C_A = 0.0004$).

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The **ITTC 1978 correlation method** is the international standard for full-scale resistance prediction. The key steps are:

1. **Measure $R_{TM}$ at the desired Froude number** during the model test.
2. **Determine the form factor $(1+k)$** from a separate low-speed (low $F_n$) test, where residuary resistance is negligible:
   $(1+k) = C_{Tm(low F_n)} / C_{Fm(low F_n)}$
3. **Compute the model frictional coefficient** using the ITTC 1957 line.
4. **Extract the residuary coefficient** $C_R = C_{TM} - (1+k) C_{Fm}$ — assumed invariant with $R_n$ (Froude hypothesis).
5. **Compute full-scale frictional coefficient** $C_{Fs}$ at the corresponding Reynolds number using the ITTC 1957 line.
6. **Reconstruct full-scale total resistance coefficient** by adding the viscous + residuary + correlation allowance:
   $C_{TS} = (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A$
7. **Compute $R_{TS}$** by multiplying by $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s$.
8. **Compute $P_E = R_{TS} \times V_s$**.

**Key Assumptions:**
- Froude similarity is exact; $C_R$ is independent of $R_n$
- The form factor $(1+k)$ captures the 3D viscous augmentation
- The ITTC 1957 line accurately represents flat-plate friction
- $C_A$ accounts for surface roughness, appendages, weather effects

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{TM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{R_{TM}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{25}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 4.0 \times 5.0} = \frac{25}{10,000} = 0.00250$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_R$:**

$$C_R = C_{TM} - (1+k) C_{Fm} = 0.00250 - 1.20 \times 0.0029 = 0.00250 - 0.00348 = -0.00098$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A **negative** $C_R$ indicates either (i) a calculation error, (ii) the test was at a $F_n$ too low where $(1+k)$ was overestimated, or (iii) the ITTC line was misapplied. In a real test, this would warrant rechecking the form factor test. For exam purposes, proceed with the algebraic value if it arises.

**Step 3 — Full-scale speed conversion:** $V_s = 15$ knots $= 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s.

**Step 4 — Compute $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 7.716^2 \times 4500 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 4500 = 137,250,000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{TS}$:**

$$C_{TS} = (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A = 1.20 \times 0.00146 + (-0.00098) + 0.0004 = 0.001752 - 0.00098 + 0.0004 = 0.001172$$

**Step 6 — Compute $R_{TS}$:**

$$R_{TS} = 0.001172 \times 137,250,000 = 160,857 \text{ N} \approx 160.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Effective Power:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \times V_s = 160,857 \times 7.716 = 1,241,170 \text{ W} \approx 1241 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to EHP:**

$$\text{EHP} = \frac{P_E}{745.7} = \frac{1,241,170}{745.7} \approx 1664 \text{ HP}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the Froude method of comparison for ship model testing, including the assumptions made and the reasons why Reynolds similarity is not maintained.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A ship of length $L_s = 150$ m is to operate at $V_s = 12$ knots. The model scale is $1/25$, and the model is tested at $V_m$ corresponding to Froude similarity. Calculate (i) the model speed in m/s, (ii) the model Froude number, and (iii) the model Reynolds number given $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Comment on the difference in $R_n$ between model and ship.

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

The **Froude Method** (proposed by William Froude, 1870) is based on the hypothesis that ship resistance can be split into two parts:

$$R_T = R_F(\text{depends on } R_n) + R_R(\text{depends on } F_n)$$

The method equates the **Froude number** of model and ship, so that wave-making resistance scales correctly. The model speed is:

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{L_m / L_s} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Frictional and wave-making resistance are **independent** (an approximation, since in reality they interact at the boundary layer).
2. The ITTC 1957 line gives an accurate frictional coefficient for both model and ship.
3. The form factor $(1+k)$ is **scale-independent** (same for model and ship).
4. $C_R$ is a function of $F_n$ only, not $R_n$.

**Why Reynolds similarity is NOT maintained:**
- Reynolds number requires $V_m L_m = V_s L_s$, i.e., $V_m = V_s / \lambda$.
- For $\lambda = 1/25$, this would require the model to be towed at $V_m = 25 \times V_s$, which is physically impossible (a 12-knot ship would need a 300-knot model test — the model would break apart or leave the water).
- Maintaining $R_n$ equality would also require the model to operate in a fluid with much lower viscosity (impossible in a water tank).
- Practically, the model $R_n$ is $1/\lambda^{3/2} \approx 1/125$ of the ship's $R_n$. This is **accepted** and corrected by the ITTC 1978 procedure using form factor and correlation allowance.

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert ship speed:** $V_s = 12$ knots $= 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.1728$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute model length:** $L_m = L_s / 25 = 150 / 25 = 6$ m.

**(i) Model speed from Froude similarity:**

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 6.1728 \times \sqrt{0.04} = 6.1728 \times 0.2 = 1.2346 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Model Froude number:**

$$F_{nm} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{1.2346}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 6}} = \frac{1.2346}{\sqrt{58.86}} = \frac{1.2346}{7.673} = 0.1609$$

**Verification (should equal ship $F_n$):**

$$F_{ns} = \frac{6.1728}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 150}} = \frac{6.1728}{38.36} = 0.1609 \checkmark$$

**(iii) Model Reynolds number:**

$$R_{nm} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{1.2346 \times 6}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.408 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Compute ship Reynolds number** (using $\nu_s \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s for seawater):

$$R_{ns} = \frac{6.1728 \times 150}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.82 \times 10^8$$

**Step 4 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{R_{ns}}{R_{nm}} = \frac{8.82 \times 10^8}{7.408 \times 10^6} \approx 119 \approx \lambda^{-3/2} = 25^{1.5} = 125$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** The ship Reynolds number is approximately **125 times** higher than the model. This is a massive difference, but it is **unavoidable** due to the Froude similarity constraint. The ITTC 1978 method compensates for this difference using the form factor and correlation allowance procedures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - **Marks deducted** for using the wrong speed unit (knots vs. m/s). Always convert to SI before substituting.
> - **Common error:** Confusing the **ITTC 1957** frictional line with the older Froude friction line ($C_F = 0.0081/R_n^{1/7}$). The 1957 line is mandatory in modern KTU papers.
> **Mistakes that cost full marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert knots to m/s (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s).
> 2. Using $\rho_{sw}$ for the model (must be $\rho_{fw}$ — the model is in the towing tank, not at sea).
> 3. Treating $C_A$ as zero in exam problems (always include it when given).
> 4. Mixing up $C_{TM}$ and $C_{Tm(low F_n)}$ — the form factor uses the **low-speed** test value, not the cruise value.
> 5. Reporting $P_E$ in horsepower without dividing by 745.7 (the question explicitly asks for EHP).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **The Towing Tank Test** is the empirical backbone of ship resistance prediction. The model is towed in a long water channel; resistance is measured; full-scale values are predicted via Froude similarity.
- **Froude Number $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$** is the **PRIMARY** similarity parameter for ship model testing (not Reynolds number).
- **Froude Hypothesis (1870):** $R_T = R_F + R_R$, with $R_F$ depending on $R_n$ and $R_R$ depending on $F_n$.
- **ITTC 1957 Frictional Line:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ — memorize this formula verbatim.
- **ITTC 1978 Correlation Method:** $C_{TS} = (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A$ — the most tested formula in KTU exams.
- **Form Factor $(1+k)$** is found from a **low-speed (low $F_n$)** subsidiary test, where wave-making is negligible.
- **Correlation Allowance $C_A$** typically ranges from $0.0002$ to $0.0006$ and accounts for surface roughness, appendages, weather.
- **Model speed formula:** $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ (Froude scaling).
- **Reynolds number mismatch:** $R_{ns} / R_{nm} = \lambda^{-3/2}$ (always larger for the ship).
- **Unit conversions** are the #1 source of mistakes: 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 HP = 745.7 W; 1 ft = 0.3048 m.
- **Effective Power** $P_E = R_{TS} \times V_s$ is the power required to overcome resistance; **EHP** is $P_E / 745.7$.
- **The "hump"** in the resistance curve occurs near $F_n \approx 0.40$ (or $V/\sqrt{L} \approx 1.4$ knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$) for displacement hulls and is a critical design constraint.
- **Fresh water vs. seawater:** Use $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³ for the **model**, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ for the **ship**.

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model Testing for Ship Resistance** is the experimental procedure in which a geometrically similar scaled-down model of a ship is towed through calm water in a controlled towing tank at varying speeds, and the total resistance experienced by the model is measured using calibrated dynamometers. The measured model resistance is then extrapolated to the full-scale (prototype) ship using dimensional analysis and the **Froude Similarity Law**, since it is impossible to achieve both Froude and Reynolds similarity simultaneously between model and ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> **Resistance Testing** is the empirical determination of a ship's total resistance ($R_T$) through model-scale experiments in a towing tank, followed by the application of the **ITTC 1978 Correlation Line** (or equivalent method) to predict the full-scale effective power ($P_E$) of the prototype vessel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a small toy boat (1 meter long) and you want to know how much "push" a real cargo ship (200 meters long) will need to sail at 20 knots. You obviously cannot test the real ship during the design stage — it hasn't been built yet! So naval architects create a **miniature replica** of the hull, place it in a long water channel (towing tank), and pull it through the water while measuring the drag force.

However, water behaves differently around a tiny model compared to a giant ship — the waves look smaller, and the flow is more "slippery" (laminar) on the model. To compensate, engineers use **physical scaling laws** to "translate" the small model's measurements back to the real ship. This is exactly like using a small-scale architectural model of a skyscraper to predict how the real building will behave in wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "scaling" problem:** You cannot make the model test in real ocean water at real ship speed — the forces would be astronomical and the tank would need to be 10 km long! So you shrink the ship AND slow it down, then mathematically "undo" the shrinking and slowing using Froude's Law.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are used universally in KTU-validated ship resistance testing:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of fresh water (tank water):** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of seawater (prototype):** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at 20°C:** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Model scale ratio:** $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ (typically $1/20$ to $1/40$)
- **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ (dimensionless speed parameter)
- **Reynolds Number:** $R_n = VL / \nu$ (dimensionless viscous flow parameter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Froude Number matters most:** In ship resistance, gravity (wave-making) and inertia dominate, so **Froude similarity** is preserved between model and ship. Viscous similarity (Reynolds) cannot be matched simultaneously — this is the famous **ship model testing dilemma** resolved by Froude in 1870.

### 1.4 Why Model Testing Cannot Be Replaced by CFD Alone

Even with modern Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), full-scale Reynolds number simulation of a complete ship hull is computationally prohibitive. Model testing remains the **gold standard** for:
- Validating theoretical resistance predictions (Holtrop, Series 60, etc.)
- Determining the **form factor ($k$)** of novel hull forms
- Capturing real-world phenomena: **propeller-hull interaction**, **bilge vortex shedding**, and **transom stern effects**

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Dimensional Analysis of Resistance

The total resistance of a ship depends on the following physical variables:
- Ship length: $L$ [m]
- Speed: $V$ [m/s]
- Gravity: $g$ [m/s²]
- Density of fluid: $\rho$ [kg/m³]
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu$ [m²/s]
- Surface roughness: $\epsilon$ [m]
- Free surface characteristics (wave effects)

Applying the **Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem**, the total resistance coefficient $C_T$ can be expressed as a function of three dimensionless groups:

$$C_T = f(F_n, R_n, \epsilon/L)$$

Where:
- $C_T$ is the **Total Resistance Coefficient**
- $F_n$ is the **Froude Number** (wave-making)
- $R_n$ is the **Reynolds Number** (viscous)
- $\epsilon/L$ is the **relative roughness**

### 2.2 The Froude Decomposition (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Resistance Split:** Froude postulated (1870) that the total model resistance $R_{TM}$ can be split into two independent components:
- **Frictional resistance** $R_F$ (depends on $R_n$)
- **Residuary resistance** $R_R$ (depends on $F_n$)

$$R_{TM} = R_F + R_R$$

**Step 2 — Why this split works:** Frictional resistance acts on a flat plate equivalent to the wetted surface, while residuary resistance is the "everything else" — primarily wave-making, eddy-making, and appendage drag.

**Step 3 — Frictional Resistance via ITTC 1957 Formula:**

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_F$$

The ITTC 1957 frictional line is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Form Factor (1+k):** Due to the ship's 3D hull form, the actual viscous resistance is higher than a flat plate. The empirical correction is:

$$R_V = (1 + k) \cdot R_F$$

**Step 5 — Effective Power:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_S \text{ [Watts]}$$

### 2.3 The ITTC 1978 Correlation Method (The Industry Standard)

This is the method you **must** memorize for KTU exams. The full-scale resistance is obtained by:

**Step A — Convert model total resistance to total resistance coefficient:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{R_{TM}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m}$$

**Step B — Compute frictional coefficient (ITTC 1957) for the model:**

$$C_{Fm} = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_{nm} - 2)^2}$$

**Step C — Determine Form Factor from the form factor test** (subsidiary test at low $F_n$, where $R_R \approx 0$):

$$(1 + k) = \frac{C_{Tm}}{C_{Fm}} \text{ at low } F_n$$

**Step D — Compute the residuary resistance coefficient of the model:**

$$C_R = C_{TM} - (1+k) C_{Fm}$$

**Step E — Add correlation allowance** $C_A$ to account for hull roughness, appendages not on model, weather, etc.:

$$C_{TS} = (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A$$

**Step F — Compute full-scale total resistance:**

$$R_{TS} = C_{TS} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|----------|--------|---------|-------|
| Scale Ratio | $\lambda$ | $L_m / L_s$ | dimensionless |
| Froude Number | $F_n$ | $V / \sqrt{gL}$ | dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number | $R_n$ | $VL / \nu$ | dimensionless |
| Total Resistance Coefficient | $C_T$ | $R_T / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S)$ | dimensionless |
| ITTC 1957 Frictional Line | $C_F$ | $\dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| Frictional Resistance | $R_F$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2 C_F$ | Newtons (N) |
| Form Factor (1+k) | $1+k$ | $C_{Tm(low F_n)} / C_{Fm}$ | dimensionless |
| Effective Power | $P_E$ | $R_{TS} \cdot V_S$ | Watts (W) |
| EHP (Effective Horsepower) | $EHP$ | $P_E / 745.7$ | Horsepower |
| Speed-Length Ratio (knots/ft) | $V / \sqrt{L}$ | Practical KTU formula | knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed-Length Ratio Conversion:** KTU examiners often use the practical form $V/\sqrt{L}$ in **knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$** (where $L$ is in feet). This is related to the SI Froude number by: $F_n = 0.298 \cdot (V/\sqrt{L})$. A ship of $L = 300$ ft at 18 knots has $V/\sqrt{L} = 1.039$ knots/$\sqrt{\text{ft}}$, which is the **hull speed** range.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Froude scaling method is used in:
- **Naval architecture firms** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas) for power prediction during ship design
- **Classification societies** to verify contractual speed-power guarantees
- **Yacht design** to estimate sailing performance
- **Submarine design** for submerged resistance estimation
- **Towing tank facilities** worldwide: MARIN (Netherlands), HSVA (Germany), SSPA (Sweden), KRISO (Korea)

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Froude's Scaling Law from Dimensional Analysis

We start with the assumption that the total resistance $R_T$ depends on the variables: $R_T = f(L, V, \rho, g, \nu)$.

**Step 1 — List dimensions of each variable using [M, L, T] system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[R_T] &= M L T^{-2} \\
[L] &= L \\
[V] &= L T^{-1} \\
[\rho] &= M L^{-3} \\
[g] &= L T^{-2} \\
[\nu] &= L^2 T^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Buckingham $\pi$-Theorem to get 3 dimensionless $\pi$ groups:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= \frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \text{(resistance coefficient)} \\
\pi_2 &= \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \quad \text{(Froude number)} \\
\pi_3 &= \frac{VL}{\nu} \quad \text{(Reynolds number)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — The functional relationship is therefore:**

$$\pi_1 = \phi(\pi_2, \pi_3) \implies C_T = \phi(F_n, R_n)$$

**Step 4 — Froude's hypothesis:** Since the model and ship are geometrically similar and tested in the same fluid, $R_n$ scaling is incomplete but Froude scaling is exact. We equate $F_n$:

$$F_{nm} = F_{ns} \implies \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the model speed:**

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$$

This is the **golden rule** of ship model testing: if the ship is 25 times larger than the model, the model must be towed 5 times slower (proportionally).

### 3.2 Full Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
A ship of length $L_s = 200$ m is to be tested at a scale of $\lambda = 1/25$. At a ship speed of $V_s = 15$ knots, the measured model total resistance is $R_{TM} = 18.5$ N. Given: wetted surface $S_m = 4.5$ m², $S_s = 2812$ m², form factor $(1+k) = 1.18$, correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0004$. Compute the full-scale effective power in kW. Use seawater density $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, tank fresh water $\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert units:** $V_s = 15 \text{ knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s. $g = 9.81$ m/s², $L_s = 200$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute model speed using Froude similarity:**

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 7.716 \times \sqrt{0.04} = 7.716 \times 0.2 = 1.5432 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute model Froude and Reynolds numbers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{nm} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{1.5432}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 8}} = \frac{1.5432}{8.858} = 0.1742 \\
L_m &= \lambda L_s = 0.04 \times 200 = 8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{TM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{R_{TM}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{18.5}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 1.5432^2 \times 4.5}$$

**Step 4a — Evaluate denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho_m V_m^2 S_m = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 2.3815 \times 4.5 = 5358.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4b — Compute $C_{TM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{18.5}{5358.4} = 0.003452$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{Fm}$ using ITTC 1957 line:**

First, $R_{nm} = V_m L_m / \nu = 1.5432 \times 8 / (1.004 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.2296 \times 10^7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{nm}) &= \log_{10}(1.2296 \times 10^7) = 7.0897 \\
C_{Fm} &= \frac{0.075}{(7.0897 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{25.91} = 0.002895
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute residuary resistance coefficient:**

$$C_R = C_{TM} - (1+k) C_{Fm} = 0.003452 - 1.18 \times 0.002895 = 0.003452 - 0.003416 = 0.000036$$

**Step 7 — Compute full-scale frictional coefficient $C_{Fs}$:**

$R_{ns} = V_s L_s / \nu_s$ (using $\nu_s \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ for seawater at 15°C, or by using the scale law)

Using scale law: $R_{ns} = R_{nm} \times (V_s/V_m) \times (L_s/L_m) / (\nu_s/\nu_m)$

Simplification: $R_{ns} = R_{nm} \times (L_s/L_m)^{3/2} = 1.2296 \times 10^7 \times 125 = 1.537 \times 10^9$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{ns}) &= 9.1867 \\
C_{Fs} &= \frac{0.075}{(9.1867 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.62} = 0.001453
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply ITTC 1978 correlation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TS} &= (1+k) C_{Fs} + C_R + C_A \\
C_{TS} &= 1.18 \times 0.001453 + 0.000036 + 0.0004 \\
C_{TS} &= 0.001714 + 0.000036 + 0.0004 = 0.002150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute full-scale total resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TS} &= C_{TS} \times \frac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s \\
\frac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 7.716^2 \times 2812 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 2812 \\
&= 85,800,000 \text{ N} = 85,800 \text{ kN} \\
R_{TS} &= 0.002150 \times 85,800,000 = 184,470 \text{ N} \approx 184.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Compute Effective Power:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \times V_s = 184,470 \times 7.716 = 1,423,400 \text{ W} \approx 1423.4 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $P_E \approx 1423$ kW (or equivalently 1908 shaft horsepower equivalent before propeller losses).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Test Speeds & Speed Coefficients

| Test Speed Point | $F_n$ Range | Dominant Resistance | Notes |
|------------------|-------------|--------------------|-------|
| Low Speed | $< 0.15$ | Frictional only | Used for form factor test |
| Cruise | $0.18 - 0.25$ | Mixed | Service speed region |
| High Speed | $0.28 - 0.40$ | Wave-making dominant | Slender vessels |
| Very High | $> 0.45$ | Wave-breaking | Planing / semi-displacement |
| Critical | $\approx 0.40$ | **Hump** (maximum resistance) | Often the design bottleneck |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Design Geometry] --> B[Scale Model Fabrication]
    B --> C[Towing Tank Preparation]
    C --> D[Instrument Calibration]

    F --> G[Form Factor Test Low Fn]

    F --> I[Main Test Cruise Speeds]
    I --> J[Compute CTM and CFm]
    H --> K[Compute CR Residuary Coefficient]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute CFs Full Scale]

    M --> N[Add CA Correlation Allowance]
    N --> O[Full Scale Total Resistance RTS]
    O --> P[Effective Power PE Calculation]
    P --> Q[Report to Designer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Resistance Decomposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Resistance RT] --> B[Frictional RF]
    A --> C[Residuary RR]
    C --> D[Wave Making RW]
    C --> E[Eddy Making RE]
    C --> F[Appendage Drag RA]
    B --> G[ITTC 1957 CF Line]
    G --> H[1 plus k Correction]
    H --> I[Full Scale Viscous RV]
    D --> J[Froude Scaling at same Fn]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[RTS Full Scale Total]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Typical Towing Tank Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T[Towing Tank Installation]
        T1[Towing Carriage with Dynamometer]
        T2[Rails along tank length 100 to 400 m]
        T3[Model attached to strut]
        T4[Wave probes and load cells]
        T5[Variable speed drive system]
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T3 --> T1
    T4 --> T3
    T5 --> T1
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Textual Fallback)

A **towing tank** is essentially a long, narrow, deep water channel (typically 100–400 m long, 3–15 m wide, 3–8 m deep) filled with fresh or salt water. A motorized **carriage** runs on rails along the top of the tank, holding the model ship via a streamlined **strut** (force dynamometer) at the front. As the carriage moves, the model is "towed" through stationary water. The strut contains **strain gauges** that measure the resistance force in three axes (surge, sway, heave). **Wave probes** (capacitance-type) measure the wave pattern generated by the model. The **carriage speed** is precisely controlled (typically 0.1 to 10 m/s) via a closed-loop servo system.
